For Iceberg and Delta Lake, table statistics are maintained automatically by the table format as data changes.

Race condition in pipeline initialization causes duplicate destination table creation in parallel environments. When multiple pipeline workers start simultaneously and check-then-act on shared resources: worker A checks if table exists (returns false); worker B checks if table exists (returns false before A creates it); worker A creates the table; worker B tries to create the table (already exists, error); the table creation race causes one worker to fail. Table initialization must be atomic: use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS which is idempotent (no error if already exists); use database-level locks or optimistic concurrency for schema migration operations; implement a leader election pattern where only the elected leader performs initialization and other workers wait; in cloud environments, use provider-native mechanisms (DynamoDB conditional writes, S3 conditional puts) for atomic initialization.

Wrong grain aggregation in fact tables mixes measures at different granularity levels, producing incorrect analytical results. A fact table that mixes order-level metrics (order total, order count) with product-level metrics (units sold per product) in the same row creates ambiguity: if an order has 3 products, does the order total appear once or three times? If three times, revenue is triple-counted. Kimball dimensional modeling principle: each fact table should contain measures at exactly one grain (one row per order, or one row per order line item, not both). Mixed-grain symptoms: total revenue appears incorrect when filtered by product (wrong because revenue is at order grain, product filter causes rows to not add up correctly); count metrics and sum metrics appear inconsistent with each other. Fix: separate order-level measures into an order fact table; product-level measures into an order line item fact table; join them in analysis queries when needed.

Data pipeline failure concealed by empty success causes downstream systems to silently serve stale data. A pipeline that reads from an empty source partition (because upstream failed to deliver data) runs successfully, writes an empty output partition, and marks itself as succeeded. Downstream systems that read from the empty partition serve zeroed metrics or show no data for the time period. No alert fires because the pipeline technically succeeded. Prevention: implement minimum record count checks as part of pipeline success criteria; a pipeline run that produces zero records should either fail explicitly or require manual approval to be marked as acceptable; use data arrival sensors that verify expected data exists before starting processing; implement output quality gate that rejects suspiciously small outputs (fewer than N% of historical average for the same period).

Accidental test data in production datasets corrupts analytics when development and testing data shares production infrastructure. Developers testing with production Kafka topics or production S3 buckets inject synthetic test events that appear as real user activity in analytics. Dashboards show artificial spikes from load tests; user cohort analyses are contaminated with test users. Prevention: strict environment separation with no cross-environment access; test-specific Kafka topics and S3 prefixes that are excluded from analytics ingestion; synthetic data markers (is_test_record: true flag) that analytics pipelines filter out; network-level isolation that prevents development environment from accessing production storage; regular audits of production data for synthetic data patterns; automated detection of test data characteristics (test email patterns like @test.com, impossible user IDs like 0 or -1).

Pipeline outputs lacking metadata headers cause downstream systems to misinterpret data. CSV files written without header rows require consumer code to hard-code column positions; if the pipeline ever adds, removes, or reorders columns, all consumers silently misread data. JSON files without schema information require consumers to infer types, leading to inconsistent type handling. Parquet files written without statistics metadata cannot use predicate pushdown optimization. Metadata requirements: CSV outputs must always include header rows; JSON outputs must include explicit type declarations or accompanying schema; Parquet outputs should include row group statistics (enabled by default but can be disabled); Avro outputs must reference schema by registry ID; all outputs should include pipeline metadata (pipeline version, run timestamp, source partition) for traceability.

Loading a full SDXL pipeline naively allocates ~6.5 GB VRAM for FP16 weights (UNet ~2.6 GB, VAE ~160 MB, two CLIP text encoders ~1.4 GB each). FLUX.1 dev requires ~24 GB in FP16. The failure mode is calling from_pretrained() without memory optimization flags on a 8/12 GB GPU, getting CUDA OOM, and concluding the model is incompatible with the hardware. The correct mitigation stack (applied in order of aggressiveness): (1) enable_model_cpu_offload() moves components to CPU between pipeline stages; (2) enable_sequential_cpu_offload() offloads at the layer level at the cost of ~10x slower inference; (3) use torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16 or torch.float8_e4m3fn where supported; (4) use a quantized variant (GGUF or bitsandbytes 4-bit) via the quantization_config parameter introduced in Diffusers 0.30.

Not all schedulers are compatible with all models, and Diffusers does not always raise an informative error when an incompatible scheduler is used. Specific failure patterns: (1) Flow-matching models (FLUX, SD3) require schedulers that implement the flow ODE (FlowMatchEulerDiscreteScheduler) — using DDIM or DPM-Solver produces garbled output, not an exception; (2) SDXL with LCM LoRA requires LCMScheduler at 4-8 steps — using any other scheduler with LCM weights produces low-quality output because the LoRA was trained specifically against LCM's distillation trajectory; (3) Some community schedulers lack the step() return values expected by pipeline internals and fail with AttributeError only mid-generation. Always verify scheduler compatibility in the model card before swapping.

Diffusers' VAE decode step is a significant source of memory and time overhead that many tutorials underemphasize. For SDXL, decoding a single 1024x1024 image requires the full VAE decoder with ~160MB of parameters — but the intermediate feature maps during decode consume up to 3GB of additional VRAM. Calling pipeline(...) with large batches (batch_size >= 4) compounds this: VAE decode is not batched-friendly and can OOM even when the UNet forward passes completed successfully. The solution is tiled VAE decoding (pipeline.vae.enable_tiling()) which decodes spatial tiles sequentially, reducing peak VRAM by 3-4x at the cost of minor seam artifacts on high-frequency texture regions. For video generation pipelines (AnimateDiff, SVD), tiled VAE decode is almost always required because the temporal batch of frames makes the decode step consume more VRAM than the entire diffusion process.

Diffusers' inpainting pipelines (StableDiffusionInpaintPipeline, StableDiffusionXLInpaintPipeline) require the mask to be a PIL Image or tensor with values strictly in {0, 1} — 0 meaning 'preserve this region', 1 meaning 'inpaint this region'. A common failure is passing a soft or anti-aliased mask with float values between 0 and 1, which causes the pipeline to inpaint all pixels to varying degrees, producing blurred transitions rather than sharp inpaint boundaries. Another failure: the mask must be the same spatial resolution as the image before passing to the pipeline — the pipeline does NOT resize the mask to match the image internally, and a mismatched mask produces silent mis-alignment. For production inpainting, always resize and binarize the mask explicitly before calling the pipeline, and validate that mask.size == image.size before each call.

Diffusers' pipeline.to(dtype=torch.float16) applies FP16 to all pipeline components uniformly, but the VAE encoder and decoder are numerically unstable in FP16 for certain image distributions — producing black images, NaN artifacts, or extreme saturation on images with unusual color distributions (e.g., near-pure black or white images, certain medical images). The SDXL VAE is particularly susceptible. The established fix is to keep the VAE in FP32 while running the UNet in FP16: load the VAE separately with `AutoencoderKL.from_pretrained(..., torch_dtype=torch.float32)` and assign it to `pipeline.vae`. Alternatively, use the SDXL VAE FP16 fix variant (madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix on HuggingFace) which was fine-tuned specifically to be stable in FP16. The black image failure mode is one of the most frequently reported issues in Diffusers GitHub and is almost always resolved by this VAE dtype fix.

The N+1 query problem: fetching 100 blog posts and then accessing post.author in a template fires 101 queries (1 for posts + 1 per post for author). Fix: Post.objects.select_related('author') for ForeignKey/OneToOne (SQL JOIN). Post.objects.prefetch_related('tags') for ManyToMany/reverse FK (separate query + Python join). Detection: django-debug-toolbar shows duplicate queries. django-silk profiles them. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a custom queryset uses Prefetch() object. Extraction signal: any view doing DB access inside a template loop is likely N+1.

Django signals (pre_save, post_save, post_delete) are synchronous and fire in the same transaction. They're used for side effects: audit logging, cache invalidation, sending emails. UNSAID: signals are invisible coupling — no type checker can trace them. post_save signal calling external APIs will delay saves and fail if the API is down. post_save fires even within transactions that will be rolled back — the signal handler may see data that never ends up in the DB. Best practice: use on_commit() for side effects that must only happen if the transaction commits.

Django wraps each request in an atomic transaction by default (ATOMIC_REQUESTS=True). UNSAID: this means a slow request holds a DB transaction open for its entire duration, blocking other queries that need row locks. For requests that do heavy computation and then a final write, use transaction.atomic() only around the write block. Also: exceptions inside @transaction.atomic rollback the transaction — raising an exception in a signal handler rolls back the entire parent transaction. Side effects (emails, external API calls) inside transactions are dangerous because they execute even if the transaction is later rolled back. on_commit() defers code until after successful commit.

Django ORM N+1 deep patterns beyond basic select_related: (1) Reverse FK relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related('comments') prefetches all comments in one query; (2) Nested relations — prefetch_related('comments__author') chains prefetches; (3) Annotations instead of properties — annotate(comment_count=Count('comments')) adds a computed field in SQL, avoiding Python-side aggregation loops; (4) Only/defer — .only('id', 'title') fetches fewer columns, avoiding expensive text fields; (5) values_list() for bulk ID extraction without model instantiation overhead. UNSAID: prefetch_related with a filtered Prefetch() object is the only way to apply WHERE conditions to prefetched relations — Post.objects.prefetch_related(Prefetch('comments', queryset=Comment.objects.filter(approved=True))). Using Python filtering after prefetch defeats the purpose (all data is fetched then filtered in Python).

Django migration strategy for zero-downtime deployments: the naive approach (add non-nullable column → deploy) causes downtime because the migration adds the column with NOT NULL before the new code is deployed. Safe three-phase pattern: Phase 1 — add nullable column (no default required, existing rows get NULL), deploy; Phase 2 — backfill with a data migration or management command, add application-level default in code; Phase 3 — add DB-level NOT NULL constraint (fast if all rows filled) and remove nullable. UNSAID: Django's migration framework does NOT automatically detect that removing a field from the model is a breaking change for running instances — the old code reading the dropped column will get AttributeError until fully redeployed. Extraction signal: projects that run migrate before deploying new code can safely add nullable columns; projects that deploy code before migrate can safely read new nullable columns as None.

Django signals anti-patterns: (1) Connecting signals in models.py at module level — signals are connected every time the module is imported, leading to duplicate handler calls if the module is imported multiple times (rare but possible in test setups); correct location is AppConfig.ready(); (2) Signals for business logic — signals create invisible coupling that breaks the 'explicit is better than implicit' principle; business logic in signal handlers is untestable in isolation; (3) post_save for external side effects — the signal fires inside the transaction; if the external call fails, the transaction is already committed; use on_commit() callback to defer external calls until after commit; (4) Circular signal chains — A.post_save triggers B.save() which triggers B.post_save which triggers A.save(). UNSAID: Django doesn't protect against signal recursion — add a 'dispatch_uid' parameter to prevent duplicate registration and guard against circular chains with a flag.

Django's ORM 'select_related()' uses SQL JOIN and is appropriate for ForeignKey and OneToOne relationships. 'prefetch_related()' uses separate queries and is appropriate for ManyToMany and reverse ForeignKey relationships. Using 'select_related()' on a ManyToMany relationship raises a ValueError or silently falls back to prefetch behavior. Using 'prefetch_related()' on a ForeignKey works but is less efficient than 'select_related()' (two queries vs one JOIN). The classic N+1 problem: 'for book in Book.objects.all(): print(book.author.name)' executes N+1 queries. Fix: 'Book.objects.select_related("author").all()'. Profile with Django Debug Toolbar before assuming you know which queries are slow.

Django migrations are generated from model state tracked in migration files, not from the current database schema. Running 'python manage.py makemigrations' when there are unapplied migrations generates a new migration based on the last migration's state, not the current database. If you manually modify the database (add a column, change a type) without creating a corresponding migration, Django won't detect the discrepancy — 'makemigrations --check' won't catch manual DB changes. This causes 'django.db.utils.ProgrammingError' at runtime when the ORM attempts an operation on a column/table that exists in Django's state but not in the actual database or vice versa.

Django's 'bulk_create()' does NOT call 'save()' on individual model instances, which means: signals ('post_save', 'pre_save') are NOT fired, custom 'save()' method logic is NOT executed, and 'auto_now_add' fields ARE populated (as a special case). This breaks any business logic relying on signals for side effects (sending emails, updating caches, creating related records). Always audit what 'save()' and 'post_save' signals do before replacing 'save()' calls with 'bulk_create()'. Similarly, 'bulk_update()' does not fire signals. For bulk operations requiring signals, process in batches using 'save()' or fire signals manually after bulk operations.

Django signals create hidden coupling that is difficult to debug and test. A 'post_save' signal on User model might update a UserProfile in one module, send a welcome email in another, and invalidate caches in a third — none of these connections are visible from the User model or view code. Failures in signal receivers can cause transaction rollbacks affecting the originating operation. Signal debugging is difficult: set 'DEBUG=True' and use 'Signal.disconnect()' in tests to isolate issues. Prefer explicit service functions over signals for business logic. Use signals only for cross-cutting concerns (audit logging, metrics) where the relationship is truly unidirectional.

Django's 'get_or_create()' is NOT atomic in all database backends. Under concurrent requests, two processes can both find that the object doesn't exist and both attempt to create it, causing an IntegrityError on the second create. The correct pattern is: 'try: obj, created = MyModel.objects.get_or_create(field=value); except IntegrityError: obj = MyModel.objects.get(field=value); created = False'. Alternatively, define a unique constraint on the fields used in 'get_or_create' and handle the IntegrityError. This race condition is rare in low-traffic apps but common in high-concurrency systems. 'update_or_create()' has the same issue.

Django's migration squashing combines multiple migrations into one for performance, but squashed migrations require careful management during deployment. A squashed migration depends on ALL migrations it replaces having been applied. If you deploy the squashed migration to a database that hasn't run the original migrations, Django will apply the squashed migration instead. If some but not all original migrations were applied, Django is confused. The safe migration strategy: 1) squash locally, 2) deploy with both original and squashed migrations present, 3) after all servers have applied the squash, remove the original migrations in a subsequent deployment.

Django's 'only()' and 'defer()' queryset methods return model instances with some fields not loaded, but accessing a deferred field triggers an additional database query per instance. When iterating over a list and accessing deferred fields, this recreates the N+1 problem you were trying to avoid. Use 'only()' only when you're certain you won't access the excluded fields, or use 'values()' or 'values_list()' for truly flat data access. A subtle failure: serializers accessing model fields that were deferred in the queryset, triggering per-object queries during serialization. Always profile with Django Debug Toolbar after adding 'only()' or 'defer()'.

Django REST Framework's 'perform_create()' and 'perform_update()' are the correct hooks for injecting request-context data (like the authenticated user) into model creation. The common mistake: overriding 'create()' in the view and manually calling 'serializer.save()'. DRF's 'create()' method calls 'perform_create(serializer)' which calls 'serializer.save()'. If you override 'create()' and forget to call 'perform_create()', your overridden 'perform_create()' never runs. Best practice: always override 'perform_create()' to add extra fields: 'def perform_create(self, serializer): serializer.save(author=self.request.user)'. This preserves all of DRF's built-in create logic.

Django's migration dependency system means migration files should never be deleted or modified after being applied in any environment. Deleting an applied migration file causes 'django.db.migrations.exceptions.NodeNotFoundInGraph' errors and prevents running 'migrate'. If you need to clean up many small migrations, use squashmigrations — never delete migration files directly. A common failure during development: deleting all migrations and regenerating them ('python manage.py makemigrations') which breaks any deployed environments that have the original migrations in their 'django_migrations' table. Use 'migrate --fake' to repair state when absolutely necessary.

Django's 'settings.DEBUG = True' in production causes several security issues: detailed error pages with code excerpts and local variables (potentially exposing secrets), 'django.test.utils.override_settings' affecting security behavior, and performance degradation from keeping all SQL queries in memory ('connection.queries'). More subtly, 'DEBUG=True' makes Django serve static files directly (bypassing whitenoise or nginx) and disables security middleware checks. Always verify 'DEBUG=False' in production. Use environment variable: 'DEBUG = os.environ.get("DEBUG", "False") == "True"' — note the string comparison since env vars are always strings.

Django's 'save()' method, when called without 'update_fields', performs a full UPDATE of all model fields. In a concurrent environment, two processes reading and saving the same model will cause one to overwrite the other's changes (last-write-wins). Solution for partial updates: use 'instance.save(update_fields=["status", "updated_at"])' to update only the modified fields. Solution for concurrent modifications: use 'select_for_update()' to lock the row, or use 'F()' expressions for atomic increments. Never use 'obj.field += 1; obj.save()' for counters — always use 'MyModel.objects.filter(pk=obj.pk).update(field=F("field") + 1)'.

Django's 'ALLOWED_HOSTS' setting is enforced only when 'DEBUG=False'. In development with DEBUG=True, any host is allowed. This means developers never see host-validation failures locally, then are surprised when they deploy. ALLOWED_HOSTS must include the production domain, load balancer hostname, and internal service hostnames (for health checks). Forgetting to add a subdomain causes 'DisallowedHost: Invalid HTTP_HOST header' in production. Use 'ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get("ALLOWED_HOSTS", "").split(",")' to configure from environment variables. Add '*' only for trusted internal services that don't face the internet.

Django's 'ManyToManyField' with a 'through' model enables extra data on the relationship. Without 'through', Django auto-creates a join table and manages it. When you later add a 'through' model, you must manually migrate from the auto-created table — there's no automated migration path. Plan your data model upfront: if you might need relationship metadata (join date, role, status), add the 'through' model from the start even if it's currently empty. Adding 'through' retroactively requires a complex migration: create new through table, copy data from old join table, update the ManyToManyField to use 'through'.

Django's 'ContentType' framework and 'GenericForeignKey' enable polymorphic relationships but have severe performance implications. Every access to a generic foreign key requires: 1) a query to the 'django_content_types' table, 2) a query to the target model's table. For lists of objects with generic foreign keys, use 'prefetch_related' with 'GenericRelatedObjectManager.prefetch_related_objects()'. A common anti-pattern: using GenericForeignKey for simple use cases where a regular ForeignKey or Union type would work better. GenericForeignKey also prevents database-level foreign key constraints and cascades, making referential integrity the application's responsibility.

Django's 'on_commit' callbacks are tied to the outermost atomic transaction. When called inside a nested 'transaction.atomic()' (savepoint), the callback doesn't fire until the outermost transaction commits. If the outermost transaction rolls back (even though the inner savepoint committed successfully from the inner block's perspective), the on_commit callback never fires. This means if you rely on 'on_commit' to dispatch Celery tasks and the outer transaction rolls back, tasks are never sent — which is the correct behavior. Developers sometimes expect callbacks from savepoints to fire when the savepoint commits; they don't.

Django REST Framework's 'ModelSerializer' 'depth' option enables nested serialization of related objects, but it's dangerous in production APIs. 'depth = 2' serializes all fields of all related models up to 2 levels deep, including sensitive fields you might not want exposed. Users of a serializer with depth don't control what gets serialized — adding a field to a related model automatically exposes it through all serializers using depth. Always use explicit nested serializers: 'author = AuthorSerializer(read_only=True)' where 'AuthorSerializer' explicitly declares only the fields you want exposed. Never use 'depth' in production serializers.

Django's 'ImageField' and 'FileField' store only the file path in the database, not the actual file. Deleting a model instance with these fields does NOT delete the associated files from storage — orphaned files accumulate indefinitely. Implement cleanup via 'post_delete' signal: '@receiver(post_delete, sender=MyModel) def delete_file(sender, instance, **kwargs): instance.image.delete(save=False)'. Similarly, replacing an image (saving with a new file) leaves the old file on disk unless you implement cleanup in 'pre_save' or 'save()'. Use 'django-cleanup' library for automatic file deletion on model delete/replace.

Django's 'auto_now' and 'auto_now_add' on DateTimeField/DateField cannot be overridden by passing the value in model creation or save. 'auto_now_add=True' always uses the current time at creation; 'auto_now=True' always uses current time on every save. This makes it impossible to set these fields to custom values, which is a problem for data imports, fixtures, and testing. Use 'default=timezone.now' instead of 'auto_now_add=True' for fields you might need to control, with 'editable=False, blank=True' to prevent form/serializer exposure. 'auto_now' fields are even more problematic — use 'F("updated_at")' expressions or custom save logic for explicit control.

Django's admin site automatically discovers and registers models from 'admin.py' files in each app, but only when the app is in 'INSTALLED_APPS'. A common deployment failure: an app that works locally fails in production because its admin.py imports a model from another app that hasn't been loaded yet, causing 'AppRegistryNotReady' exception. Fix: use lazy model imports inside functions rather than at module level, or ensure proper INSTALLED_APPS ordering. Another failure: registering the same model twice with different admin classes causes 'AlreadyRegistered' error — use 'admin.site.unregister(Model)' before re-registering.

Django's 'middleware' order in MIDDLEWARE setting is critical and errors are silent. Request processing goes top-to-bottom through middleware; response processing goes bottom-to-top. 'SecurityMiddleware' must be first to enforce HTTPS redirects before other middleware processes potentially insecure requests. 'SessionMiddleware' must come before 'AuthenticationMiddleware' since auth depends on session. Placing 'CommonMiddleware' before 'SessionMiddleware' causes session data loss on trailing-slash redirects. Django's middleware order in the default project template is carefully tuned — always add custom middleware after researching the correct position, not at the end by default.

Django's CSRF protection is bypassed by incorrectly excluding views with '@csrf_exempt'. This decorator disables ALL CSRF protection for that view. A common failure: applying '@csrf_exempt' to fix webhook endpoints and accidentally excluding API endpoints that should be protected. For webhooks with signature-based authentication, '@csrf_exempt' is correct — but restrict to specific paths. For DRF views, CSRF is handled by the authentication classes (session auth requires CSRF, token auth doesn't) — adding '@csrf_exempt' to DRF views is usually unnecessary and potentially dangerous. Use 'CsrfExemptSessionAuthentication' in DRF for session-based auth without CSRF (non-standard; document why).

Django's 'QuerySet.iterator()' bypasses the in-memory cache and streams results directly, ideal for processing large datasets without memory exhaustion. However, 'iterator()' is incompatible with 'prefetch_related()' — prefetching requires loading all objects into memory to build the prefetch cache. Using 'iterator()' with 'prefetch_related()' silently disables prefetching, potentially causing N+1 queries. Also, 'iterator()' doesn't work with sliced querysets. For large datasets with related objects, process in batches: 'qs.order_by("pk")' then use keyset pagination (WHERE pk > last_pk LIMIT 1000) rather than 'iterator()' with 'prefetch_related()'.

Django's 'DateTimeField(default=datetime.now)' vs 'default=datetime.now()' is a critical distinction. Using 'default=datetime.now()' (with parentheses) evaluates the datetime once at class definition time — all records will have the same creation timestamp (the time the Python module was imported). Using 'default=datetime.now' (without parentheses) evaluates at instance creation time. This is a common Python gotcha that affects any mutable default: lists, dicts, dates. The correct pattern is always 'default=timezone.now' (without parens) or 'auto_now_add=True'. Django's system checks may not catch this bug; it manifests as suspicious data with identical timestamps.

Django's test runner creates a test database by default and runs each test in a transaction that's rolled back after the test. 'TestCase' (from 'django.test') wraps each test in a transaction. 'TransactionTestCase' (base class) doesn't — it resets the database between tests by truncating tables, which is slower. Tests that test signals, 'on_commit' callbacks, or database-level constraints must use 'TransactionTestCase' because these behaviors require transaction commit. A subtle failure: 'on_commit' callbacks tested with regular 'TestCase' never fire because the transaction never commits. Use 'TestCase.captureOnCommitCallbacks()' context manager (Django 4.1+) to test on_commit callbacks with TestCase.

Django REST Framework's 'write_only=True' on a serializer field prevents the field from being included in serialized output (responses) while allowing it in input (requests). A common failure: defining 'password = serializers.CharField(write_only=True)' but forgetting that 'ModelSerializer' might add the field with 'write_only=False' if 'password' is in the model's fields list. Always verify write_only behavior by testing the actual API response. DRF's extra_kwargs provides centralized write_only declaration: 'class Meta: extra_kwargs = {"password": {"write_only": True}}'. Use this consistently rather than scattered field declarations.

Django's admin 'list_display' cannot include ManyToMany fields directly — it raises an 'ImproperlyConfigured' error. Use a custom method on the ModelAdmin class: 'def get_categories(self, obj): return ", ".join(c.name for c in obj.categories.all())'. This triggers N+1 queries in the admin list view. Fix with 'prefetch_related': override 'get_queryset()' on the ModelAdmin to prefetch: 'def get_queryset(self, request): return super().get_queryset(request).prefetch_related("categories")'. The admin panel is a common source of unnoticed N+1 queries — monitor it with django-debug-toolbar even if the public API is optimized.

Django's management commands run in the context of the Django application with DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE set. A common failure: using relative imports or assuming a working directory in management commands, which breaks when run via cron or supervisord from different directories. Always use absolute paths: 'BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent.parent'. Another failure: management commands that call 'sys.exit()' directly — management commands should raise 'CommandError' for errors instead. Calling 'sys.exit()' inside a management command called from another context (tests, other commands) terminates the entire process unexpectedly.

Django REST Framework's 'HyperlinkedModelSerializer' and 'HyperlinkedRelatedField' generate URLs as relationship identifiers instead of PKs. They require the view to be named consistently: a 'User' model needs a 'user-detail' view name by default. Renaming views without updating serializers breaks URL generation with 'NoReverseMatch' errors. DRF's URL-based serializers also require the request context to generate absolute URLs: serializers used outside views (in Celery tasks, management commands) need explicit request injection. Custom 'view_name' parameter overrides the default naming: 'HyperlinkedRelatedField(view_name="api:user-detail", read_only=True)'.

Django's 'unique_together' Meta option is deprecated in favor of 'UniqueConstraint' in Meta.constraints. Using both on the same fields creates duplicate constraints in the database. The migration system treats them separately, so you end up with two constraints where one is sufficient. Worse, removing 'unique_together' generates a migration that drops the constraint, and removing 'UniqueConstraint' generates another — if you do both simultaneously in one migration, the final state may have no constraint. Always migrate incrementally: add UniqueConstraint first, deploy, then remove unique_together in a separate migration, verify, then deploy again.

Django REST Framework's 'to_representation()' override runs on serialization (output) while 'to_internal_value()' runs on deserialization (input). A common mistake: overriding 'to_representation()' to transform output data while inadvertently breaking input handling, or vice versa. Each method has different responsibilities: 'to_internal_value()' validates and converts input data to Python objects; 'to_representation()' converts model instances to output dicts. When overriding both, ensure field name changes are consistent. The 'validate()' method (cross-field validation) runs after 'to_internal_value()' — use for business rule validation that requires multiple fields.

Django's 'ATOMIC_REQUESTS = True' database setting wraps every HTTP request in a transaction, ensuring atomicity but creating long-lived transactions that hold database locks. Long requests (file uploads, calls to slow external APIs) keep transactions open, potentially blocking other requests waiting for locks on the same rows. Solutions: use 'transaction.non_atomic_requests()' decorator on views that shouldn't be wrapped, or process the slow parts outside the request transaction (Celery tasks). Also, never send emails, call external APIs, or publish to message queues inside request-level transactions — if the email succeeds but the DB transaction rolls back, you have inconsistent state.

Django's 'ForeignKey' with 'on_delete=models.CASCADE' deletes child objects when the parent is deleted. With 'on_delete=models.PROTECT', deletion is blocked if children exist. Choosing the wrong option has severe consequences: CASCADE can delete thousands of records from a single delete operation; PROTECT surfaces as an IntegrityError that developers must handle explicitly. 'SET_NULL' and 'SET_DEFAULT' are safer for optional relationships. A common production failure: deleting a User with CASCADE set on profile, orders, reviews, and everything else — a single admin action cascades to delete gigabytes of data. Audit every CASCADE on models that represent important user data.

Django REST Framework's 'perform_destroy()' (called by DestroyModelMixin) deletes objects without any soft-delete behavior. Many production applications need 'deleted_at' timestamp instead of actual deletion for audit trails. Implementing soft-delete: override 'perform_destroy()' to set 'deleted_at' field; override 'get_queryset()' to exclude soft-deleted objects with '.filter(deleted_at__isnull=True)'. Use a custom manager to always exclude deleted records. Danger: if you add soft-delete after initial deployment, existing code doing '.all()' or '.filter()' without the custom manager will include deleted records. Use 'django-safedelete' library for a consistent soft-delete implementation.

Django's 'request.user' is populated by 'AuthenticationMiddleware', which must run after 'SessionMiddleware'. If middleware is misconfigured, 'request.user' is 'AnonymousUser' even for authenticated users. Similarly, DRF's authentication runs separately from Django's session authentication — 'request.user' in DRF is set by DRF's authentication classes, not Django's middleware. A view using DRF's 'APIView' has DRF auth; a plain Django view uses Django's session auth. Mixing both in the same project can cause confusion where 'request.user' behaves differently depending on which view class is used. Document this dual-auth system clearly.

Django's 'TestCase.setUpTestData()' runs once per class and shares data across all test methods in the class via a transaction savepoint. Modifying the shared data in a test method corrupts it for subsequent tests in the same class. Objects created in 'setUpTestData' are wrapped in a transaction; mutations are rolled back between tests only if they go through the database (ORM operations are rolled back, but in-memory modifications to model instances are NOT rolled back). Always create fresh data or call 'refresh_from_db()' in tests that modify shared objects. For truly isolated tests, move to 'setUp()' which runs per-test.

Django's 'model inheritance' with proxy models shares the database table but allows different Python behavior (custom methods, managers, Meta options). A subtle failure: creating a proxy model and adding a custom default manager that filters results — users of the proxy model see filtered results, users of the parent model see all results, both operating on the same table. This can cause confusion and subtle bugs when code inadvertently uses the parent model instead of the proxy. Document clearly which model to use in which context. Proxy model managers don't affect the parent model's querysets.

Django's 'static files' with 'collectstatic' copies files from each app's 'static/' directory to 'STATIC_ROOT'. If two apps have files with the same path (e.g., both have 'static/css/style.css'), the last app processed wins — silently overwriting the other's file. This causes mysterious UI breakage where one app's styles override another's. Fix: namespace static files within app subdirectories: 'myapp/static/myapp/css/style.css' and reference as 'myapp/css/style.css'. Use 'STATICFILES_DIRS' for project-level static files that don't belong to any app. Run 'manage.py findstatic <filename>' to identify which app's file would win.

Django's 'JSONField' (added in Django 3.1) stores JSON data natively but has cross-database compatibility issues. 'JSONField' on SQLite uses TEXT; on PostgreSQL uses JSONB (queryable); on MySQL uses JSON type. Queries like 'filter(data__key="value")' work across all backends but 'filter(data__contains={...})' and JSON path lookups are PostgreSQL-specific. A common failure: developing with SQLite where JSON queries silently fall back to string matching, then deploying to PostgreSQL where behavior differs. Test with the target database backend (or use 'django.test.utils.override_settings' with PostgreSQL in CI even if SQLite in development).

Django's migration 'dependencies' field controls migration execution order. When two migrations in different apps both modify the same model (through ForeignKey relationships), failing to add explicit dependencies causes 'OperationalError: no such table' or 'column does not exist' on fresh database setup. The migration auto-detector adds dependencies for ForeignKey fields but not for data migrations or operations that implicitly depend on another app's schema. When writing data migrations, explicitly add dependencies: 'dependencies = [("app1", "0005_add_field"), ("app2", "0003_other")]'. Test 'migrate' from scratch on a clean database in CI to catch missing dependencies.

Django's 'aggregate()' with multiple aggregations on the same queryset can produce unexpected results when combined with 'annotate()'. Adding 'annotate(Count("related"))' before 'aggregate(Sum("amount"))' can inflate aggregate results due to JOINs multiplying rows. The safe order: use 'aggregate()' alone for grand totals, or use subqueries. For combined annotation + aggregation, use 'Subquery' expressions: 'annotate(total=Subquery(Order.objects.filter(user=OuterRef("pk")).values("user").annotate(s=Sum("amount")).values("s"))'. Django's ORM generates different SQL depending on annotation order; always verify with 'queryset.query' or Django Debug Toolbar.

Django's 'DATABASES["default"]["CONN_MAX_AGE"]' controls database connection persistence. The default is 0 (close connection after each request), which is safe but creates a new connection per request — expensive for high-traffic apps. Setting 'CONN_MAX_AGE = None' (persistent connections) or a high value can cause connection pool exhaustion on PostgreSQL (which has a 'max_connections' limit, often 100). With gunicorn 4 workers and CONN_MAX_AGE=500, you can hold up to 4 persistent connections per worker. For Django apps behind load balancers, use PgBouncer as a connection pooler rather than relying on CONN_MAX_AGE alone.

Django's 'reverse()' URL function fails with 'NoReverseMatch' when the URL pattern doesn't match the arguments provided. A common failure when using 'namespaced' URLs: 'reverse("profile")' fails when the URL is in a namespace — must use 'reverse("accounts:profile")'. Another failure: calling 'reverse()' at module level (outside a request context) works in most cases but fails for URLs that require arguments. In class-based views, 'self.get_success_url()' often uses 'reverse_lazy()' instead of 'reverse()' — lazy evaluation defers resolution until needed, preventing import-time failures.

Django's 'STATIC_URL' and 'MEDIA_URL' must be different paths and both need to be served differently. Static files (CSS/JS/images): collected by 'collectstatic', served from 'STATIC_ROOT'. Media files (user uploads): stored in 'MEDIA_ROOT', served from 'MEDIA_URL'. In development, Django can serve media files when DEBUG=True with a URL pattern. In production, BOTH must be served by Nginx or a CDN — Django itself should not serve them. A common failure: setting up static file serving correctly but forgetting MEDIA_URL configuration, causing user uploads to be saved but never accessible via URLs. Always verify both serve correctly after deployment.

Django's 'signals are synchronous' — a slow signal receiver blocks the entire request. A 'post_save' signal that sends an HTTP request to a third-party API or processes images blocks the view until it completes. This is a common performance trap: adding slow operations to signals because it seems convenient. Rule: signal receivers should complete in under 10ms. For any slower operation, dispatch a Celery task instead. Also, exceptions in signal receivers propagate to the sender unless explicitly caught — a failing email signal can abort a database write if uncaught. Wrap signal receiver bodies in try/except.

Django's 'ModelAdmin.readonly_fields' only makes fields non-editable in the admin interface; it does NOT prevent the field from being updated via the Django ORM or Django shell. Admin protections are for the admin UI only. A common security misconception: marking 'created_by' as readonly in admin thinking it prevents tampering — but anyone with Django shell access or using the ORM directly can modify it. For truly immutable fields, enforce immutability in the model's 'save()' method: check if the field has changed since creation and raise 'ValidationError' if someone tries to modify it.

Django REST Framework's 'request.data' is parsed lazily and can only be read once. Unlike Django's 'request.body', 'request.data' in DRF is a mutable QueryDict or dict depending on Content-Type. Accessing 'request.data' in a permission class (which runs before the view) is fine — DRF parses it once and caches it. However, if middleware reads 'request.body' before DRF processes the request, 'request.body' is consumed and subsequent reads return empty bytes. This is the same issue as Django's request.body stream consumption. In middleware, use 'request.body' and cache it: 'request._body = request.body' to allow re-reading.

Django's 'migration autodetector' does not detect changes to 'validators' list on model fields — validators are not stored in migration state. If you add a validator to a field, 'makemigrations' won't generate a migration, and existing data won't be validated. Validators only run on 'full_clean()', forms, and DRF serializers. Database-level constraints (CheckConstraint, UniqueConstraint) DO generate migrations and are enforced by the database. Never rely solely on Django validators for data integrity on fields where the rule should always hold — pair with database constraints. This also means removing a validator doesn't require a migration.

Django's admin 'search_fields' with foreign key traversal can cause very slow queries on large tables. 'search_fields = ["user__email"]' generates a LIKE '%term%' query with a JOIN to the users table, which cannot use indexes effectively. On tables with millions of records, admin searches become unusable. Fix: use 'django-admin-searchable-dropdown' for foreign key searches, add database-level full text search indexes, or override 'get_search_results()' to implement efficient search. Always test admin search performance with production-size data in staging before going live.

Django REST Framework's 'PrimaryKeyRelatedField' by default allows any object in the queryset as a valid value, without ownership validation. If 'queryset=Order.objects.all()' and a user submits another user's order ID, DRF validates it as valid. Always scope the queryset to the current user: override 'get_fields()' or use 'CurrentUserDefault': 'class OrderSerializer(ModelSerializer): user = serializers.HiddenField(default=serializers.CurrentUserDefault())' and scope related querysets in the view's 'get_serializer_context()'. Never trust that client-provided IDs reference objects the user owns without explicit ownership validation.

Django's 'ORM QuerySet is lazy' — it doesn't execute SQL until evaluated (iteration, slicing, 'list()', 'len()', 'bool()'). Assigning a QuerySet to a variable and passing it to functions doesn't trigger evaluation. The failure mode: 'qs = MyModel.objects.filter(...)' followed by later code that modifies the underlying data and then evaluates the same 'qs' — the QuerySet re-executes the SQL at evaluation time, getting fresh data (which might differ from when it was created). Developers sometimes expect the QuerySet to capture a snapshot at assignment time. Cache the results explicitly: 'items = list(MyModel.objects.filter(...))' for a point-in-time snapshot.

Django's 'TestClient' does not enforce 'ALLOWED_HOSTS' by default (Django sets 'SERVER_NAME' to 'testserver' and disables the check). Tests that check redirect URLs or absolute URLs using 'testserver' as hostname will pass locally but fail in production where the actual domain is different. Always test URL generation with realistic hostnames in integration tests. For testing ALLOWED_HOSTS specifically, use 'self.client.get("/path/", HTTP_HOST="invalid.example.com")' and verify the 400 response. Also, TestClient uses Django's test runner's database, not the actual database — migrations must be run before tests.

Django's 'session_key' is regenerated on 'request.session.cycle_key()' or 'login()' to prevent session fixation attacks. Failing to call 'cycle_key()' after authentication (e.g., after a successful 'authenticate()' call without using Django's 'login()' function) leaves the same session ID from pre-authentication state, enabling session fixation. Django's 'auth.login()' automatically calls 'cycle_key()'. If you manually authenticate and set session variables without calling 'login()', you must explicitly call 'request.session.cycle_key()'. This is a common oversight when implementing custom authentication flows.

Django's 'migration zero-downtime' practices are essential for production deployments. Adding a NOT NULL column without a default causes 'ALTER TABLE' to lock the table until all existing rows are backfilled — can lock for minutes on large tables. Safe approach: 1) Add nullable column (migration 1), 2) Backfill with a data migration, 3) Add constraint (migration 2), 4) Make not-null (migration 3). Use 'django-pg-zero-downtime-migrations' for PostgreSQL to ensure all migrations use safe ALTER TABLE statements. For renaming columns, use add_field + data_migration + remove_field pattern; never just rename — it's a drop and recreate in terms of data risk.

Django REST Framework's 'authentication_classes = []' on a view completely disables authentication — not just authentication enforcement. DRF first authenticates (populates request.user), then checks permissions. Setting empty authentication_classes means request.user is always AnonymousUser, even if the client sends valid credentials. Setting 'permission_classes = [AllowAny]' allows unauthenticated access while still populating request.user for authenticated clients. Use 'AllowAny' permission class to allow public access while preserving authentication context. Use empty authentication_classes only for truly public endpoints that must never identify users.

Django's 'SECRET_KEY_FALLBACKS' (Django 4.1+) enables key rotation without immediately invalidating all sessions, CSRF tokens, and signed cookies. A common failure during key rotation: changing SECRET_KEY without fallbacks instantly invalidates all active sessions, logging out all users. Proper rotation: 1) Move current key to SECRET_KEY_FALLBACKS, 2) Generate new SECRET_KEY, 3) Deploy, 4) After sufficient time (session lifetime), remove old key from fallbacks. Without this procedure, a key rotation causes a mass logout event visible to all users. Django signs cookies and tokens with the primary SECRET_KEY but validates against fallbacks too.

Django REST Framework's 'Serializer.is_valid()' returns False on validation errors but does NOT raise an exception. Code that doesn't check 'if serializer.is_valid():' before calling 'serializer.save()' raises 'AssertionError: You must call .is_valid() before calling .save()'. The pattern 'serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)' is the recommended approach for views — it raises 'ValidationError' which DRF's exception handler converts to a 400 response with error details automatically. Without 'raise_exception=True', you must manually handle errors: 'if not serializer.is_valid(): return Response(serializer.errors, status=400)'. Use 'raise_exception=True' consistently in views.

Django's 'QuerySet caching' only applies to already-evaluated querysets. A queryset is cached after first evaluation, but subsequent filters or modifications create new querysets that are not cached. 'qs = User.objects.all(); list(qs); qs.filter(active=True)' — the second filter creates a NEW queryset and hits the database again. Developers sometimes assume all operations on 'qs' use the cached data. Also, model instances have an internal prefetch cache — accessing 'user.groups.all()' twice on the same instance uses the cache only if 'prefetch_related' was used; otherwise, it queries twice. Use 'refresh_from_db()' explicitly to force refresh when needed.

Django's 'admin.site.register()' without a custom ModelAdmin class uses the default admin which shows all model fields in list and detail views. This exposes potentially sensitive fields (password hashes, internal tokens, flags) in the admin panel. Always define a ModelAdmin class with explicit 'list_display', 'list_filter', 'search_fields', 'readonly_fields', and 'exclude'. Also: Django admin does not enforce row-level permissions by default — all staff users can see and edit all objects of registered models. Use 'get_queryset()' override to filter objects by ownership if needed. Treat the admin as a power tool, not a secure interface.

Django's 'forms.FileField' and 'request.FILES' provide the uploaded file as an 'InMemoryUploadedFile' or 'TemporaryUploadedFile' depending on size (configurable with 'FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE'). A common failure: accessing 'uploaded_file.read()' and then passing the same file object elsewhere — the file pointer is at the end after reading. Call 'uploaded_file.seek(0)' before re-reading. Temporary files are deleted when the request ends — process them within the request or copy to permanent storage. Never store the path to a temporary upload file for later processing; copy the content or save it to a model's FileField immediately.

Django's 'test isolation' is broken when tests use 'setUpClass()' instead of 'setUpTestData()' — 'setUpClass()' doesn't have transaction rollback support built in. Data created in 'setUpClass()' persists across all tests in the class AND bleeds into other test classes if the test runner doesn't reset the database. Use 'setUpTestData()' for class-scoped test data with automatic rollback. Also, test isolation breaks when tests use signals that create side effects not cleaned up after each test. Use 'mock.patch' or 'disconnect' signals in setUp/tearDown to prevent signal side effects from polluting other tests.

Django's 'FIXTURE' loading with 'loaddata' is order-dependent for ForeignKey relationships. Fixtures must be loaded in order of their dependencies (parent before child). Using 'natural keys' in fixtures avoids this by using human-readable identifiers instead of PKs, but natural keys require the model to implement 'natural_key()' and 'NaturalKeyManager'. A common CI failure: fixtures load correctly in development because of lucky ordering, but fail in CI where tables are empty and loading order is different. Use factory_boy instead of fixtures for most test data — factories handle dependencies automatically and are more maintainable than JSON fixtures.

Django REST Framework's 'permission_classes' applied to a ViewSet apply to ALL actions including 'list', 'create', 'retrieve', 'update', 'destroy'. If you want different permissions per action (e.g., allow unauthenticated read but require auth for write), override 'get_permissions()': 'def get_permissions(self): if self.action in ["list", "retrieve"]: return [AllowAny()]; return [IsAuthenticated()]'. A common failure: applying 'IsAuthenticated' globally to a ModelViewSet and wondering why public read endpoints return 401. The 'permission_classes = [IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly]' shortcut works for simple cases but 'get_permissions()' provides full control per action.

Django's 'get_user_model()' can fail with 'django.contrib.auth.get_user_model has not been called' if called before Django's app registry is fully initialized. This happens at module level (top of file), in metaclass code, or in migration files that import user model directly. Always import 'get_user_model' and call it inside functions, not at module level. In migrations, use 'settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL' string for ForeignKey: 'ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=CASCADE)' — never import the User model directly in migrations as the model state may differ from current code.

Django's 'prefetch_related' cache is invalidated when you modify the model instance. Calling 'user.orders.add(order)' or 'user.orders.clear()' after prefetching clears the prefetch cache. Subsequent accesses to 'user.orders.all()' will execute a new database query. This is correct behavior but surprises developers who modify relationships mid-loop expecting the prefetch cache to stay current. Also: accessing 'user.orders.filter(status="active")' after prefetching 'orders' does NOT use the prefetch cache — it executes a new query. The prefetch cache only serves calls to 'user.orders.all()' exactly.

Django's 'upload_to' parameter in FileField/ImageField can be a callable for dynamic paths: 'def user_directory_path(instance, filename): return f"uploads/{instance.user.pk}/{filename}"'. However, when the model instance is being created for the first time, 'instance.pk' might be None if the instance hasn't been saved yet. Using 'instance.pk' in 'upload_to' during initial creation generates 'None' in the path. Fix: use 'instance.user.pk' (FK to an existing object) or use a UUID field as part of the path. For updating file fields, the old file is NOT deleted — you must handle old file cleanup explicitly to avoid storage accumulation.

Django's 'signals.disconnect()' must be called with the exact same receiver function reference used in 'connect()'. If the signal was connected using a lambda, you cannot disconnect it by creating a new identical lambda — lambdas have different identities each time they're created. The correct pattern for disconnectable signal connections: store the connected function: 'def handler(sender, **kwargs): ...; post_save.connect(handler, sender=MyModel)'. To disconnect: 'post_save.disconnect(handler, sender=MyModel)'. In tests, use 'mock.patch.object' or 'unittest.mock' to temporarily replace signal receivers, which automatically restores them after the test.

Django's 'double-save' anti-pattern in signals: a 'post_save' signal handler that calls 'instance.save()' triggers another 'post_save' signal, causing infinite recursion or at minimum double the database writes. Use 'update_fields' to prevent re-triggering: 'if not kwargs.get("update_fields"): instance.update_fields(["computed_field"])'. Use 'update_fields=["status"]' in the signal to mark it as a targeted update, then check in the signal: 'if update_fields and "status" in update_fields: return'. Better: avoid modifying the originating model in its own post_save signal. Use 'pre_save' to modify the instance before the original save, not after.

Django's 'bulk_update()' (Django 2.2+) requires 'update_fields' to be specified explicitly — unlike 'save()' which updates all fields by default. Calling 'MyModel.objects.bulk_update(objects, ["status", "updated_at"])' is correct. Omitting fields (not providing the list) raises TypeError. Also, 'bulk_update()' does not call 'save()' (same as 'bulk_create'), so validators, signals, and custom save logic don't run. For large updates where signals are needed, process in batches: chunk the queryset and call 'save()' per object. 'bulk_update()' with very large batch sizes (thousands of objects) can generate SQL that exceeds database parameter limits.

Django's 'DATABASES setting with SSL requires' 'OPTIONS': 'DATABASES = {"default": {"ENGINE": "django.db.backends.postgresql", "OPTIONS": {"sslmode": "require"}}}'. Without 'sslmode': 'require', connections to PostgreSQL may fall back to unencrypted connections, especially when connecting to managed database services (AWS RDS, Heroku Postgres) that support both. Many developers assume SSL is automatic but it requires explicit configuration. Use 'sslmode': 'verify-full' with CA certificate for maximum security in production. Check active connections' SSL status with 'SELECT ssl FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid()'.

Django REST Framework's 'ModelSerializer.validate_{field_name}()' validators run per-field after type coercion. A common mistake: 'validate_email()' attempting to check database uniqueness by calling 'User.objects.filter(email=value).exists()' — this doesn't account for the case where the user is updating their own email (the current email would appear as a duplicate). For update scenarios, access the instance via 'self.instance': 'if User.objects.filter(email=value).exclude(pk=self.instance.pk if self.instance else None).exists(): raise ValidationError(...)'. Always handle both create (no instance) and update (has instance) in uniqueness validators.

Django's 'cache_page' decorator doesn't work correctly with views that return different content for different users (authentication-dependent views). '@cache_page(300)' caches the first response and serves it to ALL subsequent visitors, regardless of authentication state. A logged-in user's private content may be served to anonymous users. Fix: use 'vary_on_cookie()' or 'vary_on_headers("Authorization")' decorators to key the cache per authentication state, or use low-level caching with user-specific keys. 'cache_page' is safe only for truly public, anonymous-accessible content that is identical for all users.

Django REST Framework's 'ListSerializer' handles many=True serializer behavior. When using 'many=True', DRF creates a 'ListSerializer' wrapping your serializer. Overriding 'create()' or 'update()' in the child serializer doesn't affect many=True behavior — you must override 'many_init()' class method or subclass 'ListSerializer' and specify 'list_serializer_class'. A common failure: expecting 'MySerializer(data=list_data, many=True).save()' to call 'MySerializer.create()' for each item — it actually calls 'ListSerializer.create()' which calls child.create() per item. For bulk update with many=True, this is especially tricky to implement correctly.

Django's 'ManyRelatedManager.add()' and 'set()' for M2M fields bypass the 'through' model when one exists. If you define a through model, you cannot use 'user.groups.add(group)' — you must create the through model instance directly: 'UserGroup.objects.create(user=user, group=group, role="admin")'. The 'add()' method raises 'AttributeError' when a through model with extra fields exists. This is a common confusion when adding a through model to an existing M2M relationship — existing code using 'add()' breaks. Audit all M2M relationship usage before adding a through model.

Django's 'IntegerChoices' and 'TextChoices' (Django 3.0+) provide enum-like behavior for choice fields. 'class Status(models.IntegerChoices): DRAFT = 0, "Draft"; PUBLISHED = 1, "Published"'. Use: 'status = models.IntegerField(choices=Status.choices, default=Status.DRAFT)'. The failure mode: legacy code using magic numbers (status=0, status=1) instead of 'Status.DRAFT' — when the integer values are reordered in the enum, all existing code breaks silently. Always use the symbolic name: 'Article.objects.filter(status=Status.PUBLISHED)'. Migration note: changing enum values requires a data migration to update existing database values.

Django's 'admin customization' with 'inline models' creates N+1 queries when displaying related objects. 'TabularInline' for a model with 20 related objects executes a SELECT per inline object by default. Fix: define 'get_queryset()' on the inline to add 'select_related()' or 'prefetch_related()'. Also, 'raw_id_fields' for ForeignKey fields in inlines prevents loading ALL related objects in a dropdown — critical when the related model has thousands of records: 'raw_id_fields = ("product",)' shows a text input with a popup search instead. Monitor admin performance separately from public API — admin N+1 queries are a common source of admin timeouts.

Django's 'class Meta: ordering' on a model applies a default ordering to all querysets. While convenient, this ordering adds an ORDER BY clause to every query on the model, even queries where ordering doesn't matter (existence checks, aggregations, update operations). This can prevent index usage and slow down queries significantly. 'exists()' and 'count()' queries are affected: 'MyModel.objects.filter(...).order_by().count()' is faster than 'MyModel.objects.filter(...).count()' when Meta.ordering is set. Clear ordering for bulk operations: 'MyModel.objects.all().order_by()'. Consider removing Meta.ordering and adding explicit '.order_by()' only where needed.

Django's 'ATOMIC_REQUESTS = True' combined with database constraints can produce confusing error messages. When a view raises an IntegrityError inside an atomic request, Django catches it, marks the transaction as needing rollback, and returns a 500 error. The original IntegrityError is logged but the HTTP response doesn't convey what went wrong. Developers see generic 500 errors in production when there are duplicate inserts, null constraint violations, etc. Solution: catch IntegrityError explicitly inside views and return appropriate 400/409 responses. Use 'transaction.savepoint()' to protect specific operations without aborting the entire request transaction.

Django's 'migration conflicts' occur when two developers create migrations in the same app simultaneously based on the same parent migration. Both migrations will have the same 'dependencies' but different operations. Running 'migrate' with both fails. Resolution: 'python manage.py makemigrations --merge' creates a merge migration that depends on both conflicting migrations. However, if the conflicting migrations touch the same table, the merge might have ordering issues. Prevention: always pull and run 'migrate' before starting work; use feature flags for parallel feature development to avoid parallel schema changes; communicate schema changes in team channels.

Django's 'LANGUAGE_CODE' and 'USE_I18N' settings control internationalization. A common failure: deploying a Django app with 'USE_TZ = False' (timezone-naive datetimes) and then enabling it ('USE_TZ = True') after data exists in the database — all existing naive datetimes are now treated as UTC by Django but may actually be local time. Never change 'USE_TZ' after data is stored. Start all projects with 'USE_TZ = True'. Another failure: 'USE_I18N = False' disables translation but you've hardcoded strings in views — later enabling i18n requires retrofitting all strings with _('...'), which is a significant refactor.

Django REST Framework's 'action decorator' with 'detail=False' creates list-level actions (e.g., '/users/bulk_create/') and 'detail=True' creates object-level actions (e.g., '/users/123/activate/'). A common failure: mismatching 'detail=True/False' with the action's logic — using 'detail=True' for a bulk action that doesn't need a pk, causing all callers to include a pk in the URL unnecessarily. Also, actions with 'methods=["get"]' will appear in the URL router but won't be documented in schema unless you add '@extend_schema'. DRF's DefaultRouter generates both list and detail URLs automatically from registered ViewSets.

Django's 'related_name' conflict occurs when two ForeignKeys from the same model point to the same target model. 'class Comment(models.Model): post = ForeignKey(Post, related_name="comments"); reply_to = ForeignKey(Post, related_name="replies")'. Without unique 'related_name', Django raises a system check error: 'Reverse accessor clash'. When using abstract models with ForeignKeys, use '%(class)s' in related_name to make them unique per subclass: 'ForeignKey(Post, related_name="%(class)s_comments")'. This generates 'articlecomment_set', 'photocomment_set' etc. on the target model. Always define explicit 'related_name' — never rely on Django's automatic '_set' naming.

Django's 'ORM .update()' method bypasses model validation, signals, and 'save()' method entirely. 'Article.objects.filter(pk=1).update(title="new")' skips 'pre_save'/'post_save' signals, 'clean()'/'full_clean()', and custom 'save()' logic. This is intentional for performance but surprises developers who rely on signals or save() for derived field updates. A specific failure: using 'update()' on a model with a 'modified_at = auto_now=True' field — 'update()' does NOT trigger auto_now, so the modification timestamp stays stale. Use 'update()' only when you explicitly don't need signals/validation, and pass 'updated_at=timezone.now()' explicitly when needed.

Django's 'admin URL security': the default admin URL '/admin/' is well-known and targeted by bots. Move it: 'path("secret-path/", admin.site.urls)'. But 'security through obscurity' is not real security — always add: 'ADMIN_MEDIA_PREFIX' settings, require 2FA for admin users ('django-two-factor-auth'), restrict by IP via middleware or Nginx, and use 'SECURE_ADMIN_LOGIN' settings. 'staff' access does not equal 'superuser' — audit who has is_staff=True. Never give all staff users 'is_superuser=True'. Regularly audit admin users: staff accounts should be service accounts with minimal permissions, not personal accounts.

Django REST Framework's 'pagination prevents count queries' — but 'PageNumberPagination' executes a COUNT(*) query to calculate 'total' pages. For very large tables, COUNT(*) is slow (PostgreSQL must scan indexes or tables). Solutions: disable count with custom pagination: 'class NoPaginationCountMixin: def paginate_queryset(self, qs, request, view=None): ... (skip count); use "next"/"previous" only'. CursorPagination avoids count entirely. For tables with millions of rows, estimate count via PostgreSQL statistics: 'SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE relname = "myapp_mymodel"' (not exact but fast). Always profile pagination performance on production-scale data.

Django's 'transaction savepoint release' semantics are database-specific and unintuitive. Releasing a savepoint in PostgreSQL does NOT commit the data — it just removes the savepoint marker. The data is committed when the outermost transaction commits. 'with transaction.atomic(): savepoint_id = transaction.savepoint(); ...; transaction.savepoint_commit(savepoint_id)' — the commit here means 'proceed, don't rollback to this savepoint' not 'commit to database'. 'transaction.savepoint_rollback(savepoint_id)' rolls back to the savepoint state. This distinction matters when debugging transaction behavior: seeing 'savepoint_commit()' in code does not mean the transaction is committed.

Django's 'SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER' must be configured when behind a load balancer for 'request.is_secure()' to return True. Without this, 'request.is_secure()' always returns False even for HTTPS traffic, breaking: SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE (secure cookies), redirect checks, 'HttpRequest.build_absolute_uri()'. Configure: 'SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER = ("HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO", "https")' if the load balancer sends this header. Only set this if you control and trust the proxy — a malicious client could spoof this header. Combine with 'SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True' for full HTTPS enforcement.

Django's 'ORM aggregate with GROUP BY' behavior is non-obvious when combining with annotations. 'User.objects.annotate(order_count=Count("orders")).filter(order_count__gt=5)' works. But 'User.objects.annotate(order_count=Count("orders")).filter(order_count__gt=5, orders__status="paid")' adds a WHERE clause BEFORE aggregation, changing the semantics: it counts only paid orders (not total) and only returns users who have at least one paid order. Use 'Subquery' or 'filter()' before 'annotate()' for pre-aggregation filtering vs 'filter()' after 'annotate()' for post-aggregation filtering. Django generates different SQL for filter position relative to annotate.

NEVER use float for financial calculations. IEEE 754 floats cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly: 0.1 + 0.2 == 0.30000000000000004. This causes balance discrepancies, rounding errors in reports, and audit failures. Use Python's decimal.Decimal with explicit precision, or store amounts as integers (cents/paise) in the database. UNSAID: Django's DecimalField maps to PostgreSQL NUMERIC, which is exact. FloatField maps to DOUBLE PRECISION — never use FloatField for money. Extraction signal: any financial field stored as float is a critical bug.

NEVER implement fraud detection as a simple threshold rule on a single attribute (e.g., block if amount > $1000). Fraudsters trivially learn and evade single-threshold rules. Effective fraud detection requires multi-signal scoring: velocity checks (N transactions in M minutes from same card/IP), behavioral deviation (transaction pattern vs historical baseline), device fingerprint consistency, geolocation anomaly (impossible travel), and BIN (Bank Identification Number) country vs billing country mismatch. UNSAID: implementing fraud detection in application code creates a race condition — fraud checks must complete before the payment is authorized, adding latency. Purpose-built services (Stripe Radar, Sift, Kount) handle this with pre-built ML models. In-house solutions require labeled fraud data (rare in new products) to train effectively. Extraction signal: any fraud system with only amount-based rules is security theater.

NEVER trust client-reported amounts for financial transactions. Amount validation must happen server-side against the authoritative price source. A common vulnerability: the client sends the price it wants to pay for an item, and the server trusts it without verification. This allows users to submit $0.01 for any product. UNSAID: the correct pattern is for the client to send the item ID and quantity only, and the server computes the total from its own price records. For payment processors, always verify the amount from the payment intent/session object returned by the processor — never from the client's callback. Stripe's checkout.session.completed webhook contains the authoritative amount_total field. Extraction signal: any endpoint that accepts amount as a client parameter for a purchase is vulnerable to price manipulation.

Race conditions in financial transaction processing cause double-spending vulnerabilities when two concurrent requests both read the same balance before either write completes, allowing a user to spend more than they have. UNSAID: Classic race condition: User has $100. Request A reads balance ($100), Request B reads balance ($100), Request A writes new balance ($50 after $50 purchase), Request B writes new balance ($50 after $50 purchase) — user has now spent $100 twice. Prevention strategies by reliability level: 1) Database-level SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock the row during the transaction (simplest, works for single-database), 2) Optimistic locking with version number (check that version hasn't changed since read, retry if it has), 3) Idempotency keys on payment requests (second identical request returns the first request's result). For distributed systems, use saga pattern or two-phase commit for multi-service financial operations. Never use application-level locks for financial state — they don't survive process restarts.

Currency rounding rules vary by currency and context — using a single rounding algorithm (always round half up) for all currencies produces incorrect results for currencies with specific rounding requirements. UNSAID: ISO 4217 defines decimal places per currency, but rounding rules vary: some currencies round to 5-cent multiples (Swiss Franc), others to 1-cent minimums. For multi-currency e-commerce: Swedish Krona rounds to nearest 1 öre (2 decimal places), Japanese Yen has 0 decimal places (never show 0.5¥). Tax calculations have additional rounding rules: some jurisdictions require rounding per line item before totaling (causing slight discrepancies from rounding total first). The GAAP/IFRS rounding rule is 'banker's rounding' (round half to even), not 'round half up' — this prevents systematic bias in large-scale financial aggregation. Real rounding errors: rounding 10,000 transactions of $0.0055 up produces $55, while banker's rounding produces $27.50. Accumulated over millions of transactions, this is material. Always specify rounding mode explicitly and document which standard you're following.

evidence_refs.description: ISO 4217 currency codes and decimal places

statement: Stripe webhook signature verification is bypassed by developers who skip it for convenience during development, creating a security vulnerability where attackers can forge payment success events. UNSAID: Stripe webhooks are sent to a public endpoint — without signature verification, any attacker can POST a fake 'payment.succeeded' event to your endpoint and receive the goods/service without paying. The verification code: stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(payload, sig_header, webhook_secret) — if this throws, the event is forged. The signing secret is different from the API key — it's generated per webhook endpoint in the Stripe dashboard. Common development mistake: bypassing verification with a try/catch that catches signature errors and proceeds anyway. Another pitfall: using the test mode signing secret in production (or vice versa) — test and live webhooks have different signing secrets. For local development, use the Stripe CLI ('stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/webhooks') which handles signing automatically. Audit your webhook handler code: any handler that doesn't call constructEvent (or equivalent) before processing is a security vulnerability.

Bank reconciliation failures occur when application-level transaction records diverge from the payment processor's records due to missing webhook events, failed API calls, or async state updates that never completed. UNSAID: The scenario: your database shows 1,000 successful payments, but Stripe's dashboard shows 1,003. The 3 discrepancies could be: webhooks that failed to deliver, race conditions in webhook processing, or events processed in wrong order. Reconciliation system: weekly job that fetches all Stripe events from the last period via Stripe API (listEvents) and compares to your local records. Any Stripe-side success with no local record indicates missed webhook — trigger the webhook handler with the event data to fill the gap. Any local-side success with no Stripe record indicates a data integrity bug — investigate immediately. The reconciliation query: SELECT stripe_payment_intent_id FROM local_payments WHERE stripe_payment_intent_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM fetched_stripe_events) UNION SELECT id FROM fetched_stripe_events WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT stripe_payment_intent_id FROM local_payments). Run reconciliation daily for high-value transactions, weekly for lower-risk use cases.

Subscription cancellation timing bugs cause customers to be double-charged or under-served — the most common error is immediately revoking access upon cancellation rather than allowing access through the paid period end. UNSAID: When a customer cancels on day 15 of a monthly subscription, they've paid for days 1-31. Immediately revoking access means you've charged for unused service, which violates consumer protection laws in many jurisdictions and creates chargebacks. Correct pattern: cancel the subscription renewal at Stripe (stripe.subscription.cancel_at_period_end = true), maintain access until the period end date, then revoke access when the subscription truly expires. The database pattern: subscriptions table with status (active/canceled/expired), current_period_end (the date access should expire), and cancel_at_period_end (boolean). The feature gate checks: is subscription.status == 'active' AND current_period_end > now()? The cancellation webhook (customer.subscription.updated with cancel_at_period_end=true) triggers setting the cancel flag but NOT revoking access. The subscription.deleted webhook (fired at period end) triggers actual access revocation.

evidence_refs.description: Stripe subscription cancellation at period end documentation

statement: Discount and coupon implementation fails to account for compounding discounts, discount stacking limits, and expiry enforcement, creating exploitable pricing vulnerabilities. UNSAID: Common vulnerabilities: 1) Two 50% discounts applied to get 75% off (each applies to the already-discounted price) instead of 50% once, 2) Expired coupons accepted because the expiry check is client-side only, 3) One-time-use codes reusable after the first application because no concurrent redemption lock prevents race conditions. Correct implementation: all discount logic server-side, expiry checked against server time (not client time), redemption recorded with SELECT FOR UPDATE to prevent concurrent redemption, maximum_redemptions enforced atomically. For subscription discounts: distinguish between discount on first invoice only (apply as a coupon to the subscription with duration: 'once') vs permanent discount (duration: 'forever') — Stripe handles this if using Stripe's coupon/discount system rather than building your own. For marketplace-funded vs seller-funded discounts, track funding source in the coupon record to correctly apportion the discount impact in reporting.

Storing card data 'for user convenience' in an improperly secured custom database field creates PCI DSS Level 1 compliance requirements and potential criminal liability under data breach laws. UNSAID: The scenario: a developer stores credit card numbers in the users table's 'saved_payment_info' JSON field 'just temporarily' during development.
