UNSAID: `readOnly=true` with Hibernate prevents automatic flushing of the first-level cache — changes to managed entities are not persisted. This is useful for read operations where you manipulate entities without wanting writes. For Spring Data JPA: mark all query repository methods with `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` by default, override with `@Transactional` for write methods. Read replicas: `@Transactional(readOnly=true)` combined with Spring's `AbstractRoutingDataSource` to route to read replicas.

FAILURE: Spring Boot lazy initialization (`spring.main.lazy-initialization=true`) masking startup errors in production. Lazy initialization defers bean creation until the bean is first requested — startup is faster but errors in bean configuration (missing dependencies, misconfigured properties) are discovered only when the bean is first used, potentially in production under load. A broken `DataSource` configuration would not fail at startup — the application starts, then the first request that needs DB access fails. Fix: use lazy initialization only for development (faster restarts). In production: keep `lazy-initialization=false` (default) to fail fast on misconfiguration. UNSAID: targeted laziness is acceptable: `@Lazy` on specific expensive beans that are rarely used. For testing: `spring.main.lazy-initialization=true` combined with `@MockBean` for fast test startup that doesn't instantiate unused beans. `SpringApplication.setLazyInitialization(true)` sets it programmatically. For health checks: if lazy init is used, ensure the readiness probe exercises all critical beans before traffic is routed. Spring Boot 2.2+ introduced lazy initialization. Context refresh time vs first-request latency tradeoff — in Kubernetes with autoscaling, slow startup may cause timeout during rollout.

FAILURE: Hibernate `LazyInitializationException` accessing lazy-loaded collection outside persistence context. Accessing a `@OneToMany(fetch=LAZY)` collection after the `EntityManager` is closed (outside `@Transactional` boundary, in service or controller layer) throws `LazyInitializationException: could not initialize proxy - no Session`. Common patterns: loading entity in repository, returning it to controller, controller accessing `entity.getOrders()` — the persistence context is closed between repository and controller. Fix: (1) `JOIN FETCH` in the query; (2) `@EntityGraph`; (3) use DTO projection; (4) open-session-in-view pattern (anti-pattern for production). UNSAID: `spring.jpa.open-in-view=true` (Spring Boot default!) keeps the persistence context open for the entire HTTP request — convenient but causes N+1 queries in controllers, binds database resources to HTTP threads, and hides architectural problems. Set `spring.jpa.open-in-view=false` in production and fix the underlying design. The correct architecture: load all needed data in the service layer (within `@Transactional`), return DTOs (not entities) to the controller layer. This eliminates lazy loading in controllers by design. Hibernate `@BatchSize(size=30)` as a partial mitigation — reduces N+1 to N/30+1 queries.

FAILURE: Spring Security CSRF protection disabled globally when only API endpoints need exemption. `http.csrf(AbstractHttpConfigurer::disable)` disables CSRF for all endpoints — including form-based login if present. This enables CSRF attacks on any state-changing endpoint accessible from a browser session. Fix: for REST APIs using JWT (stateless): CSRF is not needed (cookies not used for auth) — disabling is correct for the API portion. For mixed apps (API + form-based auth): `http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.ignoringRequestMatchers("/api/**"))` — disable only for API paths. UNSAID: CSRF protection is only needed when using cookie-based session authentication (JSESSIONID) — if authentication uses `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header, CSRF is not exploitable (cross-origin requests cannot set custom headers). Disabling CSRF is safe for stateless JWT APIs. The mistake is in mixed apps where disabling all CSRF leaves the form-based login endpoint unprotected. Spring Security 6 default: CSRF protection is enabled. For SPA with same-origin backend: use `CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse()` so JavaScript can read the CSRF token from cookie.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Async` exception handling silently swallowing errors on `void` return methods. `@Async` methods with `void` return type: uncaught exceptions are passed to `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` — if none is configured, exceptions are logged at ERROR level and silently discarded. The caller has no way to know the async task failed. `@Async void sendEmail(String to)` — if `sendEmail` throws, the caller is never notified. Fix: return `CompletableFuture<Void>`: `@Async CompletableFuture<Void> sendEmail(String to) { try { emailClient.send(to); return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null); } catch (Exception e) { return CompletableFuture.failedFuture(e); } }`. Caller: `sendEmail(to).exceptionally(e -> { log.error("Email failed", e); return null; })`. UNSAID: for fire-and-forget tasks where failure is acceptable (best-effort notifications): configure a custom `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` via `AsyncConfigurer` to at least log failures to your monitoring system. For critical async tasks: always use `CompletableFuture` return to propagate errors. `@Async` with `void` return is appropriate only for truly optional background work where failure has no business consequence.

FAILURE: Spring Boot circular dependency injection causing application startup failure. Circular dependencies: `ServiceA` depends on `ServiceB`, `ServiceB` depends on `ServiceA` — Spring 6+ throws `BeanCurrentlyInCreationException` by default (Spring 5 allowed this via field injection + proxy). Fix: (1) Redesign — the most common cause is a missing third service that both depend on; (2) use `@Lazy` on one constructor parameter to break the cycle: `ServiceA(ServiceB b)` → `ServiceA(@Lazy ServiceB b)`. (3) Extract the shared logic to a new `ServiceC` that both `ServiceA` and `ServiceB` depend on. UNSAID: `spring.main.allow-circular-references=true` re-enables circular dependency resolution (Spring 5 behavior) — strongly discouraged as it patches the symptom not the cause. Circular dependencies often indicate a layering violation: Service A calls Service B's user-facing method, Service B calls Service A's user-facing method — this means they're not properly layered. The correct fix is almost always to extract shared logic. `@Lazy` on `@Autowired` field is the quickest fix but hides the design issue. Use `spring.main.allow-circular-references=false` (default in Spring Boot 3) as a guard.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Value("${property}")` returning null or default when property is not in application.properties but expected from environment variable. Spring Boot maps environment variables to properties: `APP_DATABASE_URL` → `app.database.url` (relaxed binding). However, `@Value("${APP_DATABASE_URL}")` does NOT match the env var `APP_DATABASE_URL` — property placeholders are case-sensitive and use the property key format, not the env var format. Fix: use the property key format: `@Value("${app.database.url}")` and set the env var `APP_DATABASE_URL=value` (Spring Boot's relaxed binding maps it). Or use `@ConfigurationProperties` with `prefix=app.database`. UNSAID: Spring Boot's relaxed binding applies to `@ConfigurationProperties` and `spring.datasource.*` style config — it does NOT apply to `@Value` placeholder resolution. `@Value("${SOME_ENV_VAR}")` only works if the environment variable is accessed as a property by the exact name. `spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding=true` is unrelated but often confused with this issue. To debug: `ConfigurableEnvironment.getProperty("app.database.url")` in `@PostConstruct` to verify property resolution. `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` resolves `@Value` placeholders.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Entity` with bidirectional relationship infinite recursion in JSON serialization. Bidirectional JPA relationships: `User` has `@OneToMany List<Order> orders`, `Order` has `@ManyToOne User user`. Jackson serializes `User` → serializes each `Order` → serializes `Order.user` → serializes `User.orders` → infinite recursion → `StackOverflowError`. Fix: (1) `@JsonManagedReference` on parent (`orders`), `@JsonBackReference` on child (`user`) — back reference is not serialized; (2) `@JsonIgnore` on one side; (3) use DTO projection instead of exposing entities directly in REST API. UNSAID: the root cause of this problem is exposing JPA entities directly in REST controllers — a design anti-pattern. JPA entities are persistence models; REST responses should use separate DTO classes. DTOs give you explicit control over what is serialized, decouple API shape from database schema, and prevent accidentally exposing sensitive fields. `@JsonManagedReference`/`@JsonBackReference` is a workaround — the correct fix is DTOs. `ModelMapper` or `MapStruct` for entity-to-DTO mapping. `MapStruct` is preferred (compile-time, no reflection, fast). `@JsonIgnoreProperties("orders")` on a specific context with Jackson Mixins for library classes you can't annotate.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Scheduled` fixedRate tasks accumulating backlog from slow execution. `@Scheduled(fixedRate=5000)` runs every 5 seconds from the start of the previous execution — if the task takes 6 seconds, the next execution starts immediately when the previous finishes (0 second delay), and a third execution is queued. With multiple slow tasks, the shared `ThreadPoolTaskScheduler` (default pool size=1) queues tasks, creating a growing backlog. Symptoms: tasks run back-to-back without gaps, increasing delay between scheduled time and actual execution, task queue growing without bound. Fix: (1) use `fixedDelay` instead of `fixedRate` for tasks where overlap is the problem; (2) increase scheduler thread pool size: `spring.task.scheduling.pool.size=5`; (3) ensure tasks complete within their rate period; (4) add `@Async` to run tasks in a separate pool (careful: removes rate-limiting protection). UNSAID: `fixedRate` is appropriate when you need exactly N executions per time period regardless of execution time (e.g., heartbeat). `fixedDelay` is appropriate when you want N seconds of rest between executions. For tasks that must not overlap: `fixedDelay` + synchronous execution is the safest pattern. Monitor task execution time via `@Timed` to detect when tasks exceed their schedule.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Cacheable` caching exceptions and returning stale data on error. `@Cacheable` by default does NOT cache exceptions — if the method throws, the cache is not populated and the next call also calls the method. However, if the method returns `null`, `@Cacheable` does NOT cache the null value by default — but this can be changed with `unless="#result == null"`. A subtler failure: using a cached method that returns stale data because the `@CacheEvict` is on the wrong method, missing the `allEntries=true` flag for broad invalidation, or using a different cache name. Fix: verify cache eviction covers all mutation paths. Use `@CacheEvict(allEntries=true)` for simple full cache invalidation on any write. UNSAID: `@Cacheable(condition="#id > 0")` — conditional caching based on input. `@Cacheable(unless="#result?.active == false")` — conditional caching based on result. `spring.cache.redis.time-to-live=30m` sets global TTL. Without TTL, cache entries live indefinitely — memory leak risk. Test cache behavior: use `CacheManager.getCache(name).get(key)` to verify cache contents in tests. `@EnableCaching(proxyTargetClass=true)` required when caching methods on classes without interfaces.

FAILURE: Spring Boot JPA `merge()` vs `persist()` confusion causing duplicate inserts. `EntityManager.persist(entity)` — for new entities (no ID). `EntityManager.merge(entity)` — for detached entities with an ID. Common mistake: calling `persist()` on an entity with an ID set — if the ID doesn't exist in the database, the entity is inserted. If the ID does exist, `EntityIdAlreadyExistsException` or `PersistenceException` is thrown (database constraint violation). Spring Data JPA `save()`: calls `persist()` if the entity is new (`@Transient` isNew() or null ID), calls `merge()` if the entity has an ID. If you manually set an ID and call `save()`, Spring Data calls `merge()`, which selects then inserts (SELECT + INSERT — 2 queries instead of 1). UNSAID: to avoid the SELECT on insert for entities with manually assigned IDs: implement `Persistable<ID>` interface and override `isNew()` to return `true` for new entities: `@Transient private boolean isNew = true; @PostLoad @PostPersist void markNotNew() { isNew = false; }`. UUID primary keys with client-generated IDs benefit from this optimization. Spring Data JPA's default new-entity detection via null ID doesn't work when IDs are assigned by the application rather than the database.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `JpaRepository.findById()` returning stale data within the same transaction due to first-level cache. Within a single `@Transactional` method, calling `findById(id)` twice returns the same cached entity from Hibernate's first-level (session) cache — even if another thread modified the database between the two calls. `entityManager.refresh(entity)` forces a reload from the database. `entityManager.clear()` clears the entire first-level cache. UNSAID: the first-level cache is session-scoped (transaction-scoped in Spring) — this is usually the correct behavior (consistency within a transaction). Problems arise when: (1) you intentionally need to see external changes mid-transaction (use `SERIALIZABLE` isolation or `refresh()`); (2) two entities of the same type and ID are loaded in the same session via different means (JOIN FETCH vs findById) — Hibernate returns the cached instance, which may be partially populated. `@Modifying(clearAutomatically=true)` on `@Query` UPDATE/DELETE statements clears the first-level cache after bulk operations — prevents the cache from returning stale pre-update entities. `@Modifying(flushAutomatically=true)` flushes pending changes before the bulk operation.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Transactional` on `private` methods with CGLIB proxying — silently ignored. `@Transactional` on a `private` method has no effect — Spring's CGLIB proxy can only intercept `public` and `protected` methods (and `package-private` in some configurations). The method executes without a transaction, and no error is thrown. Similarly, `@Cacheable`, `@Async`, and `@Retry` on private methods are silently ignored. Fix: make the method `public`, or extract the transactional logic to a separate `@Service` bean. UNSAID: this is a Spring proxy limitation — AspectJ weaving (compile-time or load-time) would intercept private methods. For code that genuinely needs private transactional methods: use programmatic transactions with `TransactionTemplate`. `TransactionTemplate.execute(status -> { /* transactional code */ return result; })`. `@Transactional` on `final` methods: CGLIB cannot override final methods — the annotation is silently ignored. `@SpringBootApplication` does not enable AspectJ by default — only Spring AOP (proxy-based). Enable compile-time AspectJ weaving with `aspectj-maven-plugin` if private method interception is required.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@EnableWebMvc` annotation disabling Spring Boot's MVC auto-configuration. Adding `@EnableWebMvc` to a `@Configuration` class in a Spring Boot application disables Spring Boot's auto-configured MVC — you lose auto-configured `ViewResolvers`, `MessageConverters` (including Jackson JSON conversion), static resource handling, and error pages. Everything must be manually configured. Symptoms: `@RequestBody` stops working (no Jackson converter), `/static/**` stops serving files, default error handling breaks. Fix: remove `@EnableWebMvc` and use `WebMvcConfigurer` interface to customize without disabling auto-config: `@Configuration public class MvcConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer { ... }`. UNSAID: this is a common mistake when developers copy Spring MVC configuration from non-Boot tutorials into a Spring Boot project. `@EnableWebMvc` is for plain Spring MVC projects without Spring Boot. Spring Boot's auto-configuration covers everything `@EnableWebMvc` enables, plus sensible defaults. Similarly: `@EnableTransactionManagement` in Spring Boot is unnecessary — auto-configured by `@SpringBootApplication`. `@EnableJpaRepositories` is also auto-configured unless you have custom requirements (multiple entity managers).

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@Scheduled` with `cron="0 0 * * * *"` executing at wrong times due to server timezone. Cron expressions in Spring use the JVM's default timezone unless explicitly specified. A server with timezone set to `Asia/Shanghai` (UTC+8) runs `0 0 * * * *` (midnight) at 16:00 UTC — unexpected in a globally deployed system. Fix: always specify timezone: `@Scheduled(cron="0 0 * * * *", zone="UTC")`. UNSAID: `TimeZone.getDefault()` in Java returns the JVM's timezone, which may differ from the OS timezone or the Docker container's timezone (which defaults to UTC). In Kubernetes: containers default to UTC unless `TZ` environment variable is set. Cron syntax in Spring: seconds are the first field (unlike Unix cron): `second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`. `@Scheduled(cron="0 0 0 * * *")` — midnight every day. Spring's cron also supports `L` (last), `#` (nth weekday), `?` (no specific value for day fields). For distributed environments: combine with ShedLock to prevent double execution across instances, and always log the next scheduled execution time for debugging.

FAILURE: Spring Boot property injection order causing `@Value` to use stale values after context refresh. `@Value` fields are injected once at bean creation — they do not auto-update when properties change (e.g., after `POST /actuator/refresh` with Spring Cloud Config). Beans with `@Value` fields serve the old value after a config refresh until they're recreated. Fix: annotate beans with `@RefreshScope` to recreate them on refresh. Or use `Environment.getProperty()` for dynamic property access — reads the current value every time. UNSAID: `@RefreshScope` has a cost — the bean is recreated on every refresh event, which can interrupt in-flight requests if the bean is a stateful service. Use `@RefreshScope` on beans that are safe to recreate (stateless services, configuration holders). Avoid `@RefreshScope` on beans holding connection pools or locks. For feature flags that must update without restart: use a dedicated feature flag service (`Unleash`, `LaunchDarkly`) rather than Spring Config refresh. `Environment.getProperty()` is thread-safe for reading current values — the simplest pattern for dynamic configuration without the complexity of `@RefreshScope`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot application context failing to start due to missing `@Configuration` dependencies in test slices. `@WebMvcTest` and `@DataJpaTest` only load specific layers. If your controller imports a `@Configuration` class that references a `@Service` or `@Component` not in the slice, the context fails with `NoSuchBeanDefinitionException`. Fix: use `@MockBean` for missing service dependencies, or `@Import(MissingConfig.class)` to import specific `@Configuration` classes. UNSAID: `@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)` limits scanning to `UserController` and related beans — `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Component` outside the controller package are not scanned. `@ControllerAdvice` classes ARE included by `@WebMvcTest` (important for testing error handling). `@SecurityConfig` may need explicit import in `@WebMvcTest` if it configures security beans. For complex `@Configuration` with infrastructure beans: use `@TestConfiguration` to provide test-specific bean definitions that override production beans. A common pattern: a `TestSecurityConfig.class` with `permitAll()` for all endpoints, imported in tests that don't test security. `@AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters=false)` as a simpler alternative to configuring security in tests.

FAILURE: Spring Boot JPA bulk delete/update not triggering `@PreRemove`/`@PostUpdate` entity lifecycle callbacks. JPQL bulk operations (`@Modifying @Query("DELETE FROM User u WHERE u.active = false")`) directly issue SQL DML — they bypass the persistence context and do not trigger JPA lifecycle callbacks (`@PreRemove`, `@PostRemove`, `@PreUpdate`, `@PostUpdate`). If your audit logging, cache eviction, or domain event publishing relies on `@PreRemove`, it won't fire for bulk deletes. Fix: (1) use `findAll()` + `deleteAll(entities)` for small sets (triggers callbacks but N+1 deletes); (2) publish events manually before bulk delete; (3) use database triggers for audit needs. UNSAID: bulk operations also do not update the first-level cache — subsequent `findById()` in the same transaction may return stale (or still-present deleted) entities. Add `@Modifying(clearAutomatically=true)` to clear the cache after bulk operations. For soft delete with bulk operations: `@Modifying @Query("UPDATE User u SET u.deletedAt = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP WHERE u.active = false")` — still bypasses callbacks. Hibernate's `@SQLDelete` override is not called for bulk JPQL operations — only for `entityManager.remove(entity)`.

FAILURE: Spring Boot `@RequestBody` with Jackson deserialization allowing mass assignment (over-posting). If your `User` entity or DTO has fields like `role=ADMIN` or `isAdmin=true`, and you deserialize the incoming JSON directly into the User/DTO, an attacker can POST `{"email":"user@example.com","role":"ADMIN"}` to set their role to admin. Jackson deserializes all fields present in JSON by default. Fix: (1) use a dedicated `CreateUserRequest` DTO with only the allowed input fields, never the entity directly; (2) `@JsonIgnore` on sensitive fields; (3) Jackson's `@JsonProperty(access=READ_ONLY)` for output-only fields; (4) Spring Security's `@PreAuthorize` to enforce role invariants. UNSAID: this is OWASP A08:2021 (Software and Data Integrity Failures). The fundamental fix is the DTO pattern — never expose JPA entities as request bodies. A separate request DTO with only client-settable fields, a separate response DTO with client-visible fields. `@JsonIgnoreProperties` on DTO for strict unknown field rejection. Regular security review: audit all `@RequestBody` parameters to ensure no sensitive fields are accepted.

NEVER use Spring Boot's default `application.properties` for secrets in containerized deployments. The failure mode: developers put database passwords, JWT secrets, and API keys in `application.properties`, commit them to version control, and then wonder why environment-specific overrides don't apply correctly in Kubernetes. Spring Boot property precedence (highest to lowest): command-line args → SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON env var → OS environment variables → `application-{profile}.properties` → `application.properties`. The correct pattern in containers: all secrets come from environment variables or mounted Kubernetes Secrets as files, with Spring Boot reading them via `spring.datasource.password=${DB_PASSWORD}` placeholder syntax. `application.properties` should contain only non-secret defaults. Using Spring Cloud Config Server or HashiCorp Vault with Spring Cloud Vault for secrets rotation is the next level: secrets are injected at startup and can be refreshed without redeployment via `@RefreshScope`.

Spring Security's CSRF protection is enabled by default for all state-changing HTTP methods (POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) in `HttpSecurity`. The failure mode: REST APIs used only by JavaScript frontends disable CSRF with `http.csrf().disable()` without understanding the implication. For truly stateless REST APIs (using JWT Bearer tokens, no cookies for auth), disabling CSRF is correct because CSRF attacks require cookies to carry auth state. But mixed auth APIs — those that use `HttpOnly` session cookies for some routes and Bearer tokens for others — are vulnerable if CSRF is disabled globally. The correct approach: `http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.ignoringRequestMatchers('/api/public/**')).sessionManagement(session -> session.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))` for mixed APIs, or full CSRF disable only when authentication is 100% cookie-free. Spring Security 6's `CsrfTokenRequestAttributeHandler` with `XorCsrfTokenRequestAttributeHandler` is the modern CSRF approach for SPAs that need cookie-based auth.

Spring Boot Actuator exposes sensitive endpoints (`/actuator/env`, `/actuator/heapdump`, `/actuator/threaddump`, `/actuator/shutdown`) that must be secured in production. The failure mode: teams enable all actuator endpoints for debugging convenience and forget to restrict access before production deploy. `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*` combined with a public-facing port exposes full environment variables (including secrets resolved from env vars), heap dumps containing user data, and shutdown capability to unauthenticated callers. Correct configuration: run actuator on a separate management port (`management.server.port=8081`) that is not exposed through external load balancers; restrict via `@Bean SecurityFilterChain managementSecurity()` requiring `ROLE_ACTUATOR`; or limit exposed endpoints to only what monitoring systems need (`management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,metrics,prometheus`). The `/actuator/env` endpoint deserves special attention — it masks values containing 'password', 'secret', 'key', 'token', but only those exact substrings; custom property names that contain secrets but not those keywords are shown in plaintext.

Spring Boot's `ObjectMapper` auto-configured by `JacksonAutoConfiguration` is shared across the entire application. Mutating this shared ObjectMapper (e.g., `objectMapper.configure(FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES, false)`) in one service affects serialization behavior for all Spring MVC REST endpoints and all other uses of ObjectMapper throughout the application. The correct patterns: (1) declare a `@Bean ObjectMapper customObjectMapper()` to replace the auto-configured one entirely, with all desired settings; (2) for per-use-case customization, create separate `ObjectMapper` instances (not beans) using `objectMapper.copy()` which creates a configured copy; (3) use `@JsonComponent` to register custom serializers/deserializers via Spring without mutating the global mapper. The most common side effect of global ObjectMapper mutation: inadvertently disabling `SerializationFeature.WRITE_DATES_AS_TIMESTAMPS` causes all date fields in REST responses to change format, breaking API clients. Use `spring.jackson.*` properties instead of programmatic configuration when possible.

Spring Boot's HikariCP connection pool default `maximumPoolSize=10` is dangerously low for production microservices under concurrent load. At 10 connections, 11 simultaneous requests requiring DB access will queue; at high traffic, connection acquisition timeout (default 30s) exceptions flood logs. The failure is subtle because load testing with 10 concurrent users passes fine, but production load of 100 concurrent users causes cascading pool exhaustion. Calculation: `pool size = thread_pool_size * (Cm - 1) + 1` where Cm is the count of concurrent connections needed per transaction (usually 1 for simple queries, 2 for operations that hold a connection while calling another service). For a service with 200 Tomcat threads and single-connection transactions, theoretical max is 200 connections — but this defeats the purpose of pooling. Real recommendation: profile actual connection hold time vs request rate, then size pool to `(peak_concurrent_requests) * (average_connection_hold_time_ms / average_request_time_ms)`. Also: `minimumIdle=maximumPoolSize` eliminates idle connection churn under variable load.

Spring Data JPA's `save()` method has counterintuitive behavior with managed entities: for a detached entity (one loaded in a previous transaction), `save()` calls `entityManager.merge()`, which creates a new managed copy of the entity. Modifications to the original detached entity after `save()` are NOT persisted — only modifications to the returned merged entity are. Pattern: `User saved = userRepository.save(detachedUser); // Use 'saved' not 'detachedUser' from here`. For a new entity (`id == null`), `save()` calls `persist()` and returns the same instance (now managed). The `saveAll()` and `save()` distinction: `saveAll(List)` batches persistence operations, but only if `spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size` is set (default is unbuffered). Without batch_size, `saveAll(1000 entities)` issues 1000 individual INSERT statements. Configure: `hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50`, `hibernate.order_inserts=true`, `hibernate.order_updates=true`, `hibernate.jdbc.batch_versioned_data=true`. Verify batching is active via `p6spy` or Hibernate statistics showing batch insert count.

Spring Boot's `RestTemplate` is deprecated in favor of `WebClient` for new applications (still supported, not removed). The failure mode with RestTemplate: it's synchronous and blocks the calling thread for the entire HTTP round-trip. In a microservice with 200 Tomcat threads that each make 3 downstream RestTemplate calls, concurrent capacity is effectively 200/3 ≈ 66 simultaneous user requests — thread pool exhaustion is the ceiling. Using `WebClient` in reactive mode (`WebClient.create().get().uri(url).retrieve().bodyToMono(Response.class)`) with WebFlux allows thousands of concurrent requests with far fewer threads via non-blocking I/O. For existing Spring MVC (servlet) applications, `WebClient` can still be used but must `.block()` to get synchronous results — this preserves non-blocking I/O at the network level while remaining callable from synchronous code. The `RestClient` (Spring 6.1+) is the new synchronous API replacing RestTemplate — same programming model, built on `WebClient` internals, no reactive programming model required.

Spring Security's method-level `@PreAuthorize('hasRole(ADMIN)')` annotation requires `@EnableMethodSecurity` (Spring Security 6) or `@EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled=true)` (Spring Security 5). The failure mode: adding `@PreAuthorize` to service methods without `@EnableMethodSecurity` results in silent authorization bypass — the annotation is present in code but has no effect, all callers succeed regardless of role. The bug is discovered only in security testing or when a penetration tester calls the endpoint. Additionally, `@PreAuthorize` on `@Bean` methods in `@Configuration` classes never fires because Spring Security proxies are applied at the bean creation level, not on factory methods. `hasRole('ADMIN')` automatically prepends 'ROLE_'; `hasAuthority('ADMIN')` does not — mismatching these with how roles are stored in `GrantedAuthority` causes all authorization checks to fail silently (returns false, not an error). Verify method security is active by writing a test that expects `AccessDeniedException` from a call with insufficient role.

Spring Boot's embedded Tomcat default thread pool `server.tomcat.threads.max=200` can be too high or too low depending on workload. The failure: I/O-bound services (making external HTTP calls, doing DB queries) set `threads.max=200` but each thread blocks on I/O, consuming 1MB+ stack memory — 200 threads × 1MB = 200MB stack alone, plus heap for request data. Under heavy load, thread context switching overhead exceeds useful work. Conversely, CPU-bound services set `threads.max=200` on an 8-core server — only 8 threads can run simultaneously; 192 threads compete for CPU with context switching overhead. Configuration formula: for I/O-bound (blocking): `max_threads = (request_rate * avg_latency_ms) / 1000`; for CPU-bound: `max_threads ≈ core_count * 1-2`. Also: `server.tomcat.accept-count=100` (queue size when all threads busy); requests beyond queue are rejected with connection refused. Spring Boot 3 + Virtual Threads (Java 21): `spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true` enables Project Loom virtual threads for Tomcat — eliminates the thread pool sizing problem entirely, as each request gets its own lightweight virtual thread.

Spring Boot `@ControllerAdvice @ExceptionHandler` error responses leak internal implementation details when not carefully designed. The failure mode: the default Spring `BasicErrorController` includes the exception `message`, `trace` (stack trace), and `path` in error responses when `server.error.include-message=always` and `server.error.include-stacktrace=always` — the defaults changed to `never` in Spring Boot 2.3 after widespread information disclosure incidents. Even with defaults, custom `@ExceptionHandler` methods that return `ResponseEntity<Map<String, Object>> handleException(Exception ex) { return ResponseEntity.status(500).body(Map.of('error', ex.getMessage())); }` expose `ex.getMessage()` which often contains SQL, file paths, or internal class names. Correct pattern: map all exceptions to user-safe messages in the handler; log full exception server-side with correlation ID; return only `{ 'errorCode': 'INTERNAL_ERROR', 'correlationId': uuid }` to the client. `ProblemDetail` (RFC 7807, Spring 6+) provides a standard error format but still requires care about what goes in `detail` and `properties`.

Spring Boot test slices (`@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest`, `@JsonTest`) load partial application contexts to speed up tests. The failure mode: `@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)` loads only the web layer (controllers, filters, converters) but not `@Service` beans — `@Service UserService` must be `@MockBean`-ed in the test class. Forgetting to mock a service bean causes `NoSuchBeanDefinitionException` at test startup, which is a clear error. The subtle failure: `@WebMvcTest` DOES include `@ControllerAdvice` beans and Spring Security auto-configuration. If Spring Security is on the classpath, `@WebMvcTest` applies security — all endpoints require authentication by default, causing 401s on all test requests. Fix: `@WebMvcTest` + `@WithMockUser(roles='USER')` for authenticated requests, or `http.authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth.anyRequest().permitAll())` for security-disabled test configuration annotated with `@TestConfiguration`. Mixed test: `@SpringBootTest` + `@AutoConfigureMockMvc` for full context with MockMvc capability — slower but no mocking needed for the web-service integration.

Spring Boot's `@Scheduled(cron='0 0 * * * *')` (every hour) fails silently in production when the application is shut down and restarted mid-window. The scheduled task does not run 'as soon as possible after missed execution' — it simply waits for the next scheduled time. For critical hourly batch jobs that must run exactly once per hour even across restarts, `@Scheduled` alone is insufficient. The failure mode: a service restarts at 12:01 AM after a crash; the midnight job never runs; downstream processes have no data for that hour; the gap is discovered 24 hours later. Correct approach: Quartz Scheduler with `@PersistJobDataAfterExecution` + `@DisallowConcurrentExecution` stores job execution state in a DB table; missed jobs trigger immediately on next startup. Simpler alternative: track last successful execution timestamp in the database; on startup, check if the last run is more than one interval ago and run immediately if so. Also: `@Scheduled` with fixed-rate semantics can overlap (concurrent execution) if execution time > rate — use `@Scheduled(fixedDelay)` for sequential or `@SchedulingConfigurer` with task locking.

Spring Boot's `@Entity` classes must have a public or protected no-argument constructor (JPA specification requirement). With Lombok's `@Data` or `@AllArgsConstructor`, the no-arg constructor is removed unless `@NoArgsConstructor` is also added. The failure: `Caused by: org.hibernate.InstantiationException: No default constructor for entity` at startup. More subtle: `@Entity` with `@Builder` pattern — `@Builder` generates only an all-args constructor; adding `@NoArgsConstructor` removes `@Builder`'s constructor access unless `@AllArgsConstructor` is also present. Correct combination for JPA entities with builder: `@Entity @Getter @Setter @NoArgsConstructor @AllArgsConstructor @Builder`. Avoid `@Data` on JPA entities: it generates `equals()` and `hashCode()` based on all fields, which breaks JPA entity equality semantics (entities should be equal based on primary key, not all field values). Use `@EqualsAndHashCode(onlyExplicitlyIncluded=true) @EqualsAndHashCode.Include` on the ID field. JPA entities should never be data classes; they are managed objects with identity semantics, not value objects.

viewModelScope only cancels work it owns. The hidden failure is launching from a ViewModel into longer-lived scopes, holding UI references in collectors, or collecting hot streams forever without lifecycle-aware restart. Teams say they used viewModelScope when the real bug is that ownership escaped the ViewModel boundary.

Fragments fail less because their API is bad and more because they expose two overlapping lifecycles: fragment and view. The hidden landmine is keeping references, observers, or adapters alive past onDestroyView. Most random crashes are really stale view work running in a still-live fragment object.

Gradle problems on Android are usually boundary problems disguised as build errors. Plugin order, version catalogs, AGP compatibility, and convention drift compound slowly until upgrades stall. The hidden rule is to centralize build logic early; duplicated module config feels fast until the first ecosystem-wide change lands.

StateFlow.collect in a Composable will re-collect from the beginning if the composable leaves and re-enters the composition, such as during navigation or configuration changes. The hidden failure is using collectAsState() on a cold or replay-1 StateFlow that represents a loading state—navigating back and returning triggers a reload that was not intended. The correct pattern is to hoist state collection into the ViewModel and expose immutable snapshot state through a StateFlow with a sensible initial value, so the UI reconnects to existing state rather than restarting it.

Room's DAO @Transaction annotation ensures that multiple queries inside the annotated method run in a single transaction, but it does not protect against concurrent access from multiple coroutines or threads. The hidden failure is assuming @Transaction prevents race conditions: two coroutines can both read-then-write in their own @Transaction calls and interleave. For true serialization, use a Mutex at the application layer or design writes to be idempotent. @Transaction prevents partial reads within a single call, not cross-call concurrency.

Android's Doze mode and App Standby buckets aggressively defer background work for apps that are not in the foreground. AlarmManager exact alarms require SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM permission since Android 12, and WorkManager jobs scheduled with constraints are batched and delayed. The hidden failure is shipping background sync that works reliably on a developer device under active use and breaks for users whose phones enter Doze overnight. The discipline is to test with adb shell dumpsys deviceidle force-idle and verify that WorkManager expedited requests are used when freshness guarantees matter.

Navigation Compose's NavController must not be passed directly to Composables below the top level. The hidden failure is that passing NavController deep into a composable tree creates tight coupling between screens and navigation logic, making the component hard to preview and test in isolation. The correct pattern is to pass navigation callbacks (lambdas) to composables and keep NavController at the navigation host level. This also makes deep-link handling and back stack manipulation easier to reason about as the app grows.

ProGuard and R8 minification silently break code that relies on reflection, including Gson deserialization, Retrofit interface introspection, and custom annotation processing at runtime. The hidden failure is that minified builds work in debug because minification is disabled by default in debug builds. The discipline is to run at least one release build in CI with minification enabled and keep proguard-rules.pro entries synchronized with any new reflective library addition. Missing keep rules produce NoSuchMethodException or JsonParseException that never appear in debug runs.

LangChain's package split (langchain, langchain-core, langchain-community, langchain-openai, etc.) is the most common source of ImportError and version conflict in production. The UNSAID trap: langchain-core is the stable base with no third-party dependencies; langchain is a thin meta-package; langchain-community holds integrations with looser pinning. Installing just 'langchain' pulls in langchain-core automatically, but installing only 'langchain-openai' does NOT install langchain — causing missing chain utilities. Pinning langchain and langchain-openai independently without checking their shared langchain-core constraint causes silent version mismatches.

The v0.1→v0.2→v0.3 migration path combined with Pydantic v2 is the single largest breakage source in LangChain production codebases. LangChain v0.2 dropped many legacy chain classes; v0.3 dropped Python 3.8 and fully migrated internal models to Pydantic v2. The hidden trap: custom classes subclassing BaseModel or BaseTool that worked with Pydantic v1 break silently at runtime under v0.3 because Pydantic v2 enforces stricter type coercion and changes field validators from @validator to @field_validator. Many tutorials still show v0.1 syntax, creating a false sense of compatibility when code runs but produces wrong behavior.

LCEL streaming and callback propagation has a non-obvious gotcha: callbacks passed to .invoke() do NOT automatically propagate into nested Runnables created inside RunnableLambda or custom tools. The UNSAID mechanism: callbacks propagate through the chain graph only if the nested call uses the config parameter (RunnableConfig) passed into the lambda. Code that calls another chain directly inside a lambda (e.g., inner_chain.invoke(input)) instead of inner_chain.invoke(input, config) silently drops tracing and token-level streaming for all downstream steps, making LangSmith traces appear to have missing spans.

ConversationBufferMemory and all classes in langchain.memory are deprecated as of v0.3 and will be removed. The UNSAID problem is that they were stateful objects that mixed concerns: they stored messages AND managed the prompt injection, making them impossible to compose cleanly with LCEL. The replacement pattern is explicit message history management using RunnableWithMessageHistory or direct ChatMessageHistory objects passed through chain state. Teams migrating from ConversationBufferMemory often reproduce the same tight coupling in their replacement code because they don't understand that the deprecation was architectural, not cosmetic.

Output parsers (PydanticOutputParser, StructuredOutputParser, JsonOutputParser) fail brittlely because they rely on the LLM following format instructions in the prompt — there is no retry or schema enforcement at the protocol level. The UNSAID fix that most teams miss: use .with_structured_output() on the chat model instead of a separate output parser. This method uses the provider's native function-calling or JSON-mode API to enforce structure at the model API level, making it 10x more reliable than prompt-based parsing. Output parsers remain useful only for models that don't support native structured output.

LangSmith tracing is enabled by setting LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true and LANGCHAIN_API_KEY in environment variables, but there is no runtime error if tracing silently fails due to network issues, wrong API key, or quota exceeded — the chain still executes and returns results. Teams that rely on LangSmith for debugging discover missing traces only during incident postmortems. The correct production pattern is to check LangSmith connectivity as a startup health check, log the trace URL per request via the run_id returned by chain.invoke() when tracing is active, and configure a dead-letter alert if trace ingestion failure rate exceeds a threshold in LangSmith's dashboards.

LangChain's text splitters (RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, TokenTextSplitter) share a non-obvious default: chunk_overlap defaults to 200 characters, not tokens. When using TokenTextSplitter, overlap is measured in tokens; when using CharacterTextSplitter, overlap is measured in characters. Mixing these splitters in a pipeline without explicit overlap specification causes inconsistent chunk boundary behavior. A second trap: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter uses character count for chunk_size by default, but most downstream vector stores and LLMs care about token count. Setting chunk_size=1000 with the character splitter gives wildly variable token counts per chunk depending on content — code (dense tokens) vs prose (sparse tokens) — making token-budget planning unreliable. Always use TokenTextSplitter with an explicit model-matched encoding when token budget matters.

LangChain's multi-modal chains for vision-language models (GPT-4o, Claude 3, Gemini) require images to be passed as HumanMessage content with a specific list format: a list of dicts with 'type': 'image_url' entries alongside 'type': 'text' entries. The failure trap: passing images as base64 strings directly in the prompt template string (not as typed content blocks) causes every tested provider to silently ignore the image and respond to text only — no error is raised. A second divergence: OpenAI expects `{'type': 'image_url', 'image_url': {'url': 'data:image/jpeg;base64,...'}}` while Anthropic expects `{'type': 'image', 'source': {'type': 'base64', 'media_type': 'image/jpeg', 'data': '...'}}`. LangChain's ChatOpenAI and ChatAnthropic accept a normalized format and handle the conversion, but only when using the `content` list format — mixing providers while using raw dict construction bypasses this normalization and silently sends malformed payloads.

LangChain's LangGraph deployment via LangGraph Platform (LangGraph Cloud or self-hosted LangGraph Server) introduces a persistence layer using Postgres as a checkpointer. The UNSAID operational trap: LangGraph's checkpointing serializes the entire state graph object to JSON on every node transition, storing it in the `checkpoints` table. For graphs with large state (e.g., retrieved documents, conversation history, tool outputs accumulated across many steps), checkpoint blob sizes grow to hundreds of kilobytes per turn, causing two hidden problems: (1) Postgres storage grows unboundedly without a checkpoint pruning strategy; (2) Deserializing multi-MB state objects on every node entry becomes a latency bottleneck at scale. The mitigation is to use state compression (exclude large intermediate values from the persisted schema using Annotated types with `operator.add` reducers) and configure TTL-based checkpoint expiry rather than keeping full history indefinitely.

LangChain's ReAct agent pattern with tools fails silently when tool names or descriptions contain characters that confuse the LLM's action parsing. The JSON format used for tool calls in older LangChain agent types (not OpenAI-function-calling-based) is generated by the LLM itself, meaning special characters in tool names (hyphens, spaces, colons) can cause malformed JSON that the agent executor cannot parse, resulting in an infinite 'Invalid tool call' loop rather than a clear error. The safe pattern: always use snake_case tool names (search_web not search-web, not 'Search Web'), keep tool names under 30 characters, and avoid any punctuation. OpenAI function-calling based agents (create_openai_tools_agent) are immune to this because the tool selection uses structured JSON mode at the API level, not free-text generation.

LangChain's async methods (ainvoke, astream, abatch) have a non-obvious behavior: calling them from a synchronous context (a non-async function or Jupyter notebook without nest_asyncio) raises a RuntimeError about event loop policy. The UNSAID root cause: Python prohibits starting a new event loop if one is already running, and LangChain's async methods use asyncio.run() internally only in the sync wrapper paths. In Jupyter notebooks, there is always a running event loop (provided by ipykernel), so asyncio.run() fails. The correct patterns are: (1) in notebooks, use nest_asyncio.apply() once at the start; (2) in production async contexts (FastAPI, asyncio application), always call ainvoke/astream directly without the sync wrapper; (3) in scripts without an existing event loop, use asyncio.run(chain.ainvoke(input)). Mixing sync and async LangChain calls in the same FastAPI endpoint — calling chain.invoke() inside an async def view — blocks the event loop and defeats FastAPI's concurrency entirely.

LangChain's create_retrieval_chain and create_stuff_documents_chain are convenience constructors that produce a hard-to-customize LCEL chain. The UNSAID failure trap: these constructors use hardcoded prompt templates internally that may not match your use case. Specifically, create_stuff_documents_chain uses a default system prompt that tells the model to answer only from the provided context — this causes the model to refuse to answer questions about general knowledge even when a hybrid (context + parametric knowledge) response would be correct. Teams that use these constructors and then try to customize behavior by patching the returned chain's internals end up in a maintenance nightmare. The production pattern is to build retrieval chains directly from LCEL primitives (RunnablePassthrough, itemgetter, prompt template, model, parser) — more verbose but fully transparent and customizable without hidden defaults.

LangChain custom tools created with @tool decorator lose their docstring-generated schema when the function has *args or **kwargs in its signature. The UNSAID root cause: LangChain's schema generation uses Python's inspect.signature() to extract parameter names and type annotations, and *args/**kwargs are treated as untyped variadic inputs with no JSON Schema equivalent, causing the schema to be generated incorrectly or to be missing required fields. A second failure mode: @tool on a function with no type annotations generates a schema with all parameters typed as 'string', which silently converts integers and booleans to strings when the LLM calls the tool with correct types. Always annotate all tool function parameters with explicit Python types and use only simple types (str, int, float, bool, list, dict) or Pydantic models as parameter types — complex Union types and Optional[X] have inconsistent schema generation across provider adapters.

LangChain's EnsembleRetriever combines multiple retrievers using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) to merge ranked result lists. The UNSAID failure trap: EnsembleRetriever's default weights=[0.5, 0.5] assumes both retrievers have equal quality, which is rarely true in production. A BM25 retriever may be authoritative for exact keyword matches but poor for semantic queries; assigning it equal weight dilutes the better vector retriever's results. Furthermore, RRF treats position in the ranked list, not similarity score — two retrievers that both return the same document at different ranks will score it differently than expected. The production pattern: tune retriever weights using held-out evaluation queries from your actual document corpus; typically vector retriever weights of 0.7-0.8 and BM25 weights of 0.2-0.3 perform best on general knowledge bases. Never deploy a hybrid retriever without evaluating it against a baseline on domain-specific queries.

LangChain's document metadata handling has a production pitfall: metadata values must be serializable to the target vector store's supported types. Chroma supports str, int, float, bool; Pinecone supports the same plus lists of strings; FAISS stores metadata as Python dicts without type validation. Passing datetime objects, nested dicts, or lists of ints as metadata works in FAISS (stored as Python objects) but silently fails on Chroma or Pinecone, which either raise a TypeError or silently drop the field. The safe production pattern: serialize all metadata to primitive types before ingestion, convert datetime to ISO strings, flatten nested dicts, and limit list metadata to string-typed values. Document loaders (PDFLoader, GitLoader) often produce metadata with non-primitive types — always validate and transform metadata before calling vectorstore.add_documents().

LangChain's Pydantic integration for tool schemas breaks in a specific way with Pydantic v2 and discriminated unions. When a tool's input schema uses a Union type with a discriminator field (e.g., Literal['type_a'] | Literal['type_b'] as a discriminator), the generated JSON Schema includes a 'discriminator' keyword that is not supported by OpenAI's function calling API (which only supports a subset of JSON Schema). The result is a 400 error from the OpenAI API with a cryptic message about unsupported schema keywords, not a clear error about discriminated unions. The safe pattern: avoid discriminated unions in tool input schemas and instead use separate tools with distinct names for different input shapes. If a single tool must handle multiple input shapes, use Optional fields with None defaults rather than Union discriminators, and validate the combination in the tool's body rather than in the schema.

LangChain's document score threshold in vector store similarity_search_with_relevance_scores() has a provider-dependent scoring range that creates portability bugs. FAISS returns cosine similarity scores in [-1, 1]; Chroma returns L2 distance converted to a pseudo-similarity in [0, 1]; Pinecone returns cosine similarity in [0, 1] for normalized vectors. A score_threshold=0.7 that correctly filters irrelevant results in Chroma may reject all results in FAISS (where similar documents score 0.95+) or pass irrelevant results from Pinecone (where semantic similarity is measured differently). The UNSAID safe pattern: never use a hardcoded score_threshold without first measuring the score distribution on your specific corpus and vector store. Calibrate thresholds by running 50+ representative queries and examining the score distribution at the correct relevance boundary, then set thresholds relative to that distribution rather than using absolute values.

LangChain's LCEL streaming with non-streaming-aware output parsers silently buffers the entire response before parsing, negating streaming latency benefits. JsonOutputParser and PydanticOutputParser are streaming-aware (they can emit partial parsed objects during streaming); StrOutputParser is trivially streaming-aware (passes through tokens directly). But most custom output parsers that subclass BaseCumulativeTransformOutputParser are NOT streaming-aware unless they implement transform() and atransform(). The symptom: chain.stream() appears to work (no error) but the caller receives only one chunk — the complete parsed output — at the end. The diagnostic: check if the output parser has transform()/atransform() implementations; if not, it buffers. The safe pattern for streaming pipelines: always use JsonOutputParser or StrOutputParser as the final step, and do any additional post-processing outside the chain in application code that receives the stream.

LangChain's HuggingFaceEmbeddings wrapper downloads models from the HuggingFace Hub on first use and caches them in ~/.cache/huggingface. In containerized production environments, this creates three failure modes: (1) first-request latency spike of 30-120 seconds while the model downloads; (2) ephemeral container storage means the model is re-downloaded on every container restart; (3) firewall rules in production environments may block outbound connections to huggingface.co. The correct production pattern for self-hosted embeddings: bake the model into the Docker image using `snapshot_download()` in the Dockerfile build step, and set TRANSFORMERS_OFFLINE=1 environment variable to prevent any hub calls at runtime. This reduces startup time to under 5 seconds and eliminates network dependency for inference. Alternatively, use HuggingFaceInferenceAPI wrapper to call a pre-deployed Inference Endpoint, avoiding model distribution in your container entirely.

LangChain's tool_calls attribute on AIMessage contains structured tool invocations, but there is a critical difference between tool_calls (present when the model uses API-native function calling) and additional_kwargs['function_call'] (legacy OpenAI format from before tool_calls). Code that checks additional_kwargs['function_call'] to detect tool use will silently miss tool calls from models using the newer tool_calls format. The unified access pattern for all providers: always check message.tool_calls first; if empty, fall back to additional_kwargs for backward compatibility. A second hidden trap: tool_calls from parallel tool use (multiple tools called simultaneously) returns a list with multiple entries — code that only handles the first element of tool_calls will silently drop all parallel tool calls beyond the first, causing the agent to appear to ignore its own tool selection.

LangChain's provider-specific model name strings are a migration trap: model names are passed as strings and there is no validation at construction time. ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4-turbo') silently accepts 'gpt-4-turb' (typo) without error, and the error only surfaces when the first API call is made, potentially after expensive setup (loading embeddings, establishing DB connections). The UNSAID production pattern: maintain a central model registry as a Python Enum or TypedDict with valid model names, and reference enum values rather than raw strings throughout the codebase. This catches typos at definition time (Python will raise AttributeError on invalid enum access), makes model deprecation changes grep-able (one place to update), and enables cost tracking by model name. A second failure mode: model name strings that worked in LangChain 0.1 (e.g., 'gpt-3.5-turbo-0613') may be deprecated by the provider and return 404 without any LangChain-layer warning — subscribe to provider deprecation notices and treat model names as configuration that requires active maintenance.

LangChain's SQLDatabaseToolkit and create_sql_agent expose a dangerous default: the SQL agent can execute arbitrary SELECT queries and by default has no read-row-count limits. An agent asked 'list all users' on a table with 10 million rows will execute SELECT * FROM users, pull all rows into memory, and either exhaust memory or exceed the context window. The UNSAID production hardening: always set db = SQLDatabase.from_uri(url, include_tables=['allowed_table_1'], sample_rows_in_table_info=3, max_string_length=300) to limit which tables are visible and how much data is sampled for schema inference. Additionally, wrap the database tool with a custom tool that adds LIMIT clauses to all SELECT queries programmatically, rejecting or modifying queries that lack LIMIT. Never expose a SQL agent to an untrusted user without row-count limits, query complexity limits, and an allowlist of accessible tables.

LangChain's serialization (to_json() / loads()) for saving and restoring chain objects is incomplete and fragile. Not all LangChain components are fully serializable — components with closures, class instances, or non-serializable dependencies (like database connections) will raise NotImplementedError or produce incomplete JSON. The UNSAID production implication: teams that try to serialize chains for caching or remote execution discover that the serialized form omits API keys (which must be re-injected on deserialization) and drops custom tool implementations (which are referenced by import path, breaking if the class moves). The correct alternative for chain persistence is to store configuration (a dict of model names, temperature values, retriever settings) and reconstruct the chain from configuration at runtime, rather than serializing the chain object itself. Treat chains as ephemeral runtime objects and configuration as the persistent artifact.

LangChain's tool message handling in multi-turn agent conversations requires that every ToolMessage follows immediately after the AIMessage that requested it in the message list. The OpenAI and Anthropic APIs enforce this ordering — ToolMessage objects that appear without a preceding AIMessage with a matching tool_call_id will be rejected with a 400 error. The UNSAID failure mode: when implementing custom agent loops that insert, reorder, or filter messages, teams accidentally violate this ordering and receive cryptic API errors. The diagnostic pattern: iterate through the message list and verify that every ToolMessage at index i is immediately preceded by an AIMessage at index i-1 containing a tool_calls entry with the matching id. LangChain's built-in AgentExecutor handles this ordering automatically, which is a hidden reason to prefer it over custom agent loops for teams unfamiliar with the message structure invariants.

LangChain's response synthesis for long retrieved contexts (10+ chunks) suffers from the 'lost in the middle' problem: LLMs systematically perform worse when the relevant information is in the middle of a long context compared to the beginning or end. This is a well-documented LLM behavior (Nelson et al., 2023) that LangChain's default document ordering does not address. The UNSAID production mitigation: reorder retrieved documents by placing the most relevant document first, the second most relevant last, and less relevant documents in the middle — this exploits the primacy/recency bias of LLMs and improves answer accuracy by 10-20% on benchmarks without any additional API cost. LangChain provides a LongContextReorder document transformer that implements this reordering strategy. Teams that sort documents by similarity score in descending order (most similar first) are inadvertently front-loading all high-relevance documents and diluting with low-relevance ones at the end — LongContextReorder distributes them more effectively.

LangChain's PydanticV1BaseModel vs PydanticV2BaseModel import confusion: in LangChain v0.3, many internal classes still use Pydantic v1 compatibility shim imported from langchain_core.pydantic_v1. If your custom tool or chain subclasses both a LangChain base class (which internally uses pydantic_v1) and a standard Pydantic v2 model, you get subtle MRO conflicts where validators from one version silently override validators from the other. The UNSAID safe rule: never mix pydantic_v1 imports from langchain_core with direct pydantic v2 imports in the same class hierarchy. For custom tools, subclass langchain_core.tools.BaseTool and use pydantic v2 models exclusively for the args_schema attribute, not for the tool class itself. If you see 'class is not fully defined' or 'validators not called' errors, check for mixed Pydantic version inheritance.

LangChain's AgentExecutor max_iterations default is 15, which creates a hidden cost time-bomb for agents stuck in loops. A poorly-specified tool that always returns 'I need more information' will consume 15 LLM API calls before stopping — at GPT-4o pricing, that's $0.30-$1.50 per failed agent run. The UNSAID production hardening: always set max_iterations to the minimum needed for your task (typically 5-10 for well-defined tasks, never more than 20), and add max_execution_time (wall-clock timeout in seconds) as a second budget constraint. Add a telemetry callback that logs agent iteration count per run so you can detect runaway agents before they exhaust your API budget. The deeper architectural principle: any agent loop without a hard budget is an unbounded cost commitment — treat max_iterations and max_execution_time as mandatory production parameters, not optional tuning knobs.

LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with Python code uses separators=['
class ', '
def ', '

', '
', ' ', ''] to split on logical code boundaries. However, this separator list has a dangerous default behavior: when no separator matches within the chunk_size limit, it falls back to splitting at arbitrary character positions — which can split function names, string literals, or syntax tokens mid-character. This silently produces syntactically invalid code chunks that cause embedding models trained on code to generate poor representations. The UNSAID correct approach for code: use Language.PYTHON (or other language enum) with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language() which uses the tree-sitter parser to split at syntactically valid boundaries (function definitions, class definitions, statement boundaries) rather than regex-based separators. For code RAG, semantic validity of chunks is critical — a partial function without its signature produces meaningless embeddings.

LangChain's Chroma similarity_search() default uses L2 (Euclidean) distance, not cosine similarity, for embedding comparison — this is counterintuitive because most embedding model papers report quality metrics using cosine similarity. L2 distance and cosine distance produce different orderings for unnormalized vectors. OpenAI's text-embedding-3 models return normalized vectors (L2 norm = 1) so cosine and L2 are equivalent for them. Sentence Transformers models may or may not normalize depending on the model and encode() call parameters. The UNSAID production verification: after indexing, run the same query with similarity_search() and similarity_search_with_score() and check if documents that are semantically similar have low L2 distances — if scores appear inconsistent, your embeddings may be unnormalized and you need to switch the Chroma collection to cosine metric at creation time (chromadb.Collection(metadata={'hnsw:space': 'cosine'})). Changing the distance metric after index creation requires re-indexing all documents.

LangChain's memory management in LangGraph uses the MemorySaver checkpointer which serializes state to in-process memory — this means all agent state is lost on process restart and is not shared between multiple worker processes. Production deployments of LangGraph that use MemorySaver behind a load balancer with multiple workers will have users whose conversations are silently split across different workers, losing context on each new worker assignment. The UNSAID production requirement: for any LangGraph agent with persistent conversation history, use PostgresSaver or RedisSaver as the checkpointer, which stores state in an external service accessible to all workers. The migration from MemorySaver to PostgresSaver is a drop-in replacement: checkpointer = PostgresSaver.from_conn_string(DB_URL) with no changes to the graph definition. Always treat in-process checkpointers as development-only tools.

LangChain's document loaders for GitHub (GitLoader) clone the entire repository during initialization, making the first run of a document ingestion pipeline extremely slow for large repos. The UNSAID performance trap: GitLoader clones using GitPython which does a full git clone including all history — a repo with 10,000 commits and 500MB of history will download all of it even if you only want to index current file contents. For large repositories, use GitLoader with a shallow clone workaround (clone_url + clone_dir pre-populated with git clone --depth=1) or use the GitHub REST API loader which fetches file contents directly without cloning. A second failure mode: GitLoader does not respect .gitignore — it will try to load binary files (images, PDFs, compiled artifacts) and produce garbage Document objects with binary content. Always filter loaders' output by file extension before ingestion.

LangChain's AzureChatOpenAI configuration has a non-obvious required parameter set that differs from ChatOpenAI: it requires azure_endpoint, azure_deployment (not model), and api_version, while ChatOpenAI requires only model. Teams migrating from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI frequently forget to change model='gpt-4o' to azure_deployment='my-deployment-name' (where the deployment name is set in Azure Portal, not the model name). The result: a 404 error with message 'The deployment specified does not exist' that looks like a networking problem. A second Azure-specific trap: API versions for Azure OpenAI lag behind OpenAI's feature releases by weeks to months — features like structured outputs or specific JSON modes may not be available until a later api_version is released. Always verify the api_version that supports your needed feature before designing a system that depends on it.

LangChain's BaseRetriever.get_relevant_documents() (sync) and BaseRetriever.aget_relevant_documents() (async) have a silent behavior difference: the sync version uses the async implementation internally (via asyncio.run) if an async implementation is provided but no sync override exists. In Python environments with an already-running event loop (FastAPI endpoints, Jupyter notebooks with nest_asyncio), this causes a 'cannot run nested event loop' RuntimeError. The correct pattern: always implement BOTH get_relevant_documents() and aget_relevant_documents() in custom retrievers, or inherit from BaseRetriever and only override _get_relevant_documents() and _aget_relevant_documents() (the private methods) which the public methods call without event loop management. Custom retrievers that only override the async version work in async contexts but fail when called synchronously — a common source of 'it works in production (async FastAPI) but not in my notebook (sync Python)' bugs.

LangChain's load_summarize_chain() and MapReduceDocumentsChain have a token counting bug in their default implementation: when splitting documents for the map step, they do not account for the prompt template tokens, only for the document content tokens. The result: map-step prompts that combine a 500-token system prompt with document chunks near the max_tokens limit will exceed the context window, causing API errors on longer documents. The UNSAID correct configuration: set token_max parameter in MapReduceDocumentsChain to (context_window_size - system_prompt_tokens - output_budget_tokens) rather than the model's full context window. For example, with a 16K context window, a 500-token system prompt, and 1000 tokens reserved for output: token_max = 16000 - 500 - 1000 = 14500. Teams that set token_max = 16000 will have intermittent failures on documents where the prompt overhead pushes the total over the limit.

LangChain's LLMChain (deprecated) silently changed behavior between v0.1 and v0.2: in v0.1, LLMChain.run() accepted a single string input; in v0.2, chain.invoke() requires a dict with named keys matching the prompt template variables. Code that calls chain.run('my input') in v0.1 style will raise a validation error under v0.2 because invoke() expects a dict, not a string. The further complication: many online tutorials written for v0.1 use the run() shorthand, so developers copying from tutorials encounter this immediately. The migration fix is simple — chain.run(x) becomes chain.invoke({'variable_name': x})['text'] — but discovering this requires understanding both the prompt template's variable name and the output key structure. A comprehensive migration guide should treat every occurrence of .run() as a potential breaking change and systematically replace with .invoke() with proper dict key mapping.

LangChain's experimental features in langchain_experimental package carry a higher breaking-change risk than core packages. AutoGPT, BabyAGI, and Plan-and-Execute agent implementations in langchain_experimental have been changed or broken across minor versions without deprecation warnings. The UNSAID practical rule: do not use langchain_experimental in production systems without locking the exact package version and committing to manual maintenance of that version. These experimental implementations are research demonstrations, not production-ready components — they are valuable for understanding architectural patterns but should be reimplemented from scratch in your codebase rather than imported and deployed. Teams that build production systems on top of langchain_experimental end up with unmaintainable code as the experimental implementations diverge from their production needs.

LangChain's Runnable.invoke() vs Runnable.stream() inconsistency in error handling: invoke() raises exceptions; stream() yields them as generator errors that must be caught in the consuming loop. Code that calls list(chain.stream(input)) will re-raise the exception correctly, but code that iterates with 'for chunk in chain.stream(input): print(chunk)' may consume partial output before the error, leaving the user with an incomplete streamed response they believe is complete. The production pattern: always wrap streaming loops in try/except and track whether the stream completed normally (via a completion flag set after the loop exits normally) versus abnormally (via exception). Send an error event to the client when the stream terminates abnormally rather than silently ending the SSE stream, which the client interprets as successful completion. This is the single most common streaming UX bug in LLM applications.

LangChain's tool result size can silently overflow context windows when tools return large datasets. The default implementations of tools like WebSearchTool, WikipediaQueryRun, and ArxivQueryRun return full text content without truncation — a Wikipedia article can be 50,000+ characters. When multiple tools return large results in a single agent turn, the accumulated tool messages can consume the model's entire context window, causing either a context_length_exceeded error or (worse) the model silently ignoring older messages due to context compression. The production hardening pattern: set max_characters on Wikipedia tool, doc_content_chars_max on Arxiv tool, and limit search result snippets in custom tools. Implement a tool output size budget at the agent level that tracks cumulative tool result tokens and truncates or summarizes overlong tool results before they are added to the message history.

LangChain's GitHub repository integration via GitHubToolkit exposes actions that include write operations (create_file, update_file, create_pull_request, create_review_request). An agent with GitHubToolkit and broad user instructions can accidentally push code to production branches if the GitHub token used has write permissions. The UNSAID production security pattern: always provision a dedicated GitHub personal access token or GitHub App token scoped to read-only permissions for RAG and code search use cases. For agents that genuinely need to create PRs, scope the token to a specific repository and enforce that the target branch is never main/master by adding a validation wrapper around the create_file and create_pull_request tools that rejects writes to protected branches. GitHubToolkit should never be configured with a token that has admin permissions — the difference between repository admin and contributor write is the difference between accidental PR creation and accidental repository settings changes.

LangChain's LCEL with complex nested chains has a subtle output key collision bug: when multiple branches of a RunnableParallel produce dicts with the same keys, the later branch's values silently overwrite the earlier branch's values in the merged output dict. For example, RunnableParallel(branch_a=chain_a, branch_b=chain_b) where chain_a returns {'result': 'a'} and chain_b also returns {'result': 'b'} produces {'branch_a': {'result': 'a'}, 'branch_b': {'result': 'b'}} — the outer keys prevent collision. However, if the branches are then merged with .assign() or dict-unpacking by a downstream lambda, the collision occurs. The safe pattern: always use the RunnableParallel's outer keys as namespaces (access via state['branch_a']['result']) rather than dict-unpacking the branch outputs, to maintain explicit namespace separation throughout the chain.

LangChain's ChatModel temperature parameter behavior differs across providers in ways that create reproducibility issues when switching models. OpenAI's temperature=0 does not guarantee identical outputs (there is documented non-determinism even at temperature=0 due to parallel GPU computation); Anthropic's temperature=0 is more deterministic. Additionally, temperature=1.0 has different effective randomness per provider — OpenAI's max temperature is 2.0; Anthropic's is 1.0. Setting temperature=1.5 on ChatAnthropic raises a validation error, while the same value on ChatOpenAI is valid. Teams that copy temperature settings across providers will encounter either validation errors or qualitatively different output variance. The portable temperature mapping: {conservative: 0.1-0.2, balanced: 0.5-0.7, creative: 0.9-1.0} works consistently across all major providers, avoiding provider-specific edge cases at the extremes.

LangChain's AgentExecutor early stopping mechanism (early_stopping_method) defaults to 'force' which forcibly stops the agent loop and asks the model to generate a final answer even if the max iterations were reached mid-task. The UNSAID failure mode: when an agent is forcibly stopped at max_iterations because it got stuck in a tool-calling loop, the 'generate_final_answer' prompt asks the model to respond with what it has so far. The model, seeing an incomplete conversation history, often generates a confident-sounding but incorrect answer rather than admitting it couldn't complete the task. The correct production behavior for incomplete agents: catch the AgentFinish exception after max_iterations, log the incomplete trace, and return an explicit 'task incomplete' response to the user rather than a fabricated answer. The early_stopping_method='generate' behavior actively produces confident hallucinations for interrupted agents.

LangChain's StrOutputParser does not validate or transform the LLM's output — it simply returns the content field of the AIMessage as a string. The UNSAID failure trap: when a chat model returns an AIMessage with a complex content structure (list of TextBlock and ThinkingBlock objects, as returned by Claude with extended thinking), StrOutputParser will concatenate all text blocks or raise an error on the list content. The symptom: code that worked with GPT-4o fails with Claude because Claude's AIMessage content is sometimes a list rather than a string. The production-safe pattern: use a custom output parser that normalizes AIMessage content to a string across all providers, handling both str content (OpenAI, standard) and list[dict] content (Anthropic with extended thinking, multimodal responses): extract text by iterating content blocks and joining text entries.

LangChain's Chroma client has a concurrency limitation in its default configuration: Chroma's default SQLite backend (used in persist_directory mode without a separate Chroma server) does not support concurrent write operations from multiple threads or processes. Multiple FastAPI workers writing to the same local Chroma directory will corrupt the database with concurrent write errors. The symptom: seemingly random database corruption errors or 'database is locked' SQLite errors under load. The safe production deployment options: (1) run a single-process FastAPI app with one Uvicorn worker for Chroma-backed applications; (2) migrate to Chroma server mode with an HTTP client that handles concurrent access correctly; (3) switch to a production vector database that is designed for concurrent access.
