Diagnosing theme-plugin conflicts: switch to the default theme and verify if the issue resolves — if yes, the custom theme is the cause. Narrow it down by disabling CSS snippets one by one. Report theme-specific issues to the theme author (GitHub Issues), not the plugin author. For production writing environments, delay theme updates by 1-2 weeks after release to let the community report compatibility issues. The most reliable approach: use the default Obsidian theme with targeted CSS snippets for specific customizations, avoiding full custom themes that touch many CSS properties.

Obsidian vaults that rely on absolute file paths in notes (hardcoded paths to attachments, OS-specific paths) break when the vault is moved to a different location or accessed from a different operating system. UNSAID: Absolute paths in markdown ('file:///Users/john/Documents/vault/attachments/image.png') only work on the specific machine with that path. They break on: a different user account, after moving the vault folder, on Windows (uses backslashes and drive letters), and in Obsidian Publish or Quartz (which can't access local file paths). The correct pattern: always use vault-relative paths ([[attachments/image.png]]) or Obsidian's wikilink syntax for attachments (![[image.png]]). Obsidian automatically maintains vault-relative paths when you move files using the vault's file pane — only files moved via OS file manager break links. The Dangling Links section (Settings > Files and Links > Detect broken links) shows all broken links in the vault, but it only finds broken wikilinks, not broken file:// absolute path references.

Obsidian's File Recovery and Obsidian Sync both retain versions of notes, but the retention policies differ enough to cause confusion about which system to use for recovery and what's actually recoverable. UNSAID: File Recovery: retains snapshots every 5 minutes, stored locally in '.obsidian/backups/', default retention is 7 days. Obsidian Sync Version History: retains all saved versions for 1 year (Sync Plus tier), 90 days (Sync standard tier), accessible across all devices. The confusion: after deleting a note, users check File Recovery and find it (because it exists in local snapshots) but can't access it from a different device. The delete recovery workflow differs by tier: for deleted notes, check the Obsidian Sync deleted items list in the sync pane (not File Recovery) to restore from cloud. File Recovery only shows versions of the current note — it doesn't list deleted notes. This asymmetry (version history in File Recovery, deleted file recovery in Sync) is documented but non-obvious, causing users to look in the wrong place during stressful recovery situations.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian Sync version history and deletion recovery

statement: Obsidian folder-based vault organization becomes a liability when knowledge is fundamentally cross-cutting — a note on 'mindfulness in leadership' doesn't belong in /Leadership/ OR /Mindfulness/ folders without duplicating it, exposing the folder model's single-placement constraint. UNSAID: The fundamental limitation of folder-based organization is that folders are a tree (each item has exactly one parent), but knowledge is a graph (each idea connects to many others). Trying to organize cross-cutting knowledge into folders creates: analysis paralysis (which folder does this go in?), duplicate notes in multiple folders, or arbitrary placement that makes retrieval unpredictable. Tags and wikilinks are the correct tools for cross-cutting concerns — they enable one note to live in one location while being discoverable through multiple retrieval paths. The irony: users who start with elaborate folder hierarchies often end up with flat folder structures over time as they realize that search + links outperform navigation for retrieval. Obsidian's recommendation is minimal folders (max 3-5) combined with tags and links for cross-cutting classification.

Obsidian vaults that grow beyond 5,000 notes without deliberate maintenance become increasingly difficult to navigate, creating the 'knowledge graveyard' anti-pattern where notes exist but are never rediscovered or used. UNSAID: The knowledge graveyard emerges from the compounding effect of capture without curation: notes added to the vault are never returned to, never linked to new content, and never synthesized into permanent notes. The symptoms: the graph view is densely connected in recent months but sparse in earlier years, the inbox folder has hundreds of notes with no processing date, Dataview queries for recent notes show the same cluster of files repeatedly. Recovery from the knowledge graveyard requires a 'vault archaelogy' process: spend 2 hours exploring old notes with the Random Note tool, link anything valuable to current permanent notes, and archive or delete anything that no longer holds value. Prevention: establish a 'process before adding' discipline — every note added to the vault requires processing at least one older note. This 1-for-1 exchange maintains vault density rather than allowing unbounded accumulation.

evidence_refs.description: The Collector's Fallacy and knowledge graveyard anti-pattern

statement: Obsidian and Logseq both fail as real-time collaboration tools for teams that need simultaneous editing, conflict-free merging, and user-level access control — features that require purpose-built collaborative infrastructure. UNSAID: The fundamental limitation: Obsidian and Logseq are single-player tools adapted for small team sharing, not purpose-built collaboration platforms. Real-time collaboration (Google Docs-style where multiple cursors edit simultaneously) is not possible in either tool — the file-based architecture doesn't support it. Obsidian Sync has 1-5 second latency for changes to propagate between devices; Logseq Sync is similar. This is sufficient for asynchronous team collaboration (one person edits at a time) but not for meetings where multiple people type into the same document simultaneously. For teams needing real-time collaboration: use Notion, Confluence, or Google Docs for the actively-edited collaborative documents, and Obsidian/Logseq for personal and archival knowledge. The hybrid approach: individuals maintain personal Obsidian/Logseq vaults for their own PKM, with a shared Notion/Confluence space for team documentation that requires active collaboration.

Ollama defaults to a context window of 2048 tokens regardless of the model's actual maximum supported context. If a conversation or prompt exceeds 2048 tokens, Ollama silently truncates from the left — the oldest messages are dropped without any warning, error, or indication in the API response. This is the most common source of 'the model forgot earlier context' complaints. The fix is to explicitly set num_ctx in the Modelfile or in the API request options, matching or approaching the model's true maximum (e.g., 131072 for Llama 3.1).

Ollama does not auto-detect and set num_ctx based on the model's advertised maximum context length. Even when pulling a model whose model card states '128K context', Ollama will still use its 2048-token default until the user explicitly sets num_ctx. Increasing num_ctx has a direct VRAM cost: a context of 128K tokens requires approximately 16GB of additional VRAM beyond the model weights for the KV cache, depending on the number of layers and heads. Setting num_ctx to the model's maximum without accounting for available VRAM causes Ollama to offload KV cache layers to CPU RAM, which reduces throughput by 10-50x.

Ollama's concurrent request handling is controlled by OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL (default 1 on CPU, up to 4 on GPU). With the default of 1, all requests are serialized — a second request blocks until the first completes. This is appropriate for interactive single-user use but causes severe queuing in multi-user or API gateway scenarios where even 2-3 concurrent users experience long waits. Increasing OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL beyond the GPU's parallel capacity causes context fragmentation: each concurrent slot shares the same total KV cache budget, so 4 parallel slots at num_ctx=8192 each consume the same VRAM as a single slot at num_ctx=32768. The correct formula is: OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL * num_ctx must fit in available VRAM KV cache budget, or slots will spill to CPU RAM and negate the parallelism gain.

Ollama's GPU layer offloading (num_gpu parameter in the API or OLLAMA_NUM_GPU env var) controls how many model layers run on GPU vs CPU. Setting num_gpu=0 forces full CPU inference even on a GPU machine — this silently works but is orders of magnitude slower. Setting num_gpu higher than the actual layer count is treated as 'all layers', which is safe. The failure mode specific to multi-GPU systems: Ollama does not automatically distribute layers across multiple GPUs; it uses only the GPU with the most free VRAM unless OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD=true is set. On a 4-GPU server where only one GPU has the model loaded, the other three GPUs sit idle while the first is overloaded. OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD distributes layers evenly across all detected GPUs, enabling utilization of aggregate VRAM for models that exceed single-GPU capacity.

Ollama's default listening address (0.0.0.0:11434 when OLLAMA_HOST is set to 0.0.0.0) exposes the API on all network interfaces with no authentication. Any process or device on the same network can pull models, run inference, and consume GPU resources. On cloud VMs with public IPs, this results in cryptominer-style abuse where external actors run inference workloads on the exposed Ollama instance. The default localhost-only binding (127.0.0.1:11434) is safe; only change it when there is a specific need for remote access, and in that case always put Ollama behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) with authentication middleware. The Ollama project has explicitly stated there is no built-in authentication and recommends network-layer controls. Running Ollama on a public cloud instance without a firewall rule restricting port 11434 is a common and costly mistake.

Ollama's VRAM auto-selection picks the quantization level that fits in available VRAM without explicit user configuration. However, on systems with both integrated and discrete GPUs, Ollama may incorrectly identify integrated GPU VRAM as available and attempt to load large models into shared system memory, causing swap thrashing and severe performance degradation. The OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD environment variable and explicit GPU selection via CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES are needed to prevent integrated GPU selection. Symptoms include model load times over 60 seconds and inference speeds under 1 token/second despite an adequate discrete GPU being present.

Ollama models pulled from the registry are stored as blob files in ~/.ollama/models/blobs using a content-addressed storage scheme. Manually deleting blobs without using ollama rm corrupts the manifest index, causing subsequent ollama run commands to fail with a manifest not found error even though the blob files still exist. Always use ollama rm <model> for model deletion. If the manifest index is corrupted, remove the ~/.ollama/models/manifests directory and re-pull required models. The blobs directory can grow to hundreds of GB on systems with many models and must be monitored for disk space.

Ollama's chat template for models must match the model's training format. When pulling a community model file from Ollama's registry that uses an incorrect chat template in its Modelfile, the model produces incoherent responses because system and user message delimiters are wrong. Symptoms include the model echoing the user's message prefix, generating responses in the wrong language, or failing to follow system prompt instructions. Verify the chat template against the original model card on HuggingFace before deployment. Custom Modelfiles can override the template using the TEMPLATE directive. Models in GGUF format embed the correct template in metadata, but Modelfile TEMPLATE overrides take precedence.

Ollama's default request timeout is infinite on the server side, but clients with their own timeouts (curl --max-time, Python requests with timeout parameter) may terminate the connection before a large model finishes generating. For 70B models generating at 3-5 tokens/second, a 5000-token response takes 15-25 minutes. Client-side timeouts set to 30 seconds (a common default) silently cut off these long generations. When using Ollama in production pipelines, set client timeouts to at least 600 seconds for large models or implement streaming consumption to keep the connection alive throughout generation.

When Ollama is updated to a newer version, existing GGUF model files in the blobs directory remain compatible but new model pulls may use updated GGUF versions that are faster or more accurate. Applications pinning to a specific ollama model tag (e.g., llama3.1:8b) get the current default quantization at pull time, which changes as Ollama updates default quantizations. A pull on a different machine may get a different quantization than the original pull, causing subtle behavioral differences between development and production. Pin the full quantization tag (e.g., llama3.1:8b-instruct-q4_K_M) for reproducible deployments.

Ollama's macOS Metal (Apple Silicon) backend uses unified memory architecture where CPU and GPU share the same memory pool. Unlike discrete GPU systems where VRAM is separate from system RAM, Apple Silicon treats memory allocation as a single pool. When Ollama loads a 13B model requiring 8GB and other applications are using 12GB of the 16GB total, the model loads into swap, causing inference speeds under 1 token/second instead of the expected 15-20 tokens/second. Monitoring actual memory pressure via Activity Monitor (not just model size vs total RAM) is essential on Apple Silicon. Closing other memory-intensive applications before loading large models dramatically improves performance.

Ollama's embedding models return different vector dimensions than expected when the model is pulled without specifying the exact variant. For example, nomic-embed-text:latest and nomic-embed-text:v1.5 produce 768-dimensional embeddings, but a future version may change this. Mixing embeddings from different model versions in the same vector store causes incorrect similarity calculations because dimensions differ or the embedding space is not aligned. Always pin the exact model version for embedding models (e.g., nomic-embed-text:v1.5) and never mix embeddings from different model versions in the same index.

Ollama's default system prompt (defined in the model's Modelfile) is overridden when a system message is included in the API request's messages array. However, when using the /api/generate endpoint (not /api/chat), the system parameter takes a string that replaces the Modelfile's system prompt entirely rather than being appended to it. Applications mixing the generate and chat endpoints for different call types may observe inconsistent system prompt behavior. Standardize on the /api/chat endpoint with explicit system messages for consistent prompt handling across all request types.

Ollama versions before 0.3.0 did not properly implement the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint's tool_calls response format. Applications using the OpenAI Python SDK pointing to older Ollama instances fail when parsing tool_call responses because the id field format differs from OpenAI's call_ prefix convention. Update Ollama to 0.3.0+ for reliable OpenAI SDK tool calling compatibility. Alternatively, use Ollama's native /api/chat endpoint and implement tool call parsing directly, as the native API format is stable across Ollama versions.

Ollama's temperature parameter behavior differs from OpenAI for values above 1.0. While OpenAI clamps temperature to the range 0-2, Ollama's llama.cpp backend passes temperature directly to the sampler without clamping, allowing values above 2.0 which produce increasingly incoherent text. Applications migrating from OpenAI that test with high temperature values (1.5-1.9) on Ollama may get noticeably different generation quality because the sampler interpretation differs. Additionally, Ollama's default temperature when not specified is 0.8 rather than OpenAI's 1.0, causing slightly more conservative default behavior without explicit configuration.

Ollama's ROCm support for AMD GPUs on Linux requires specific ROCm versions that match the AMD GPU architecture. Using ROCm 5.x with RDNA3 GPUs or ROCm 6.x with older GCN GPUs causes Ollama to fall back to CPU inference silently. The OLLAMA_DEBUG=1 environment variable reveals whether GPU acceleration is active in the startup logs. On AMD systems, verify ROCm installation matches the GPU with rocminfo and check that /dev/kfd and /dev/dri devices are accessible to the Ollama process. The Docker ollama/ollama:rocm image bundles the correct ROCm dependencies and eliminates compatibility issues at the cost of a larger container image.

Ollama's context handling truncates input silently when the prompt exceeds num_ctx tokens. Unlike OpenAI which returns a 400 error for prompts exceeding the context window, Ollama truncates the beginning of the conversation history without any warning or error response. This means long conversations lose their earliest context silently, causing the model to appear to forget earlier discussed topics. Applications must implement client-side context management using a tokenizer to estimate prompt length and prune history proactively before sending to Ollama. Use the model's vocabulary tokenizer (accessible via Modelfile metadata) for accurate token counting.

Using Ollama's /api/generate endpoint with raw: true bypasses the chat template formatting, sending the prompt exactly as provided to the model's tokenizer. This is only appropriate for models used in raw text completion mode. Passing a chat-formatted prompt string with raw: true to an instruction-tuned model produces poor results because the model's instruction-following capabilities depend on its specific chat template delimiters being present. Conversely, not using raw: true with a base (non-instruct) model causes the chat template to be applied to what should be a raw continuation prompt. Always match the endpoint and raw setting to the model's intended usage mode.

Ollama's concurrent request queue has no maximum depth limit by default. On a system where model inference is slow (CPU-only, or thermal throttling), requests pile up in the queue indefinitely while clients wait. A web application using Ollama without its own request timeout will have user requests hanging for minutes. Implement a maximum queue depth at the application layer: if the Ollama request queue depth exceeds N requests (monitored via request tracking), reject new requests with a 503 error rather than queuing indefinitely. Use OLLAMA_SCHED_SPREAD=true to distribute load more evenly across available models in multi-model deployments.

Ollama's embedding API returns embeddings as a flat float64 array, but some vector databases expect float32 arrays. NumPy conversion from float64 to float32 is necessary before inserting into ChromaDB, Qdrant, or Pinecone which use 32-bit float vectors internally. Passing float64 arrays to ChromaDB causes silent type coercion that doubles memory usage; passing to Pinecone raises a type validation error. Always explicitly cast: numpy.array(embedding, dtype=numpy.float32) before vector store insertion. Additionally, Ollama's embedding endpoint does not support batch embedding of multiple texts in a single call; each text requires a separate API call, unlike OpenAI's batch embedding endpoint.

Ollama's /api/generate endpoint does not support the images field for multimodal input; images must be sent via the /api/chat endpoint using the role: user message with an images array. Applications that attempt to pass images to /api/generate receive a 400 error. This API asymmetry is non-obvious because both endpoints appear to accept the same options. Additionally, the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint handles images differently from the native /api/chat endpoint: images must be in the message content array as type: image_url rather than in the top-level images field.

Ollama server logs sensitive information at the INFO level, including partial request bodies when debug mode is enabled. On shared machines where Ollama serves multiple users via a shared endpoint, enabling OLLAMA_DEBUG=1 can expose user query content to anyone with access to the server logs. Disable debug logging in production environments by ensuring OLLAMA_DEBUG is not set. For operational monitoring that requires detailed logging, use a middleware proxy layer (nginx, Caddy) to log request metadata (path, response time, status) without logging request bodies.

Ollama's automatic GPU layer selection places all model layers on GPU when VRAM is sufficient, but does not account for VRAM needed for other running GPU processes. If a PyTorch training job or another ML workload is concurrently using GPU memory, Ollama may successfully load the model during low-memory periods but fail with CUDA out of memory errors when GPU memory pressure increases from the concurrent workload. Use nvidia-smi to check available VRAM before starting Ollama model serving, and set OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD to reserve GPU memory for other processes when co-hosting multiple GPU workloads.

Ollama's /api/tags endpoint lists all locally available models but does not indicate which models are currently loaded in VRAM. A model listed by /api/tags requires a cold load (10-60 seconds for large models) if it was unloaded due to keep_alive expiry. Applications that check model availability via /api/tags before routing requests do not distinguish between warm (VRAM-loaded) and cold (disk-only) models, causing unexpected latency spikes. Use the /api/ps endpoint (available from Ollama 0.1.33+) to list currently running (VRAM-loaded) models and their memory usage, enabling routing that prefers warm models to minimize cold-start latency.

Ollama does not validate that the Modelfile TEMPLATE directive uses valid Go template syntax before creating the model. An invalid template (missing closing braces, incorrect function calls) causes the model creation to succeed but all inference requests to fail with a confusing template execution error. The error only manifests at inference time, not during ollama create. Always test new Modelfile templates by running ollama run <new-model> hello immediately after creation to catch template errors before deployment. The most common template error is incorrect use of .System vs .Prompt variables which differ between the /api/generate and /api/chat endpoints.

Ollama's network binding security defaults changed between versions. Before version 0.1.24, Ollama bound to 0.0.0.0 by default, exposing the API to all network interfaces without authentication. After 0.1.24, the default changed to localhost-only. Installations upgraded from older versions may retain old configuration or have OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 set explicitly, leaving the API exposed without authentication. Run netstat -tlnp | grep 11434 to verify Ollama's actual binding address. For team deployments requiring network access, place Ollama behind an authenticated reverse proxy rather than exposing it directly with OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0.

Ollama's model loading on macOS uses mmap with MAP_PRIVATE to map GGUF files into virtual memory. On macOS 13+, large file mmap operations require the file to be on a non-encrypted APFS volume. Placing the Ollama models directory (~/.ollama/models) on an encrypted APFS volume or an external drive with different filesystem characteristics causes significantly slower model loading times (5-10x slower than expected). If Ollama model loading consistently takes over 60 seconds for a 7B model on Apple Silicon, check that the models directory is on the main internal APFS volume rather than a mounted encrypted volume or external storage.

Ollama's behavior when the requested model is not found locally differs from OpenAI's 404 response. Instead of returning an HTTP error, Ollama either automatically pulls the model from the registry (if OLLAMA_AUTO_PULL is enabled) or returns an error with model not found in the response body with HTTP 404. Applications that check for HTTP 200 as the success indicator and then parse the body may not notice the model not found error embedded in an otherwise well-structured response. When OLLAMA_AUTO_PULL causes automatic downloads in production, a simple typo in a model name can trigger a 40GB download unexpectedly. Disable auto-pull in production and always validate model availability at startup.

Ollama's OLLAMA_MODELS directory path must not contain spaces or special characters on Windows due to a Go path handling bug that was fixed in some but not all versions. Models stored in paths like C:\Users\John Smith\ollama\models cause manifest loading failures with cryptic path parsing errors on Windows. Always configure OLLAMA_MODELS to a path without spaces on Windows (e.g., C:\ollama\models). On macOS and Linux, spaces in paths are handled correctly. This limitation affects users who installed Ollama with the default user home directory when the Windows username contains spaces.

When running Ollama behind a reverse proxy like nginx, response streaming breaks if the proxy buffers responses. nginx's proxy_buffering on configuration buffers the entire response before forwarding, converting streaming responses to non-streaming from the client's perspective. Set proxy_buffering off and proxy_read_timeout 600 in nginx for the Ollama upstream. For Traefik, disable response buffering with the traefik.http.middlewares.ollama.buffering.maxRequestBodyBytes=0 label. Without these settings, users see no streaming output until the full generation completes, defeating the purpose of streaming and causing timeouts for long generations.

Ollama's behavior when processing requests faster than the client reads the streaming output causes internal buffer overflow issues in versions before 0.3.5. If a client reads streaming chunks slowly (or processes each chunk before reading the next), Ollama's internal write buffer fills up and the server stalls, causing other concurrent requests to queue up. Always consume Ollama streaming responses as fast as possible in a background thread/coroutine, buffering received tokens for processing rather than processing inline during the read loop. This backpressure issue was fixed in 0.3.5+ but remains relevant for older deployments.

Ollama's default sampler parameters (temperature 0.8, top_p 0.9, top_k 40) are set for general conversational use and may produce suboptimal results for specialized tasks. For deterministic extraction tasks, temperature must be explicitly set to 0 in each request; Ollama does not provide a way to globally override defaults without creating a custom Modelfile for every model. Applications that forget to set temperature: 0 in structured extraction requests get non-deterministic results where the same input produces different JSON outputs. Always explicitly specify all sampling parameters in production request payloads rather than relying on Modelfile or server defaults.

Running Ollama inside a Docker container on macOS does not give the container access to the Apple Silicon GPU (Metal). Docker Desktop for Mac uses a Linux VM for containers, and Metal GPU access is not passed through to the Linux VM. All model inference inside Docker on macOS runs on CPU only, regardless of the --gpus flag which has no effect on macOS. For GPU-accelerated Ollama on macOS, run Ollama natively as a macOS application, not in Docker. This is a fundamental limitation of Docker Desktop's hypervisor architecture on macOS and not an Ollama issue.

Ollama's model manifest uses SHA256 hashes for layer verification but does not re-verify layer integrity on every load, only on first pull. If a GGUF blob file is corrupted (partial write from an interrupted pull, filesystem corruption, or disk error), Ollama loads the corrupted model and produces incoherent or empty outputs without reporting an error. Run ollama rm <model> followed by ollama pull <model> to force a fresh verified download if a model that previously worked starts producing garbage output. The manifest checksum mismatch is the most common cause of sudden model quality degradation without any code changes.

Ollama's num_gpu parameter controls how many model layers are placed on GPU, with 0 forcing CPU-only inference. Setting num_gpu to a value higher than the model's actual layer count has no additional effect but is harmless. However, setting num_gpu to 1 on a multi-layer transformer model places only the first layer on GPU and all remaining layers on CPU, causing dramatically slower inference than either fully GPU or fully CPU inference due to constant GPU-CPU data transfers. Always set num_gpu to 999 (effectively all layers) for GPU inference, or 0 for CPU inference. Never set num_gpu to a small positive integer unless deliberately profiling partial-GPU placement.

Ollama's model weights in GGUF format are stored as a single file (or occasionally split across multiple files for very large models). If the system runs out of disk space during a model pull, the partial download creates an incomplete GGUF file that fails manifest verification. Ollama does not automatically retry or resume interrupted downloads; ollama pull must be restarted from scratch. For very large models (70B Q4 = ~40GB), interrupted downloads on metered or slow connections are a significant risk. Pre-check available disk space (need 2x the model size: one for download and one for the final blob) before pulling large models.

Ollama's model context (conversation history compressed as a context array in the /api/generate response) is invalidated when the Ollama server restarts or when the model is unloaded from VRAM due to keep_alive timeout. Applications that store the context array for continued conversations get an error when attempting to use a stale context after a server restart or model reload. The context is an in-memory representation tied to the model's loaded state. For persistent conversations, always store the full message history client-side and reconstruct the conversation via message history rather than relying on server-side context arrays.

Ollama's concurrency behavior when OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL > 1 allocates separate KV caches per parallel slot. If a request arrives when all slots are busy, it waits in the Ollama internal queue with no timeout or queue depth visibility from the API. Applications using Ollama with high parallelism and variable request rates experience unpredictable latency spikes when the queue fills up, with no way to detect queue depth from the outside. Implement application-level queue depth monitoring by tracking in-flight request counts, and use circuit-breaker patterns to reject requests when estimated queue wait time exceeds the application's latency SLA.

Ollama's Modelfile PARAMETER directive for setting defaults has lower priority than per-request options in the API. Setting PARAMETER temperature 0.1 in a Modelfile does not prevent callers from overriding with temperature: 0.9 in the request options. This is intentional design but surprises developers who expect Modelfile settings to act as enforced constraints. Enforcing parameter bounds for specific use cases requires application-level enforcement in the API proxy layer rather than Modelfile configuration. Never rely on Modelfile PARAMETER settings as security boundaries for production deployments where callers might pass arbitrary options.

Ollama's error responses do not consistently use HTTP error codes. Some errors (model not found, invalid parameters) return HTTP 404 or 400 with a JSON body containing an error field. Other errors (server-side generation failures) return HTTP 200 with an error field embedded in the streaming response body alongside the done: true field. Applications that check only HTTP status codes for error detection miss generation-time errors that arrive as HTTP 200 responses. Always check both the HTTP status code AND the error field in every response object, including streaming response chunks, to detect all error conditions.

On systems with NVIDIA GPUs and multiple CUDA installations (common on development machines with conda environments), Ollama may load against the wrong CUDA version, causing subtle numerical errors or crashes. Ollama uses its bundled CUDA runtime rather than the system CUDA, which prevents most version conflicts, but cuDNN library conflicts can still occur when OLLAMA_CUDA_MALLOC is set or when using certain GPU configurations. If Ollama crashes immediately after model loading on CUDA-capable hardware, unset all CUDA_* environment variables before starting Ollama to eliminate environment contamination from other CUDA installations.

Ollama's default logging on macOS writes to ~/Library/Logs/Ollama/server.log without log rotation. On systems running Ollama continuously for months, this log file can grow to 10-50GB, eventually causing disk-full errors. Configure log rotation using newsyslog on macOS or logrotate on Linux with a 100MB size limit and 5 retained files. The log file path is not configurable via Ollama environment variables; log rotation must be configured at the OS level. Monitor disk usage in /home or the user home directory on Ollama deployments to catch log growth before it causes system failures.

When Ollama is updated, the bundled llama.cpp version may change computation behavior for certain operations, causing model outputs to shift subtly. This breaks applications relying on Ollama version pinning for consistent model behavior, as the GGUF model file version is the same but the inference implementation changes. Version-pin the Ollama binary (Docker image with specific version tag) rather than always using latest to ensure inference consistency. For production deployments, test the new Ollama version on a canary instance with a representative query set before rolling out to all instances.

Ollama's embedding API does not normalize embedding vectors to unit length by default. Some embedding models (nomic-embed-text) return unit-normalized vectors, while others return vectors with varying magnitudes. When mixing embeddings from different models or model versions in the same index, unnormalized embeddings from one source cannot be compared via dot product with normalized embeddings from another; cosine similarity via normalized dot product is only equivalent to Euclidean distance when vectors are unit-normalized. Always explicitly normalize embedding vectors to unit length using numpy: vector / numpy.linalg.norm(vector) before storing in vector indexes, regardless of the model source.

When Ollama serves multiple users simultaneously via OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL, the KV cache for each parallel slot is allocated in VRAM at model load time. If a user's request exceeds the per-slot KV cache (their conversation is longer than num_ctx tokens), the oldest tokens are silently evicted from the KV cache, causing the model to lose earlier conversation context. This eviction happens per-slot, not globally; different parallel users each have their own context window and eviction behavior. The KV cache eviction is not reported in the API response and manifests as the model forgetting earlier conversation content without any error.

Ollama's prompt template variable substitution uses Go's text/template syntax, which is different from Jinja2 (used by HuggingFace) and Python f-strings. Common mistakes in custom Modelfile templates include using Python-style {{ variable }} (correct in Go templates) but also accidentally using Python-style {%if%} conditions which are invalid in Go templates. The correct Go template conditional is {{if .System}}. Go template parsing errors in Modelfiles produce unhelpful runtime errors at inference time rather than clear syntax errors at model creation time. Test custom templates with ollama run immediately after creation to catch all template errors before deployment.

Ollama on systems with multiple NVIDIA GPUs assigns all GPUs to model serving by default (CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is not set). This prevents other processes from using GPU resources on a shared machine. Setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 in Ollama's environment restricts it to GPU 0, leaving GPU 1 available for other workloads. Failing to configure GPU isolation on shared research or development servers leads to resource contention where Ollama's idle model keep_alive holds VRAM that other processes need. Always configure GPU assignment explicitly in shared environments using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES before starting Ollama.

Ollama's embedding API response format changed between versions: early versions returned embedding as a flat array at the top level of the JSON response; newer versions wrap it in an embeddings array (plural). Code written against older Ollama versions that accesses response.embedding (singular, flat array) fails on newer versions that return response.embeddings (plural, array of arrays). When using the Python ollama library, version pinning prevents this breaking change from affecting production. Always inspect the actual API response format when upgrading Ollama and update the response parsing code accordingly.

On systems with multiple users sharing an Ollama instance, the default Ollama server has no per-user rate limiting or quota enforcement. A single user running a batch job with OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL concurrent requests monopolizes all available parallel slots, starving other users. The Ollama server provides no built-in admission control or fair queuing. Implement fair queuing and per-user rate limiting in a proxy layer (nginx + Lua, or a custom FastAPI proxy) that routes requests to Ollama with user-specific request throttling. This proxy pattern is the only way to enforce fair resource sharing in multi-user Ollama deployments.

Ollama's network transport does not implement HTTP keep-alive connection reuse for the Python requests library by default. Each requests.post() to Ollama creates a new TCP connection, adding 1-5ms TCP handshake overhead per request. For applications making hundreds of requests per minute, this overhead is significant. Use a requests.Session() object for connection reuse, or preferably use the httpx library with async support and connection pooling. The ollama Python library uses httpx internally and does support connection reuse, making it more efficient than raw requests for high-frequency Ollama clients.

When using Ollama's structured output format (format: json_schema), the constrained generation can become confused by schema properties named the same as common JSON keywords like type, format, or properties. The GGUF grammar-based sampler interprets these as meta-level JSON schema keywords rather than property names, producing malformed output. Rename schema properties that conflict with JSON Schema reserved keywords when using Ollama's constrained generation. For example, rename a property called type to entity_type or record_type to avoid conflicts with the JSON Schema type keyword used in the schema definition itself.

Ollama's behavior under memory pressure on macOS with Apple Silicon is unpredictable. When other applications claim large amounts of unified memory while a model is loaded, the macOS VM subsystem may page out parts of the model's memory-mapped GGUF file to disk, causing inference speed to drop dramatically and non-deterministically. Unlike CUDA VRAM which is completely isolated from system RAM, Apple Silicon's unified memory means Safari with many tabs, Xcode compilation, or Figma with large files directly compete with Ollama for memory bandwidth. For consistent Ollama performance on macOS, close memory-intensive applications during active model use.

Ollama's SIGTERM handling during model inference causes the current inference to be terminated mid-generation with a partial response. Applications using systemd or Docker that send SIGTERM for graceful shutdown receive an incomplete final response for in-progress requests. There is no graceful drain behavior where Ollama finishes current requests before shutting down. For applications requiring graceful shutdown, implement a connection draining mechanism in the application layer: stop accepting new requests, wait for all in-progress requests to complete (with a timeout), then send SIGTERM to Ollama. Without this drain, users receive truncated responses during deployments.

On Ubuntu 22.04 with NVIDIA drivers installed via apt, Ollama may fail to detect the GPU if the NVIDIA driver modules are not fully loaded at the time Ollama starts. This commonly occurs in automated deployment scripts that install drivers and immediately start Ollama without a reboot or modprobe nvidia. The symptom is Ollama reporting CPU-only inference mode despite a GPU being present. Always include a driver readiness check (nvidia-smi should succeed) in deployment scripts before starting Ollama, or perform a full reboot after driver installation on fresh Ubuntu deployments.

Ollama's chat endpoint returns the full assistant message in the message.content field only in the final (non-streaming) response. When using streaming mode (stream:true), the assistant message content is distributed across multiple chunks, each with a partial message.content field containing only the new tokens for that chunk. Code that attempts to access the full response from the first streaming chunk receives only the first few tokens. Applications must aggregate all message.content values from all streaming chunks before processing the full response, or switch to stream:false for cases where the complete response is needed before processing can begin.

Migrating from SDK v0.x to v1.0 breaks every call site because the module-level `openai.ChatCompletion.create()` API was removed entirely — it is not deprecated with a shim, it simply does not exist. The failure mode is a hard `AttributeError` at runtime, not at import time, so tests that mock the old path pass while production calls crash. The correct migration is: (1) instantiate `client = OpenAI()`, (2) replace `openai.ChatCompletion.create(**kwargs)` with `client.chat.completions.create(**kwargs)`, (3) note that response objects changed from dict-like to Pydantic models so `.get('choices')` must become `.choices`. OpenAI provides an official migration guide and a `openai migrate` CLI tool that runs codemods, but the codemods miss dynamic call sites constructed with `getattr`.

Token counts returned in `response.usage.prompt_tokens` and `response.usage.completion_tokens` do not always match what appears on the billing dashboard. Known causes: (1) the API counts tokens server-side using its own tokenizer version, which may differ from `tiktoken` used locally; (2) function/tool call definitions are injected into the prompt by the server before tokenization — these hidden tokens are counted in `prompt_tokens` but many developers forget to account for them when estimating costs; (3) image inputs (vision API) use a tile-based token formula that is computed server-side and cannot be predicted client-side without replicating the exact tile logic. The correct approach is to trust `response.usage` for billing estimation and treat local `tiktoken` counts as approximations only.

OpenAI's Assistants API maintains conversation state server-side via threads, and each thread can accumulate thousands of messages over time. The failure mode: threads are never automatically truncated or pruned — they grow indefinitely, and older threads with 200+ messages cause the Assistant to silently truncate early messages when constructing the context window for a new run. Unlike the chat completions API where context management is explicit (you control the messages array), Assistants API context management is opaque. Teams have discovered this only when users complained that the Assistant 'forgot' information from earlier in long conversations. Mitigation: implement thread rotation (create a new thread and summarize the old one) when thread message count exceeds a threshold, or set truncation_strategy on the run to control which messages are preserved.

OpenAI SDK's httpx timeout configuration applies separately to connect timeout and read timeout. The default read timeout is 600 seconds, which means a hung server call will block for 10 minutes before raising a TimeoutError. For applications with strict latency SLOs, this default is catastrophically long. Setting timeout=httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=30.0, write=10.0, pool=5.0) on the OpenAI client constructor correctly bounds all network operations. A common mistake is setting timeout=30 (scalar) thinking this covers all operations — a scalar timeout only sets the read timeout, leaving connect and pool timeouts at their defaults. Under network failures where the TCP connection hangs at the SYN stage, a scalar read timeout does not prevent the 10-minute block. Testing timeout behavior requires a tool like toxiproxy to simulate network delays, as real API endpoints are too reliable to trigger timeout paths in development.

OpenAI's moderation endpoint (/v1/moderations) returns a categories dict and a category_scores dict, but the flagged boolean is computed from a proprietary threshold applied server-side to the scores. The threshold changes over time as OpenAI updates the moderation model, which means the same text can be flagged differently across weeks without any API version change or notification. Code that caches 'safe' moderation results and reuses them should set TTLs of hours, not days. A second failure: the moderation endpoint is NOT the same model used to moderate requests to chat/completions — the production moderation guardrails are applied internally and are more conservative than the standalone moderation endpoint. Passing content through /v1/moderations and receiving 'not flagged' does not guarantee the corresponding /v1/chat/completions call will succeed if the content is borderline.

The OpenAI SDK does not automatically retry on 429 (rate limit) errors unless max_retries is explicitly set at client construction time. The default max_retries value is 2, providing limited resilience for bursty traffic. Production applications that do not configure max_retries=5 or implement their own exponential backoff experience visible failures when rate limits are hit. The SDK's built-in retry uses exponential backoff with jitter and respects the Retry-After header when present. Setting max_retries=0 disables all retries, which is appropriate only for testing or when implementing custom retry logic externally.

The OpenAI SDK streaming API (stream=True) does not automatically accumulate the full response text. Each chunk in the stream is a ChatCompletionChunk object with a delta field containing only the new tokens. Code that assigns the last chunk to a variable and reads its content gets only the final token, not the complete response. The correct pattern is to accumulate all delta.content values in a string buffer across all chunks. The SDK's newer streaming helper (client.chat.completions.stream()) handles accumulation automatically and provides a final_message() method, but requires using a context manager pattern.

OpenAI API responses include a content_filter_results field that indicates whether content was flagged. Applications that ignore this field and directly use the response text may silently get partially redacted responses where the model refused to complete certain sections. The finish_reason field will be content_filter when this occurs, but many applications only check for finish_reason=stop. Responses truncated by content filters appear as normal text but are missing the filtered portions, causing silent corruption of AI-generated content in production pipelines. Always check finish_reason before processing the response content.

The OpenAI SDK raises APIConnectionError for network-level failures and APIStatusError for HTTP error responses. A common mistake is catching only Exception or OpenAIError at the top level, which treats rate limits (429) the same as invalid API keys (401) and server errors (500), losing the ability to handle each case appropriately. Rate limits should trigger retry with backoff, authentication errors should alert operations immediately, and server errors should be retried a fixed number of times. Implementing granular exception handling (RateLimitError, AuthenticationError, APIStatusError with status_code checks) prevents silent error conflation in production.

Applications that pass None values in the messages array content field cause silent truncation or 400 errors depending on the content type. When building message histories programmatically, None content values arise when tool results are empty, when document retrieval returns nothing, or when user input validation is skipped. The SDK does not validate message structure before sending, so None content is serialized as JSON null, which the API rejects. Always sanitize message content before appending to the history: replace None with an empty string or a placeholder like no content available to prevent malformed requests.

The OpenAI SDK timeout parameter controls the maximum time for the HTTP request to complete, not the time to receive the first token. For streaming requests where stream=True, the timeout governs the time to establish the connection and receive the first chunk, not the total streaming duration. A timeout=30 causes a TimeoutError if the first chunk takes over 30 seconds, but does not limit total stream duration. Long-running streaming responses that start quickly but run for many minutes are not constrained by the timeout parameter. Implement an external streaming duration limit using asyncio.wait_for() or a thread-based timer for streaming requests.

Applications that pass images to GPT-4 Vision using URL references fail silently when the URLs become inaccessible. The model receives an unloadable image URL and may either respond based on the URL filename alone, refuse to answer, or hallucinate image content. Unlike base64-encoded images that are embedded directly in the request, URL-referenced images must be publicly accessible at inference time. Internal URLs, signed URLs with short expiry, and localhost URLs all cause this failure. For production image-in-context applications, pre-download images and encode them as base64 data URIs to guarantee availability.

Using the OpenAI SDK in Python multiprocessing environments where the client is initialized at module import time causes connection pool exhaustion and SSL certificate verification failures. The httpx client's connection pool is not fork-safe, and forked processes inherit the parent's connection pool state without proper initialization. This manifests as intermittent ConnectionError or SSL handshake failures in the child processes. The fix is to initialize the OpenAI client inside the worker function (after fork) rather than at module level. Using threading instead of multiprocessing avoids this issue as threads share the connection pool safely.

OpenAI's JSON mode (response_format={type:json_object}) guarantees valid JSON output but does not guarantee the JSON matches any particular schema. The model may return a valid JSON object with completely different keys than the application expects, causing KeyError or schema validation failures downstream. JSON mode prevents parse errors but not semantic schema mismatches. For strict schema conformance, use Structured Outputs with a JSON schema definition and strict=true instead of JSON mode. Many applications switched from prompt-based JSON instructions to JSON mode expecting schema enforcement, only to discover that schema violation is still possible.

The OpenAI SDK's streaming response does not include usage statistics by default. When stream=True, the response.usage field is None because usage is only available in the final non-streaming response. To get token usage in streaming mode, pass stream_options={include_usage:True} in the API call. This causes an additional final chunk with usage data to be emitted at the end of the stream. Applications tracking token costs for streaming responses that do not set include_usage=True will have incomplete cost accounting, potentially underreporting API costs in billing systems.

Applications that compare model names as strings to select behavior fail when OpenAI releases new model aliases. For example, code that checks model_name == gpt-4 fails to apply GPT-4 optimizations when OpenAI introduces gpt-4-2024-11-20 as the current gpt-4 alias, because the alias resolution happens server-side and the returned model field in the response reflects the resolved model name, not the alias. Always use model name prefix matching (model_name.startswith(gpt-4)) for capability classification rather than exact string equality, or maintain an explicit capability mapping from model families to capability flags.

The OpenAI SDK's default behavior when api_key is not provided is to read from the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable. Applications that do not set this environment variable but also do not pass api_key explicitly receive an AuthenticationError at first API call, not at client instantiation. This means the error surfaces during the first request rather than at startup, potentially failing mid-operation in a multi-step pipeline. Explicitly validate the API key at application startup by instantiating the client and calling a lightweight endpoint like client.models.list() to catch authentication failures early.

Using temperature=0 does not guarantee deterministic outputs from the OpenAI API even with the same seed. Floating-point non-determinism in GPU kernels and load-balanced routing across different server hardware can produce different token selections even at temperature=0. The system_fingerprint field indicates which backend configuration generated the response; identical fingerprints are more likely to produce identical outputs, but cross-machine floating point differences persist. For applications requiring byte-identical reproducibility (such as cryptographic applications or deterministic audit logs), caching must be used at the application layer rather than relying on API-level determinism.

OpenAI's GPT-4 class models will refuse to continue generating after hitting a content filter mid-response, returning a truncated response with finish_reason=content_filter. Applications that check only for finish_reason=stop to detect complete responses will treat these truncated filtered responses as complete, potentially surfacing incomplete and misleading information to users. This is particularly problematic for long-form content generation where a filter trigger mid-essay produces an abruptly incomplete article. Always check finish_reason and handle content_filter as an error condition requiring user notification or prompt reformulation.

The OpenAI Responses API previous_response_id parameter for conversation continuity silently creates orphaned conversation threads when an error occurs mid-conversation. If a response returns a 500 error, no response_id is generated, and the next turn has no valid previous_response_id to chain to. Applications that do not handle missing response_ids fall back to starting a new conversation, losing conversation history. Maintain a separate client-side message history as a fallback, and when previous_response_id is unavailable (due to error), reconstruct the conversation from the client-side history by passing messages explicitly instead.

The OpenAI Assistants API run polling pattern using runs.retrieve() in a loop has a problematic default behavior: there is no built-in exponential backoff between polls, and naive polling loops hitting the retrieve endpoint every 100ms can themselves trigger rate limits. The SDK's runs.poll() helper method and runs.create_and_poll() implement proper polling with backoff, but many developers implement manual polling loops that create unnecessary load. Additionally, runs that fail due to server errors return status=failed with a last_error field; code that only polls until status != in_progress misses this failed state and hangs indefinitely on server errors.

Applications that include the entire OpenAI response object in error logging inadvertently log sensitive user data. The response object includes the full messages array (containing user input), the model-generated content, and metadata. When these responses are written to centralized logging systems, user conversations become accessible to log readers who should not see them. Implement a log sanitizer that extracts only non-sensitive fields (request_id, model, token counts, finish_reason, latency) for operational logs, storing the full conversation only in a separate encrypted audit log with appropriate access controls.

The OpenAI SDK's built-in request logging (enabled by setting log=debug in the OpenAI constructor or OPENAI_LOG=debug environment variable) logs complete request and response bodies to stderr, including user messages, system prompts, and API keys. Enabling debug logging in production accidentally exposes sensitive data in application logs and stderr output. Debug logging should only be enabled in development environments, and production log levels should be set to error or warning. When debugging production issues, use the request_id from response headers to retrieve specific requests through the OpenAI support portal without enabling full request logging.

Constructing system prompts using f-strings with user-provided values is a critical security vulnerability enabling prompt injection attacks. If a user's input contains instruction-override patterns like ignore all previous instructions and users can control any part of the system prompt, they can redirect the model to produce harmful or unauthorized outputs. All user-provided values must be isolated from the instruction layer: pass user content in separate user messages (never in the system message), wrap user-provided context in XML-style delimiters such as user_context tags, and explicitly instruct the model to treat content within those tags as data to analyze rather than instructions to follow.

The OpenAI SDK's timeout parameter applies to the entire HTTP request lifecycle including connection establishment, request sending, and response receiving. For very large requests (uploading 25MB audio files via the Whisper API), the default timeout of 600 seconds may be insufficient on slow networks. Additionally, increasing timeout also increases the maximum time an application thread blocks waiting for a response, which multiplies resource consumption for concurrent requests. Configure separate timeout values for different endpoint types: shorter timeouts (30s) for chat completions that should respond quickly, longer timeouts (300s) for file uploads and batch processing endpoints.

The OpenAI SDK does not validate the messages array before sending the request. Common validation failures that only surface as 400 API errors include: empty content strings (content=''), messages with both content and a function_call in the same message (mutually exclusive in older API versions), tool_call_id in a tool message that doesn't match any tool_call_id in the preceding assistant message, and base64 image data URLs with incorrect MIME type prefixes. Implementing a client-side validation function that checks these conditions before API calls dramatically reduces debugging time by surfacing these errors locally with descriptive messages rather than as generic 400 errors.

The OpenAI API does not maintain any state between API calls by default. Applications that rely on the model to remember previous conversations across separate API sessions (without including the history in messages) find the model has no memory of prior interactions. This is a fundamental design choice—each API call is stateless. The Assistants API threads provide server-managed history but within a single thread, not across threads. Applications expecting cross-session memory without implementing it explicitly will deliver a poor user experience where the model repeatedly asks for already-provided information in new sessions.

The OpenAI API's context window is shared between input tokens (prompt) and output tokens (completion). When max_tokens is set too high relative to the prompt length, the model may generate unnecessarily long responses to fill the allocated space, increasing cost and reducing precision. Conversely, setting max_tokens too low truncates responses. The optimal max_tokens is 2-3x the expected output length for the task. For extraction tasks, set max_tokens just above the expected extraction size. For open-ended generation, use a reasonable upper bound (500-2000 tokens). Never set max_tokens to the model's full context window minus the prompt; this wastes money on unused allocation.

Including long PDF text extracted via OCR directly in OpenAI API prompts frequently causes quality degradation due to OCR noise. Scanned document OCR output contains character substitutions (O vs 0, l vs 1), missing spaces, merged words, and broken line breaks that are invisible when reading but confuse the model's tokenization. The model attempts to interpret garbage OCR artifacts as meaningful content, producing incorrect answers for numerically precise content (financial figures, legal citations). Pre-processing OCR output with a text normalization pass (fixing common OCR errors, standardizing spacing) significantly improves model accuracy on OCR-processed documents.

The OpenAI SDK's automatic retry mechanism does not retry on connection timeout errors (httpx.ConnectTimeout) by default, only on HTTP-level errors (429, 5xx). Applications running in environments with unstable network connectivity experience unhandled ConnectTimeout exceptions that propagate to the caller. Wrapping API calls in a tenacity retry decorator that handles both ConnectTimeout and RateLimitError provides uniform retry coverage. Setting a connection_timeout separate from the read_timeout in the httpx client (e.g., 10s for connection, 300s for read) better handles the different failure modes of network unavailability vs slow API response.

Applications that parse the model response assuming a specific response length or format fail when OpenAI updates the default model behavior. For example, GPT-4o often generates more verbose responses than GPT-4 for the same prompt, and introducing explicit length instructions (respond in 2-3 sentences) in the system prompt is necessary to maintain response length consistency across model updates. Applications that set character limits in post-processing by truncating the response string may cut responses mid-sentence. Always use max_tokens for length control rather than string truncation, and include explicit length guidance in the prompt for tasks where response format matters.

The OpenAI SDK does not provide built-in response caching across process restarts. When an application restarts after a crash, all in-memory cache entries are lost, causing a cold start where all requests hit the API again. For applications with high traffic, this cold start can trigger rate limit errors as all concurrent requests simultaneously miss cache. Persisting the response cache to Redis or a database before shutdown and warming it up at startup prevents cold start rate limit spikes. Implementing a write-through cache (write to both memory and Redis on every cache miss) ensures cache persistence without a separate warm-up process.

The OpenAI SDK raises an error when the messages array contains a system message that is not the first message. Applications that dynamically build message histories and insert the system message at a position other than index 0 receive a 400 error with an unintuitive message about invalid message ordering. This occurs when a previous system message is removed and re-added at the current position (not index 0), or when conversation history reconstruction fails to maintain the system message at position 0. Always validate that messages[0] has role=system before every API call in dynamic message history management.

Using the same OpenAI API key for both development and production environments creates multiple risks: development experiments can exhaust production rate limits, accidental high-cost development calls impact production budgets, and development API calls appear in production audit logs. Organizations that do not separate dev/staging/production API keys experience production rate limit exhaustion from developer testing and are unable to audit production usage independently. Create separate projects with separate API keys and spend limits for each environment. Set strict spend limits on development keys to prevent runaway experimental costs from impacting production budgets.

Applications that rely on the model's training cutoff date for current information produce hallucinated up-to-date facts. GPT-4o's training data has a knowledge cutoff date, and the model will confidently generate plausible-sounding but incorrect information about post-cutoff events when asked. The model sometimes generates dates that are after its training cutoff as if it has access to that information. Applications handling time-sensitive queries (current prices, recent events, regulatory changes) must either use web search integration or explicitly instruct the model to state when information may be outdated. Never rely on the model's training data for facts that change over time.

The OpenAI API does not support batch embedding with more than 2048 inputs per request. Applications that attempt to embed thousands of texts in a single API call receive a 400 error. More subtly, very long text inputs in a batch cause the entire batch to fail if any single input exceeds the token limit (8191 tokens for text-embedding-3 models). A single oversized text in a batch of 1000 texts causes all 1000 to fail. Implement pre-validation that checks each text's token count before batching, filters out overlong texts with a warning, and splits the valid texts into batches of maximum 2000 to provide a safety margin below the 2048 limit.

The OpenAI SDK's streaming helper with_streaming_response does not automatically reconnect on network interruptions during a stream. If the connection drops mid-stream, an httpx.ReadError is raised and the partial response is lost. Applications must implement their own reconnection logic: catch the ReadError, note the last received token position, and restart the stream from scratch (the API does not support resumable streams). For long-generation tasks critical enough to require resume, implement a checkpoint pattern that periodically saves accumulated response text to a database, allowing recovery to the last checkpoint rather than starting from the beginning.

The OpenAI Assistants API charges storage fees for Vector Store objects that continue to accrue even when the assistant is inactive or deleted if the vector store is not explicitly deleted. Many developers delete the assistant but forget to delete the associated vector store, leading to ongoing storage charges for unused data. Implement an assistant lifecycle manager that: on assistant deletion, lists all vector stores attached to the assistant, deletes each vector store, then deletes the assistant. The SDK provides client.beta.vector_stores.delete(vs_id) for vector store deletion. Automate cleanup in the application's deprovisioning workflow rather than relying on manual dashboard cleanup.

Using the OpenAI SDK in Python Lambda functions (AWS Lambda) without properly configuring the httpx connection pool causes each Lambda invocation to create a new connection to OpenAI's API endpoints, eliminating connection reuse benefits. Lambda's execution environment freezes between invocations, and the HTTP connection pool is not preserved across invocations. Setting max_connections=1 in the httpx client reduces overhead from abandoned connection pool slots. For high-throughput Lambda functions, consider using Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to keep instances warm and maintain connection pools, reducing per-invocation latency from 100ms to under 20ms for the connection establishment phase.

The OpenAI Assistants API requires polling the run status to detect completion, and the maximum safe polling interval depends on expected completion time. Polling too frequently (every 100ms) consumes rate limit quota on the assistants run retrieve endpoint. Polling too infrequently (every 30 seconds) adds unnecessary latency for fast tasks. The SDK's built-in poll_interval_ms parameter in runs.poll() defaults to 500ms, which is reasonable. However, custom polling loops without exponential backoff create unnecessary load during server slowdowns when run completion takes longer than expected. Always use the SDK's built-in polling helpers rather than implementing custom polling loops.

The OpenAI API's n parameter and stream=True are mutually exclusive. Attempting to use both simultaneously returns a 400 error stating stream is not supported for n > 1. Applications implementing real-time streaming of multiple response alternatives must make n separate streaming requests in parallel rather than a single request with both parameters. This increases API overhead proportionally to n but is the only supported approach. When designing features requiring both parallelism and streaming (like streaming a best-of-5 response where the best is selected as it generates), implement the parallel streaming pattern with early cancellation when one stream clearly outperforms others.

The OpenAI SDK's request body is not validated before sending, and deeply nested invalid structures in tool definitions cause confusing 400 errors. A common issue is defining a function parameter with both type and enum where the enum values do not match the type, or nesting objects more than 5 levels deep. OpenAI's JSON Schema validation for tool definitions is stricter than general JSON Schema and has additional constraints beyond the spec. Always validate tool definitions using OpenAI's documented schema format before deployment, and test all tool definitions with the models they will be used with as different models have subtly different schema support levels.

Applications that use the OpenAI SDK in test suites without mocking the API incur real API costs for every test run and introduce test flakiness due to network failures and model output variability. Even small test suites with 50 tests can cost tens of dollars monthly in API calls. The correct approach is to mock OpenAI responses in unit tests using unittest.mock.patch or the respx library for httpx mocking, testing the application logic rather than the API behavior. Integration tests that do call the real API should be clearly marked, run infrequently, and use a dedicated test project with strict spend limits to prevent runaway costs from test failures that cause retries.

The OpenAI API has a per-request input token limit that varies by model (128k for GPT-4o, 200k for o1). When the sum of system prompt + conversation history + retrieved context + user message approaches this limit, the API returns a 400 error with context_length_exceeded. Applications using sliding window context management based on message count (rather than token count) may not notice when a single large message causes the window to exceed the limit even after recent messages are dropped. Token-count-based context management using tiktoken before every API call is the only reliable approach, as message count is not proportional to token count.

The OpenAI SDK's AsyncOpenAI client is not safe to use across multiple asyncio event loops. Creating the client in one event loop and using it in another (which can happen with improper pytest-asyncio configuration or when mixing sync and async test runners) raises RuntimeError: Session is closed or RuntimeError: Event loop is closed. Each AsyncOpenAI client instance is bound to the event loop that was active when it was created. For testing, create a fresh AsyncOpenAI instance in each test function or use the session scope with a properly configured event loop fixture. Never share async clients across event loop boundaries.

The OpenAI fine-tuning API does not support evaluating fine-tuned models against a test set as part of the fine-tuning job. The validation_file parameter provides only loss metrics on the validation set during training, not task-specific quality metrics. Organizations that deploy fine-tuned models based solely on validation loss without external evaluation often find that lower validation loss does not correlate with better task performance for their specific use case. Always run a separate evaluation on a held-out test set using task-specific metrics (accuracy, F1, BLEU, human preference) before promoting a fine-tuned model to production.

The OpenAI Assistants API run objects do not support cancellation of in-progress runs in a reliable way. Calling runs.cancel(run_id) sends a cancellation request but the run may complete before the cancellation is processed, returning status=completed rather than cancelled. Applications that wait for a specific cancelled status to confirm user-initiated stops may wait indefinitely. The cancel API returns the run object immediately with status=cancelling, but the final transition to cancelled may take 10-30 seconds. Implement a timeout on the cancel-and-wait pattern; if the run completes before cancellation, treat the completed result as valid rather than discarding it.

Using OpenAI API keys in client-side JavaScript applications (browser, mobile) directly exposes the API key to anyone who inspects network traffic. The API key must never be used client-side; all OpenAI API calls must be proxied through a server backend that handles authentication, rate limiting, and usage tracking per user. Common mistakes include embedding the API key in React or mobile app builds using environment variables that are bundled into the client code, or calling the API from edge functions that log request parameters containing the API key. Implement a thin backend proxy that validates user authentication before forwarding requests to OpenAI, hiding the API key entirely from client-side code.

The OpenAI Assistants API automatically selects chunking parameters for file_search based on file type and size. For technical documentation with very dense information, the default chunk size may produce chunks that split important tables or code examples, degrading retrieval quality. The max_chunk_size_tokens parameter allows specifying a custom chunk size when creating a vector store. Setting it too small (under 200 tokens) produces too-granular chunks that lack context; setting it too large (over 1000 tokens) reduces search precision. The default 800 tokens works for general text but technical documentation often benefits from smaller 400-600 token chunks that keep related information together.

Using print() statements to debug OpenAI API responses during development frequently leads to accidentally logging API keys and user data to stdout, which gets captured by logging aggregators in production. The API key appears in the repr() of the OpenAI client object, and user messages appear in the messages list repr(). Replace all debug print() statements with structured logging at DEBUG level before deployment, ensuring production log level is INFO or higher. The SDK's own debug logging (set via OPENAI_LOG=debug) is separate from application debug prints and must also be disabled in production.

Eloquent N+1 is usually a boundary design failure, not just a missing with call. When templates and serializers pull relations ad hoc, query planning happens too late. The durable fix is to decide data shape at the application boundary and load that graph explicitly.

Queue workers leak memory when code assumes request-lifetime cleanup still exists in a long-running process. Static caches, large collections, and native resources accumulate quietly because the worker keeps serving jobs. The hidden rule is to code workers like daemons: release resources, restart intentionally, and watch memory over time.

Eloquent model events (creating, updating, deleting) do not fire when using bulk operations like Model::insert(), update(), or delete() on the query builder. The hidden failure is that observers and event listeners registered to enforce business invariants—audit logging, cache invalidation, index synchronization—are silently skipped for bulk writes. The correct pattern is to treat query builder bulk operations as a bypass of the domain layer, use them only for performance-critical paths, and document explicitly which lifecycle hooks are not firing.

Laravel's job batching silently swallows failed jobs unless a catch callback is registered on the batch. The default behavior marks a job as failed but the batch continues, and the batch's finished callback fires even when some jobs errored. Teams discover this by noticing that batch->pendingJobs reaches zero and finished fires while data is incomplete. The correct pattern is to register explicit catch handlers on the batch, decide whether allowFailures should be true or false for the workload, and assert batch failure state in tests.

PHP's sessions and static properties persist across requests in Laravel Octane's long-running worker model, but they do not in traditional PHP-FPM. The hidden failure is porting a Laravel app to Octane without auditing for request-scope assumptions: singletons that capture request data, static caches that fill across users, or services that assume a fresh bootstrap. Octane's documentation lists known incompatible patterns but teams miss edge cases in their own code. Testing with octane:start locally before shipping to Octane-based hosting is mandatory.

Laravel's Horizon dashboard shows queue metrics based on Redis keys that expire. When Horizon is stopped for an extended period and restarted, historical throughput charts reset and peak queue depth records are lost. The deeper failure is teams using Horizon's dashboard as the sole monitoring surface: it is designed for operational visibility, not alerting. Critical queue health indicators—failed job rate, queue depth above threshold, dead-letter backlog—need external alerting (Prometheus, Datadog, or a simple cron check) that operates independent of Horizon's process state.

Python module import executes top-level code immediately. Code at module scope runs on import, not on call. This means: (1) expensive initialization at module level slows startup for all importers; (2) database connections opened at module level fail in test environments without DB; (3) circular imports cause AttributeError at runtime, not at definition time. Common anti-pattern: putting 'db = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)' at module level in models.py.

Python evaluates default argument values ONCE at function definition time, not on each call. def f(items=[]) creates ONE list shared across all calls — a notorious bug source. Correct pattern: def f(items=None): items = items or []. Extraction signal: any function with list, dict, or set as a default argument value is likely buggy. This also applies to class-level mutable attributes shared across instances.
