Agent scaffolding that doesn't handle partial tool_use blocks fails on streaming responses. When using the Anthropic streaming API, tool use blocks are streamed as partial JSON. Processing them requires accumulating 'input_json_delta' events until the block is complete. A common scaffolding bug: trying to parse tool input JSON from the first 'input_json_delta' event before the full JSON is received. Correct approach: accumulate all 'input_json_delta.partial_json' strings for a given 'index', then parse the complete JSON when the stream ends. Test with large tool inputs that span multiple chunks. The error manifests as JSONDecodeError on partial JSON strings.

Skills that provide inconsistent error responses across different error conditions force orchestrators to implement complex error handling logic. If a validation error returns '{"error": "invalid_input"}' but a rate limit returns 'Too Many Requests' as plain text and a timeout returns HTTP 504 with empty body — the orchestrator must handle three completely different error formats. Standardize on one error response format for all skill errors: '{"error_code": "RATE_LIMITED", "message": "...", "retry_after": 60, "request_id": "uuid"}'. Use HTTP status codes consistently but don't rely on them alone — include the error_code in the body. Never return plain text for error responses from skills that are consumed by machines (not humans directly).

Skill systems that don't account for model deprecation create sudden production failures. LLM providers deprecate models on set timelines — claude-3-opus-20240229 will eventually be unavailable. A skill hardcoding a deprecated model name will start returning API errors on the deprecation date with no warning. Proactive model lifecycle management: subscribe to LLM provider deprecation announcements; maintain an internal 'approved models' list with deprecation dates; CI check: reject skills using models within 6 months of deprecation; automated alerts: when a model in use is within 90 days of deprecation, create an upgrade ticket; plan migration 3 months in advance. Never let model deprecation be a surprise.

Agentic systems that expose too many tools to the LLM at once reduce task performance and increase hallucination risk. Research shows LLM tool selection accuracy degrades significantly beyond 20-30 tools. With 100+ tools available, the LLM frequently: selects the wrong tool, invents tool names that don't exist, confuses similar tool purposes. Mitigation: implement dynamic tool loading — start with 5-10 core tools, load additional tools only when the task type requires them. Use a 'tool discovery' skill: 'find me tools relevant to this task'. Group tools by domain (search tools, write tools, data tools) and load groups as needed. Test tool selection accuracy as a key metric when adding new tools to a system.

Retain cycles survive code review because ARC manages counts, not ownership intent. The hidden failure is long-lived closures held by views, coordinators, or tasks that also capture self strongly. weak is not the goal by itself; the real goal is to make the ownership graph obvious enough that lifetimes can be explained.

UI updates must happen on the main thread not because Apple is arbitrary, but because UIKit and much of SwiftUI assume a serialized view state model. The hidden failure is that async code makes thread hops implicit, so background work feels safe until rendering bugs appear nondeterministically. MainActor is as much a documentation tool as a scheduler.

SwiftUI's @State and @StateObject are not the same thing even though both create view-local state. @State is for value types managed by the view; @StateObject is for reference types whose lifetime is tied to the view's identity. The hidden failure is initializing an ObservableObject inline inside a view body with @ObservedObject—SwiftUI may recreate the view struct multiple times per render cycle, and each recreation creates a new instance, destroying accumulated state. Using @StateObject tells SwiftUI to initialize exactly once per view identity.

Task cancellation in Swift structured concurrency is cooperative: the task's isCancelled flag is set, but execution continues until the code checks it or calls a cancellation-sensitive API. The hidden failure is assuming that cancelling a Task automatically stops an ongoing URLSession download or a database write—it does not. The correct pattern is to check Task.isCancelled at natural suspension points and call cancellationPoint() for CPU-bound loops. Callers that cancel tasks expecting immediate resource release will be disappointed without explicit cooperative checking.

Xcode's App Store Connect submission rejects builds that use private APIs, contain deprecated entitlements, or include frameworks with incorrect privacy manifest declarations. The hidden failure mode is that these rejections arrive after days of review, not at submission time. Privacy manifests (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) became required in 2024 for apps using certain APIs; missing entries cause automated rejection. The discipline is to run the privacy report locally (Product > Archive > Validate) before submitting and to audit third-party SDK documentation for SDK Privacy Manifests.

SwiftUI previews break at compile time for any view that has a required environment object not provided in the preview provider. The hidden failure is that developers skip previews entirely rather than providing lightweight preview-specific implementations of dependencies. This erodes preview coverage silently until previews are so rarely used that they are removed. The correct pattern is to use protocol-backed previews: inject mock implementations of all dependencies in the preview, keeping preview data in a well-named PreviewData file so the setup cost stays low.

WKWebView's decidePolicyFor navigationAction delegate method must call the completion handler on the main thread, and it must be called exactly once. The hidden failure is async decision logic that calls the handler from a background queue or, worse, in a DispatchQueue.main.async nested inside a delay, calling it twice. Both produce a crash at the native layer that appears unrelated to the decision handler. The correct pattern is to make the policy decision synchronously or, if async evaluation is needed, call .allow provisionally and cancel the navigation from a subsequent delegate callback.

Using 'any' defeats TypeScript's type system silently. Variables typed as 'any' spread type unsafety to every consumer: assignments from 'any' widen the receiving variable's type, function calls on 'any' return 'any', and errors that would be caught at compile time are deferred to runtime. Common hotspots are JSON.parse() (returns 'any'), untyped third-party library imports, and catch clause bindings (pre-TS 4.0 default). Prefer 'unknown' with explicit narrowing, or 'as const' for literal JSON.

In Node.js, an unhandled promise rejection (a rejected Promise with no .catch() or try/catch in an async context) emits an 'unhandledRejection' event. Since Node 15, this terminates the process with a non-zero exit code by default. The silent failure mode in older versions caused production services to silently discard errors. Always attach a top-level error handler, use process.on('unhandledRejection', ...) as a last-resort safety net, and enable TypeScript's 'strict' mode together with eslint's no-floating-promises rule.

ESM/CJS interop produces three recurring failure modes: (1) require() of an ESM-only package throws ERR_REQUIRE_ESM — fix by dynamic import() or switch to ESM; (2) Named exports from CJS re-exported through an ESM wrapper may disappear (only 'default' is guaranteed) — fix by accessing module.exports members explicitly; (3) tsconfig 'module' and 'moduleResolution' settings that mismatch the runtime loader (e.g., 'bundler' moduleResolution used with raw Node.js) cause tsc to accept code that Node rejects. Always align tsconfig, package.json 'type', and file extensions.

Relying on ts-node or tsx directly in production is a common but dangerous shortcut. These tools transpile TypeScript on-the-fly at runtime, adding cold-start latency (200-500ms for moderate projects), preventing ahead-of-time type errors from being caught in CI, and making container images depend on dev dependencies. The correct production pattern is to compile with tsc (or esbuild/swc for speed) as part of the build step, run the emitted JavaScript, and ensure CI runs tsc --noEmit to catch type errors separately. Using ts-node in production also means you lose source map accuracy unless tsconfig's sourceMap and inlineSources are configured, making production stack traces hard to read.

Memory leaks in long-running Node.js services most commonly originate from four sources: (1) EventEmitter listeners added repeatedly without removal — always call removeListener() or use once() for one-time subscriptions, and set emitter.setMaxListeners() appropriately; (2) global Maps/Sets used as in-process caches that grow unboundedly — use a library with TTL eviction (lru-cache); (3) closures in timers/intervals capturing large objects — always clearInterval() on teardown; (4) stream errors without error handlers causing internal buffers to be retained. Diagnosis: use process.memoryUsage() snapshots over time, heapdump via v8.writeHeapSnapshot(), or the --inspect flag with Chrome DevTools Heap Snapshots to identify object retention trees.

TypeScript path aliases configured in tsconfig.json (compilerOptions.paths) are a compile-time feature only — tsc resolves them during type checking but does NOT rewrite import paths in emitted JavaScript. This means `import { x } from '@app/utils'` compiles without error but fails at runtime with MODULE_NOT_FOUND. The fix depends on your build tool: for tsc-only builds use tsconfig-paths with a register hook or tsc-alias as a post-process step; for esbuild/Rollup/webpack, configure the same path aliases in the bundler config separately. Monorepo setups using project references sidestep this entirely by using workspace packages with proper package.json exports instead of path aliases.

TypeScript's `as` type assertion bypasses type safety and is a common source of runtime errors. `const user = json as User` tells TypeScript 'trust me, this is a User' without any actual validation. If the JSON doesn't match User's shape, TypeScript is silent but runtime code will fail at property access. Safe alternatives: (1) Zod — `UserSchema.parse(json)` throws if validation fails; (2) io-ts — functional type-safe decoding; (3) typebox — JSON Schema + TypeScript types in sync. The double-cast escape hatch `x as unknown as TargetType` bypasses all type checking — only use when you KNOW the types are compatible and you can't prove it to TypeScript. UNSAID: `as const` is NOT a type assertion — it widens literal inference: `['a', 'b'] as const` gives type `readonly ['a', 'b']`, not `string[]`. This is safe and valuable for preventing literal widening.

TypeScript's strict null checks with optional chaining pitfalls: `obj?.prop?.method()` returns undefined if any link in the chain is null/undefined — but TypeScript types the result as `T | undefined`. Code that ignores the undefined case causes runtime errors. Common pattern: `const name = user?.profile?.name.toLowerCase()` — if name is undefined, `.toLowerCase()` throws. Fix: `user?.profile?.name?.toLowerCase()`. The non-null assertion operator `!` (`user!.profile`) suppresses null/undefined from the type without any runtime check — it's a silent time bomb when the assumption is wrong. UNSAID: optional chaining short-circuits at the first null/undefined — `user?.profile?.settings.theme` will throw if settings exists but theme is undefined on a non-optional access. The fix is `user?.profile?.settings?.theme`. Nullish coalescing `??` is distinct from `||`: `0 ?? 'default'` gives `0`; `0 || 'default'` gives `'default'` — use `??` when 0, false, and '' are valid values.

Node.js memory leaks from EventEmitter: `EventEmitter.on('event', handler)` adds a listener without removing it. In Node.js, EventEmitters warn when more than 10 listeners are added to a single event (MaxListenersExceededWarning) — often a signal of a leak. Common patterns: (1) `server.on('request', ...)` in a loop creates a new listener each iteration; (2) `socket.on('data', ...)` where sockets are created per-connection but listeners are added to a shared emitter; (3) adding listeners in class constructors without cleanup in destructors. Fix: use `once()` for one-time listeners, or store the reference and call `removeListener(event, handler)` in cleanup. `emitter.setMaxListeners(0)` disables the warning — but this hides a real problem. UNSAID: `AbortController` and AbortSignal are the modern pattern for cancellable operations — `signal.addEventListener('abort', cleanup)` registers cleanup that runs when the signal is aborted. This is the Node.js 20+ recommended pattern over manual EventEmitter cleanup.

Node.js `process.env` typing and validation: all `process.env` values are `string | undefined` — accessing `process.env.PORT` gives `string | undefined`, not `number`. Common failures: (1) `parseInt(process.env.PORT)` when PORT is undefined returns NaN — no error; (2) `process.env.API_KEY.toLowerCase()` throws if API_KEY is not set; (3) using environment variables without validation at startup means misconfiguration errors surface at runtime deep in request processing. Best practice: validate all required environment variables at startup with a schema validator. Pattern with Zod: `const Env = z.object({ DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(), PORT: z.coerce.number().default(3000) }); export const env = Env.parse(process.env)`. UNSAID: `z.coerce.number()` converts `string | undefined` to number with validation — crucial for numeric env vars. In TypeScript projects, `declare global { namespace NodeJS { interface ProcessEnv { MY_VAR: string } } }` adds type declarations for known env vars, preventing accidental access of undeclared ones.

TypeScript's `enum` vs const enum vs union type tradeoffs: numeric enums (`enum Direction { Up, Down }`) compile to a bidirectional object (runtime overhead) and are hard to type-check narrowly — `Direction.Up === 0` (unsafe). String enums (`enum Status { Active = 'ACTIVE' }`) are safer but still add runtime object overhead. `const enum` is inlined at compile time (no runtime object), but breaks when the enum is in a .d.ts declaration file imported from other packages (values aren't available). Modern recommendation: use union types (`type Status = 'ACTIVE' | 'INACTIVE'`) or `as const` objects (`const STATUS = { ACTIVE: 'ACTIVE', INACTIVE: 'INACTIVE' } as const; type Status = typeof STATUS[keyof typeof STATUS]`) — both are tree-shakeable, don't add runtime overhead, and work across module boundaries. UNSAID: `const enum` with `isolatedModules: true` (used by Babel, esbuild, SWC) raises an error — const enum is incompatible with transpiler-based builds.

TypeScript `strictPropertyInitialization` and class field initialization: with `strict: true`, TypeScript requires all class properties to be initialized in the constructor or have a definite assignment assertion (`!`). Using `!` (e.g., `name!: string`) tells TypeScript 'I guarantee this will be initialized' but TypeScript doesn't verify the guarantee — accessing it before assignment gives undefined. Angular's `@Input() name!: string` is a common example. Proper patterns: (1) initialize in constructor; (2) use `name?: string` (optional, possibly undefined); (3) use `name: string | undefined` (explicit undefined type). Framework-specific: `definiteAssignmentAssertion` is sometimes required for DI-initialized properties in NestJS/Angular — but it's a footgun; prefer constructor injection. UNSAID: `declare name: string` in a TypeScript class tells the compiler the property exists without emitting any initialization — used when another mechanism (e.g., Object.defineProperty, decorators) handles initialization.

Node.js `net.Socket` and TCP keep-alive: by default, TCP connections have no application-level keep-alive — an idle connection can be silently dropped by NAT gateways, firewalls, or load balancers (typically after 30-900 seconds). `socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30000)` enables TCP keep-alive with 30s probe interval. HTTP keep-alive (Connection: keep-alive) reuses TCP connections for multiple requests — enabled by default in Node.js http.Agent. Timeout settings: `socket.setTimeout(ms)` triggers a 'timeout' event but does NOT close the socket — you must call `socket.destroy()` in the timeout handler. UNSAID: `http.request()` without an explicit `agent` uses the global agent with `maxSockets: Infinity` — in high-concurrency scenarios, this creates too many connections and exhausts OS file descriptors. Set `keepAlive: true, keepAliveMsecs: 30000` on the agent for connection reuse. Node.js 17+ uses `keepAlive: true` by default in the global agent.

Node.js `setImmediate` vs `process.nextTick` vs `Promise.resolve().then()` ordering: `process.nextTick(fn)` runs BEFORE any I/O events, before the next event loop iteration — it's not technically part of the event loop. `setImmediate(fn)` runs in the 'check' phase, after I/O callbacks. `Promise.resolve().then(fn)` runs in the microtask queue, which drains AFTER process.nextTick and before the next I/O callback. Ordering: `nextTick callbacks → Promise microtasks → setImmediate → I/O callbacks → timers`. UNSAID: `process.nextTick` can starve I/O callbacks if called recursively — always-queuing nextTick causes the event loop to never advance to I/O. `setImmediate` in recursive use DOES allow I/O interleaving. The `--max-old-space-size` flag limits V8 heap size; Node.js 20+ has `--heap-prof` for heap profiling. `setTimeout(fn, 0)` and `setImmediate` have nondeterministic ordering relative to each other when called from the main module (outside any I/O callback).

TypeScript's circular dependencies and their effects: circular module imports in TypeScript/Node.js can cause undefined values at module load time. `module A imports B; B imports A` — when A is being loaded, B is loaded, B tries to access A's exports, but A's exports aren't fully populated yet (still loading) — result: some exports are undefined. Node.js handles circular requires by returning whatever has been exported SO FAR from the partially-loaded module — the first module to be required in a cycle gets a partial view of the second. Fix: (1) extract shared code to a third module; (2) use lazy requires (import inside a function); (3) rethink the dependency graph. UNSAID: TypeScript type-only circular imports (`import type { Foo } from './foo'`) are erased at compile time and do NOT cause runtime circular dependency issues — only value imports create the circular dependency problem. `madge --circular .` detects circular dependencies in a project.

TypeScript's `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` compiler option: when enabled, accessing array elements by index or object string-index signature returns `T | undefined` instead of `T`. `arr[0]` returns `string | undefined` even when arr: string[]. This catches many common off-by-one errors and undefined access bugs but requires explicit null checks everywhere. Not enabled by `strict: true` — must be enabled separately. The `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` option requires that optional properties typed `T | undefined` must be explicitly written as `T | undefined`, not just `T?`. UNSAID: enabling noUncheckedIndexedAccess in an existing codebase produces thousands of type errors — migrate gradually with @ts-expect-error or by narrowing index access patterns. For tuple types with known lengths, TypeScript already tracks element types precisely without this flag — the flag only affects dynamic index access.

TypeScript `as const` versus `Object.freeze()`: `as const` is compile-time only — it makes TypeScript narrow literal types and makes properties readonly IN THE TYPE SYSTEM. At runtime, the object is still mutable. `Object.freeze(obj)` is runtime-only — it prevents property mutations at runtime (silently in non-strict mode, throws in strict mode) but TypeScript doesn't automatically infer readonly from freeze (needs explicit typing). For true compile-time AND runtime immutability, use both: `const config = Object.freeze({ host: 'localhost' } as const)`. Deep freeze is not provided by stdlib — nested objects are still mutable unless each nested object is also frozen. UNSAID: `Readonly<T>` and `as const` in TypeScript prevent reassignment through that specific typed reference, but the underlying object can still be mutated through another mutable reference — TypeScript's readonly is not Java's `final` on the heap. Truly deep immutability requires a deep clone + freeze or a library like `immer`.

Node.js package.json `exports` field and module resolution: the `exports` field (Node 12+) restricts which files in a package can be imported and enables conditional exports for different environments. `'exports': { '.': { 'import': './dist/index.mjs', 'require': './dist/index.cjs' } }` provides different entry points for ESM and CJS consumers. Deep imports like `import { util } from 'package/src/util'` are blocked when `exports` doesn't include that path — causes `ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED`. This breaks old code that imported internal package paths. UNSAID: `exports` completely overrides `main` when present — `main` is only a fallback for Node < 12 and very old bundlers. `imports` field (Node 12+) is for self-referential imports: `#utils` maps to `./src/utils.js` — enables path aliasing within a package. `peerDependenciesMeta: { 'packageName': { optional: true } }` marks peer dependencies as optional (won't warn if not installed).

Node.js `Buffer` and security: `Buffer.allocUnsafe(n)` allocates memory WITHOUT zeroing it — the buffer contains random heap data that may include previous request data (passwords, tokens, private keys). Always use `Buffer.alloc(n)` for zero-initialized buffers, or `Buffer.from(data)` when initializing from data. `Buffer.concat([buf1, buf2])` copies into a new buffer. `Buffer.compare(a, b)` is safe for ordering but NOT for secret comparison (timing attack vulnerable) — use `crypto.timingSafeEqual(a, b)`. `buf.toString('base64')` vs `buf.toString('base64url')` — base64url is URL-safe (replaces + with -, / with _). UNSAID: Buffers are Uint8Array subclasses — modern Node.js code should prefer TypedArrays (Uint8Array, ArrayBuffer) for binary data. Buffers have additional Node.js-specific methods. `buf.readUInt32BE(offset)` and similar methods for structured binary data reading.

Node.js `path` module and cross-platform path handling: `path.join()` uses the platform separator (`\` on Windows, `/` on Unix) — code using `path.join()` is cross-platform. `path.resolve()` returns an absolute path from the process CWD. NEVER manually concatenate paths with `/` or `\` — breaks on the opposite platform. `__dirname` is not available in ES modules — use `import.meta.url` instead: `const __dirname = new URL('.', import.meta.url).pathname`. On Windows, `import.meta.url` starts with `file:///C:/...` — use `fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)` for the correct path. `path.posix` and `path.win32` access platform-specific implementations explicitly. UNSAID: URL-style paths in `import()` always use forward slashes regardless of OS. `path.normalize('a//b/../c')` cleans up double slashes and `..` components. Never trust user-supplied paths without `path.normalize()` + bounds checking — `../../../etc/passwd` path traversal is the most common exploit in file-serving code.

TypeScript's `prototype` chain and class transpilation: TypeScript classes transpile to ES5 prototype-based inheritance when targeting older environments. `class Child extends Parent {}` emits `__extends(Child, Parent)` in ES5 output — using libraries that check `instanceof` may behave differently with transpiled classes. With ES2015+ targets, TypeScript emits native `class` syntax. Mixins in TypeScript: `type Constructor<T = {}> = new (...args: any[]) => T; function Serializable<TBase extends Constructor>(Base: TBase) { return class extends Base { serialize() { ... } } }` — the mixin factory pattern. UNSAID: `instanceof` works with transpiled classes in the same module but can fail across iframe boundaries (different global contexts) or with multiple copies of the same library (e.g., two instances of lodash in the same bundle). TypeScript's `class fields` emit (with `useDefineForClassFields: true`) changes property assignment order — properties defined with `class { x = value }` now use `Object.defineProperty` instead of assignment, potentially breaking frameworks that use prototype property interception.

Node.js `require.cache` and hot module reloading pitfalls: Node.js caches modules in `require.cache` after the first load — subsequent `require()` calls return the cached version. `delete require.cache[require.resolve('./module')]` forces a reload on the next require. Circular dependencies in the cache can cause stale references after partial cache clearing. Hot module reloading (HMR) in Webpack/Vite: `module.hot.accept('./module', updater)` for client-side HMR. In ESM, there is NO equivalent of `require.cache` — ES modules are immutable once loaded (no cache clearing mechanism), and dynamic import caching cannot be bypassed. UNSAID: the Node.js module system acquires a lock during module loading — circular dependencies that trigger loading of the same module re-entrantly receive the partially-initialized exports object. This is why circular dependencies that use `export default` (an object) may work but those using named exports (which may not be defined yet) fail.

Node.js `Buffer.from()` and text encoding edge cases: `Buffer.from(string, 'utf8')` — default encoding. `Buffer.from(string, 'base64')` — decodes base64; silently ignores invalid base64 characters (no error thrown). `Buffer.from(string, 'hex')` — decodes hex; truncates at first invalid character. `Buffer.from(number)` — DEPRECATED in newer Node, currently equivalent to `Buffer.alloc(number)` which creates a zero-filled buffer of that size. Confusingly, `Buffer.from(5)` does NOT create a buffer from the bytes of the number 5 — it allocates 5 bytes. For number-to-bytes: `const buf = Buffer.alloc(4); buf.writeUInt32BE(value, 0)`. UNSAID: `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)` does NOT copy — it creates a Buffer that shares memory with the ArrayBuffer. Modifying one modifies the other. `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer).subarray(start, end)` creates a view (no copy); `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer).slice(start, end)` also creates a view in Node.js (despite the name suggesting a copy).

TypeScript's `Promise.all` vs `Promise.allSettled` vs `Promise.race` semantics: `Promise.all(promises)` fails fast — rejects as soon as ANY promise rejects; other promises continue running (but their results are ignored). `Promise.allSettled(promises)` waits for ALL to settle (fulfill or reject) — returns an array of `{ status: 'fulfilled', value } | { status: 'rejected', reason }`. Use allSettled when: you need results from all operations regardless of partial failures; use all when you need ALL to succeed and want early cancellation signal. `Promise.race(promises)` resolves/rejects with the FIRST settled promise — useful for timeouts: `Promise.race([fetch(url), new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new TimeoutError()), 5000))])`. UNSAID: `Promise.any(promises)` (Node 15+) resolves with the FIRST FULFILLED promise — if all reject, it throws an AggregateError. This is the inverse of `Promise.race` for success cases. None of these methods cancel the other promises — they only determine which result to use.

Node.js `process.exit()` and cleanup: `process.exit(code)` immediately terminates the process WITHOUT waiting for pending I/O, flushing logs, or running cleanup handlers (except `process.on('exit', ...)` synchronous handlers). `process.exit(0)` means success; `process.exit(1)` means error. The `exit` event handler CANNOT be async — any async operations started in 'exit' won't complete. The correct shutdown pattern: (1) stop accepting new work; (2) finish in-flight requests; (3) close connections; (4) flush logs; (5) call `process.exit(0)`. `process.on('beforeExit', async (code) => { ... })` fires when the event loop is empty and no async operations are pending — safe for async cleanup, but exiting here requires explicit `process.exit()`. UNSAID: `process.exitCode = 1; return` at the end of main function is cleaner than `process.exit(1)` — allows natural event loop drain. Unhandled rejections in Node 16+ automatically call `process.exit(1)` — don't swallow them.

TypeScript's `Object.keys()`, `Object.values()`, `Object.entries()` typing limitations: `Object.keys(obj)` returns `string[]` NOT `(keyof typeof obj)[]` — TypeScript intentionally widens the return type because at runtime, objects may have additional string keys not in the type (e.g., from JavaScript code that adds dynamic properties). Using `Object.keys(obj) as (keyof typeof obj)[]` is a cast that's generally safe for plain objects but incorrect for class instances with prototype properties. Type-safe iteration: `for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(config) as Entries<typeof config>) {...}` requires a custom `Entries<T>` type helper. UNSAID: `for (const key in obj)` iterates ALL enumerable properties including inherited ones — always add `if (Object.hasOwn(obj, key))` check (Node 17+ / ES2022). `Object.hasOwn(obj, key)` is safer than `obj.hasOwnProperty(key)` because `hasOwnProperty` can be overridden by the object itself.

TypeScript's `any` vs `unknown` in catch blocks: in TypeScript 4.0+, catch clause bindings default to `unknown` when `useUnknownInCatchVariables: true` (included in strict mode). This is correct because ANY value can be thrown in JavaScript (not just Error instances). `catch (e) { e.message }` is a type error because e is `unknown`. Correct: `catch (e) { const err = e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e)); logger.error(err.message) }`. Common anti-pattern: `catch (e: any) { log(e.message) }` — suppresses the type error but allows runtime exceptions if e is not an Error. `isError(e)` type predicate helper: `function isError(e: unknown): e is Error { return e instanceof Error }`. UNSAID: libraries sometimes throw non-Error objects: Axios wraps errors in `AxiosError`; fetch throws `TypeError` for network errors; Prisma throws `PrismaClientKnownRequestError`. Always check the specific type before accessing custom properties. Never access `e.message` without narrowing to `Error` first.

Node.js `EventEmitter` memory leak detection: `process.on('warning', warning => { if (warning.name === 'MaxListenersExceededWarning') logAndAlert(warning) })` catches listener accumulation. The warning includes the event name and count. `emitter.listenerCount('event')` checks how many listeners are registered. Common sources: (1) `req.on('close', handler)` in middleware called once per request — the event emitter is a shared server object; (2) `process.on('SIGINT', handler)` called at startup in a function that runs multiple times (e.g., test setup/teardown); (3) Socket.io event handlers added in client disconnect handlers. The `events.getMaxListeners()` default is 10. UNSAID: `emitter.rawListeners('event')` returns the actual listener functions including `once()` wrappers. `emitter.removeAllListeners('event')` removes all listeners for an event — use in cleanup/teardown. `EventEmitter.captureRejections = true` automatically catches rejected promises from async event handlers and routes them to the 'error' event.

TypeScript's `interface` declaration merging with generics: merging generic interfaces requires matching type parameter names: `interface Box<T> { value: T }; interface Box<T> { transform(fn: (t: T) => T): Box<T> }` — this works because T is used consistently. Merging interfaces with different generic parameters causes errors. Declaration merging does NOT merge method signatures in a way that preserves overload semantics — merged methods become overloads. UNSAID: module augmentation with generics: `declare module 'express' { interface Request { user?: User }; interface Response { json<T>(body: T): this }` — the Response generic augmentation may conflict with existing overloads. When augmenting third-party types with conflicting generics, use a wrapper type instead: `type TypedResponse<T> = Response & { json(body: T): void }`. Declaration merging applies to interfaces, namespaces, and enums — NOT to type aliases or classes.

Node.js `EventSource` vs `WebSocket` vs Server-Sent Events (SSE) choice: SSE (`text/event-stream`) is one-way server-to-client push over HTTP — no upgrade needed, works through proxies, automatic reconnect built into browsers, simpler than WebSocket. `res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/event-stream'); res.write('data: message\n\n')`. WebSocket is bidirectional. SSE limitations: binary data requires base64 encoding; max 6 concurrent SSE connections per domain in HTTP/1.1 (lifted with HTTP/2 multiplexing). Common failure: SSE connections through nginx without `proxy_buffering off; proxy_cache off` — nginx buffers the response and the client never receives events in real-time. UNSAID: SSE connections must handle disconnection and reconnection — the Last-Event-ID header sent by the client on reconnection allows the server to replay missed events. In serverless (Lambda, Vercel), SSE doesn't work for long-lived connections — use WebSocket API Gateway or polling.

TypeScript's `async generators` and for-await-of: `async function* stream() { while (true) { yield await fetchNextBatch() } }`. `for await (const batch of stream()) { process(batch) }` iterates asynchronously. Return: the generator is cleaned up when the for-await-of loop breaks (calls `.return()` on the iterator). Common failure: break without cleanup — if the generator holds an open database cursor or network connection, the `finally` block in the generator DOES run when the iterator is forcibly closed via break. UNSAID: `Symbol.asyncIterator` enables any object to be iterable with `for await ... of`. Node.js Readable streams implement `Symbol.asyncIterator` — `for await (const chunk of readableStream) {...}` is the idiomatic way to consume streams. Async generators are lazy — they only compute the next value when the consumer requests it via `next()`. For infinite async generators, always implement a cancellation mechanism (AbortSignal check inside the loop) to avoid goroutine-like leaks.

Node.js `zone.js` and async context tracking (historical context): `zone.js` (used by Angular) patches global async APIs to track context, but it causes performance regressions and compatibility issues with native async/await. Node.js's `AsyncLocalStorage` is the standard solution (no monkey-patching, built into Node 16+). Transition failures: code that depends on `zone.js` doesn't work in React or Vue; Angular's zone-less change detection mode (`zoneless: true` in Angular 17+) requires manual `ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck()`. UNSAID: `AsyncLocalStorage.getStore()` returns `undefined` if called outside of an `.run()` context — always provide default handling. Multiple concurrent async operations inherit context from their parent Task — this is correct for request-scoped logging but requires explicit reset for background jobs that shouldn't inherit request context. `AsyncLocalStorage.enterWith(store)` (unstable in older Node versions) sets context without a callback — avoid in production.

Node.js `crypto.scrypt` and password storage: `crypto.scrypt(password, salt, 64, callback)` — memory-hard, resistant to GPU attacks. `crypto.randomBytes(16)` for salt. Store as `salt:hash` where both are hex-encoded. Verification: `crypto.timingSafeEqual(stored, computed)`. Modern alternative: `argon2` (npm) — Argon2id variant is the recommended standard (PHC winner, 2015). `argon2.hash(password, { type: argon2.argon2id, memoryCost: 65536, timeCost: 3 })`. `argon2.verify(hash, password)` for verification. UNSAID: NEVER use `md5`, `sha1`, or even `sha256` for password storage — they're too fast (GPU can compute billions per second). `bcrypt` has a 72-character password limit (truncates silently at 72 bytes) — passwords longer than 72 chars have the same hash, which is a security vulnerability for users with long passwords. Argon2 has no such limitation. Pepper (application secret + password before hashing) adds another layer against database breaches — store pepper in environment variables, not in the database.

Node.js `HTTP/1.1 keep-alive` and connection headers: `Connection: keep-alive` from clients means 'reuse this TCP connection for more requests'. Without proper timeout handling, keep-alive connections accumulate — a server that never times out idle keep-alive connections can exhaust file descriptors. Node.js `http.Server` has `keepAliveTimeout` (default 5000ms in Node 18+, was 0ms before) and `headersTimeout` (default 60000ms). `server.keepAliveTimeout = 65000` (set slightly higher than load balancer idle timeout to avoid race conditions). AWS ALB has a 60s idle timeout by default — Node.js's 5s timeout causes the server to close connections that ALB still considers alive, causing 502s. Fix: set `keepAliveTimeout` to 70000ms when behind ALB. UNSAID: `server.requestTimeout` (Node 14.11+) limits how long a request can take including idle time waiting for request headers. `server.maxRequestsPerSocket` limits request reuse per socket — use to prevent stale connections from accumulating.

Node.js and `SIGPIPE` signal handling: when writing to a closed pipe (e.g., `node app.js | head -5`), Node.js receives SIGPIPE when writing after the pipe consumer exits. Default behavior: SIGPIPE terminates the process. For CLI tools that need to handle broken pipes gracefully: `process.stdout.on('error', (err) => { if (err.code === 'EPIPE') process.exit(0) })`. In Express/Fastify servers writing responses to closed client connections, Node.js handles EPIPE internally for HTTP — the connection is just closed without crashing. UNSAID: writing to `process.stdout` in a Node.js script piped to another command (`node script.js | grep ...`) and then grep exiting early causes EPIPE. Without the error handler, the script crashes with `write EPIPE`. This is common in scripts that process and filter output — always add the stdout error handler. `process.stderr` has a separate stream and needs its own error handler.

TypeScript's `co-variant` and `contra-variant` positions in generic types: function return types are covariant (subtypes are assignable to supertypes); function parameter types are contravariant (supertypes are assignable to subtypes — you can pass a more general function). `strictFunctionTypes: true` (part of strict) enforces contravariance for function parameters. Common failure: `type Callback<T> = (value: T) => void; const strCallback: Callback<string> = (s) => console.log(s); const anyCallback: Callback<any> = strCallback; anyCallback(42)` — passing a number to a function expecting string. With strictFunctionTypes, this is a type error. Method signatures in interfaces and classes use bivariant parameter checking (both covariant and contravariant allowed) for backward compatibility — this is a known TypeScript unsoundness. UNSAID: `interface Handler<T> { handle(value: T): void }` uses bivariant method checking (unsafe); `interface Handler<T> { handle: (value: T) => void }` uses contravariant function property checking (safe). Always prefer function property syntax for handlers.

Node.js `cluster` and `socket.io` sticky sessions: Socket.io requires all connections from the same client to go to the same server instance (sticky sessions) because the in-memory event store is per-process. Without sticky sessions in a cluster: WebSocket connections work (TCP-level stickiness from the OS load balancer), but polling fallback fails (HTTP requests may hit different processes). Solutions: (1) use `@socket.io/cluster-adapter` for sharing events across cluster workers via IPC; (2) use `@socket.io/redis-adapter` for sharing events across all Node.js instances via Redis pub/sub; (3) disable long-polling fallback (`transports: ['websocket']`) — no sticky session needed. UNSAID: Socket.io `namespace` and `room` concepts are memory-intensive — broadcasting to many rooms simultaneously consumes O(room_members) message copies. For large-scale real-time (>10k concurrent connections), consider a dedicated MQTT broker or Apache Kafka for fan-out, not Socket.io rooms.

TypeScript's `import()` dynamic import and type narrowing: `const module = await import('./module')` returns the module namespace; `module.default` for default export; named exports as properties. Dynamic import allows code splitting in bundlers. Type narrowing: the return type is `typeof import('./module')` — TypeScript infers all exports and their types. Common failure: `const { default: Class } = await import('./heavy-module')` — destructuring dynamic imports works for named exports but TypeScript sometimes infers the type incorrectly for complex re-exports. `import type` (static) is erased at runtime; `import()` (dynamic) is a function call at runtime. UNSAID: `import()` in Node.js ESM context requires the path to include the file extension — `import('./utils')` fails if the actual file is `utils.js`; write `import('./utils.js')`. In `moduleResolution: bundler` mode, extension-less imports work because the bundler resolves them. Vite's `import.meta.glob` enables importing multiple modules matching a pattern.

Node.js `unhandledRejection` and `uncaughtException` production handling: `process.on('uncaughtException', (err, origin) => { logger.fatal(err); process.exit(1) })` — log and EXIT after uncaught exceptions (the process state is unknown/corrupted). `process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason, promise) => { logger.error(reason) })` — log but don't necessarily exit (Node 15+ exits with code 1 by default). Common anti-pattern: using `unhandledRejection` handler to suppress errors and keep the process alive — this masks bugs and causes unpredictable behavior. The correct pattern: fix the root cause (add proper .catch() or try/catch) rather than global error suppression. UNSAID: `process.on('uncaughtExceptionMonitor', callback)` (Node 12.17+) is called BEFORE the `uncaughtException` event — use for logging/telemetry without interfering with the exit behavior. Domain module (deprecated) was the old way to handle errors per-request; replace with proper async error propagation and middleware-level try/catch.

Node.js `ENOMEM` and `EMFILE` (too many open files) errors: `EMFILE` occurs when Node.js has more open file descriptors than the OS limit (`ulimit -n`, typically 1024 on Linux by default, 8192+ in Docker). Common causes: (1) reading many files in parallel without limiting concurrency (`Promise.all(files.map(f => fs.readFile(f)))` on 10000 files); (2) opening database connections that aren't properly closed; (3) HTTP servers keeping too many keep-alive connections open. Fix for file reading: use a semaphore (`async-sema`, `p-limit`) to limit concurrency: `const limit = pLimit(20); await Promise.all(files.map(f => limit(() => readFile(f))))`. System limit increase: `ulimit -n 65536` or in Docker `--ulimit nofile=65536:65536`. UNSAID: `ENFILE` is different from `EMFILE` — ENFILE is a system-wide limit (all processes combined), EMFILE is per-process. In Kubernetes, the limit is set in the pod spec: `securityContext: { sysctls: [{ name: 'fs.file-max', value: '65536' }] }`.

TypeScript's `strict: true` and gradual migration pitfalls: enabling `strict: true` in an existing codebase without migration produces thousands of errors. Gradual migration: add `// @ts-nocheck` to files not yet migrated; use `skipLibCheck: true` temporarily for third-party types issues; use `noImplicitAny: false` first (the most disruptive change), then add it. `allowJs: true` + `checkJs: true` enables TypeScript checking for `.js` files. Common migration blockers: (1) `noImplicitAny` — every untyped parameter and variable needs types; (2) `strictNullChecks` — `null` and `undefined` are no longer assignable to every type; (3) `strictPropertyInitialization` — class properties must be initialized. Tooling: `ts-migrate` (from Airbnb) automatically adds `// @ts-ignore` and type annotations to migrate JavaScript codebases. UNSAID: the `diagnostics` configuration in tsconfig can suppress specific errors during migration while fixing the codebase incrementally. Never silently add widespread `as any` casts during migration — they hide bugs.

Node.js `getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND` and DNS failures in containers: `ENOTFOUND` means the hostname couldn't be resolved. Common causes: (1) service name typo in Docker Compose or Kubernetes config; (2) trying to connect before the target service is ready (race condition at container startup); (3) incorrect DNS configuration in Kubernetes namespace isolation; (4) IPv6-only DNS responses when the app only supports IPv4. Debug steps: `dig serviceName` inside the container; check if the target service pod is running and has an IP; verify `kube-dns` is healthy. `dns.setDefaultResultOrder('ipv4first')` (Node 17+) forces IPv4 preference. UNSAID: in Docker Compose, service names are resolved via the embedded DNS server at 127.0.0.11 — services can reference each other by service name. In Kubernetes, fully-qualified domain names (FQDN) avoid cross-namespace resolution issues: `service.namespace.svc.cluster.local`. Health check with exponential backoff before initial connection is best practice for container startup sequencing.

TypeScript's `co-routine-style` async patterns and interleaving pitfalls: two async operations that appear sequential may interleave if they share state. `let count = 0; async function inc() { const c = count; await delay(0); count = c + 1 }; Promise.all([inc(), inc()])` — both read count=0, both set count=1, final count is 1 not 2. This is a classic read-modify-write race condition in async JavaScript, equivalent to a data race in multi-threaded code. Fix: use a Mutex from `async-mutex` library; or use atomic database operations; or redesign to avoid shared mutable state. UNSAID: `await` in JavaScript is a single-threaded yield point — but between any two awaits, another async function can run if it was awaiting something that resolves. The event loop processes ONE task at a time but async code interleaves at await boundaries. This is similar to cooperative multitasking — you yield control at every await.

Node.js `garbage collection` and long-pause GC: V8 uses generational GC (minor GC for young objects, major GC for old generation). Major GC pauses can last 50-200ms+ for heaps > 1GB — visible as latency spikes in production services. Causes of large heap: (1) caching large objects in memory without TTL; (2) accumulating closures in long-lived callbacks; (3) retaining DOM nodes (in SSR contexts) or stream buffers. Mitigation: `--max-old-space-size=512` to limit heap (forces OOM before GC thrashing); monitor `v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()` for old space growth. Incremental marking (V8's default in recent versions) reduces pause times by interleaving marking with JavaScript execution. UNSAID: `process.memoryUsage().external` tracks memory held in C++ objects and Buffers (not in V8 heap) — this can grow unboundedly if Buffers aren't released. `--expose-gc; global.gc()` forces a GC cycle — useful in tests to verify there are no leaks after an operation.

TypeScript's `declaration file` (.d.ts) authoring pitfalls: `declare module 'package'` declares the entire module — any import from 'package' returns `any` if you don't declare all exports. Missing specific exports cause TypeScript to fall back to `any`. `export = Module` (CJS-style export) and `import Module = require('module')` — the old CJS import syntax; in ESM context use `import Module from 'module'` but this requires `esModuleInterop: true` or `allowSyntheticDefaultImports: true`. UNSAID: `@types/` packages are community-maintained and may be outdated — always check the version compatibility between the `@types/` package and the library version you're using. For packages without `@types/`, create a local `types/package-name.d.ts`: `declare module 'untyped-package' { export function doThing(): void }`. The `typeRoots` tsconfig option controls where TypeScript looks for declaration files beyond node_modules/@types.

Node.js `http2.Http2Session` and stream multiplexing pitfalls: HTTP/2 streams share a single TCP connection via multiplexing. When the connection stalls (network issue, server GOAWAY), ALL streams fail simultaneously — this is worse than HTTP/1.1 where only the affected connection fails. HTTP/2 `RST_STREAM` tears down a single stream; `GOAWAY` tears down the entire connection. Common failure: load balancers that use HTTP/2 between client and LB but HTTP/1.1 from LB to backend — mismatched protocols cause `ERR_HTTP2_STREAM_ERROR`. `http2Session.settings.maxHeaderListSize` limits total header size — exceeding it causes `ENHANCE_YOUR_CALM` errors from servers. UNSAID: Node.js's http2 client does NOT multiplex requests to the same host by default when using the built-in http2 module — each `connect()` call creates a new session. The `undici` library's `Pool` handles H2 multiplexing correctly with connection reuse.

Node.js `process.env` type is `{ [key: string]: string | undefined }` — accessing a missing key returns `undefined`, not an error. Common failure: `const port = parseInt(process.env.PORT)` where `parseInt(undefined)` returns `NaN`, causing `server.listen(NaN)` to silently bind to port 0 (random ephemeral port). Pattern: validate all env vars at startup before binding: use a schema library (Zod, envalid, env-schema) to parse and validate `process.env` once, then export a typed config object. `const config = z.object({ PORT: z.coerce.number().int().min(1024).max(65535).default(3000), DATABASE_URL: z.string().url() }).parse(process.env)`. Crash on startup for missing required env vars — failing fast prevents subtle runtime errors hours later. UNSAID: `dotenv` loads `.env` files but must be called before any `process.env` access — calling `require('dotenv/config')` or `import 'dotenv/config'` as the first import ensures this. In containers, never use `.env` files — pass env vars directly; `.env` is for local dev only.

TypeScript `strictFunctionTypes` (enabled by `strict: true`) makes function types contravariant in parameter positions. This means `(arg: Animal) => void` is NOT assignable to `(arg: Dog) => void` — the handler accepting any Animal is not safe to use where a Dog-specific handler is expected, because the broader type would be called with things that aren't Dogs. Common failure: event emitter callbacks typed as `(e: SpecificEvent) => void` being passed to a generic `addEventListener(type, (e: Event) => void)` — works at runtime, fails type check. Method signatures (using `:` syntax) are BIVARIANT for historical compatibility; function properties (using `=` syntax) respect contravariance. UNSAID: `strictFunctionTypes` only applies to function types written in function syntax, not method syntax in object types — this is an intentional TypeScript design choice to avoid breaking common object method patterns. Use function property syntax for strict variance checking.

Node.js stream `backpressure` failure: when a readable stream produces data faster than a writable stream consumes it, unhandled backpressure causes memory to grow unboundedly. Common failure: `readableStream.on('data', (chunk) => writableStream.write(chunk))` — `write()` returns `false` when the internal buffer is full, but the 'data' event listener ignores this signal and keeps feeding data, causing the process to OOM. Correct pattern: use `pipeline(readable, writable, callback)` or `readable.pipe(writable)` — both handle backpressure automatically by pausing the readable when the writable buffer is full. For custom transform logic: use `Transform` stream class, not event listeners. UNSAID: `stream.pipeline()` correctly handles cleanup (unpiping, error propagation, stream destruction) whereas `pipe()` does NOT propagate errors — if the writable emits an error, the readable is not automatically unpiped, causing another leak. Always use `pipeline()` over `pipe()` in production code.

Node.js `unhandledRejection` behavior changed in Node.js 15: before v15, unhandled promise rejections print a warning but do NOT crash the process. From v15+, unhandled rejections crash the process with exit code 1. This is a BREAKING CHANGE for applications that silently rely on swallowed rejections. Audit code for promise chains without `.catch()`, `async` functions called without `await` (fire-and-forget with no error handling), `Promise.all` where one rejection should be isolated. Pattern: `process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason, promise) => { logger.error({ reason }, 'Unhandled rejection'); gracefulShutdown(1) })`. For intentional fire-and-forget: `.catch(err => logger.warn(err))` minimum. UNSAID: `Promise.allSettled()` never rejects — use it when you want all promises to complete regardless of individual failures. `Promise.race()` rejects immediately if the first settled promise rejects — subtle difference from `Promise.any()` which only rejects when ALL promises reject.

Node.js memory leak patterns from closures: event listeners that capture variables in closure scope prevent garbage collection of large objects. Pattern: `function setup(largeData) { emitter.on('event', () => process(largeData)) }` — `largeData` stays alive as long as the listener is registered. Fix: remove listeners with `emitter.removeListener()` or `emitter.off()` when done; use `emitter.once()` for single-use listeners. `AbortController` signal listeners: `signal.addEventListener('abort', handler)` — handler must be removed with `signal.removeEventListener('abort', handler)` or use the `signal` option in `fetch`/`EventTarget` APIs that auto-clean. Node.js DevTools Memory tab or `--inspect` + `v8.writeHeapSnapshot()` identifies retained objects. UNSAID: `EventEmitter` default max listeners is 10 — exceeding this prints a warning (NOT an error). Use `emitter.setMaxListeners(0)` to disable the warning if you intentionally have many listeners, but first audit for actual leaks. `WeakRef` and `FinalizationRegistry` in Node.js 14+ allow holding references without preventing GC.

Node.js `ECONNRESET` and socket handling: `ECONNRESET` means the remote side closed the connection abruptly (RST packet). In HTTP servers, this is normal — clients disconnect mid-request (browser navigation, network interruption). Without error handling: uncaught ECONNRESET on a socket crashes the process (in older Node.js) or emits on the process. Fix: `server.on('clientError', (err, socket) => { if (err.code === 'ECONNRESET' || !socket.writable) return; socket.end('HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request

') })`. For HTTP clients: ECONNRESET during `keep-alive` reuse means the server closed the idle connection — detect and retry once (it's idempotent). `ECONNREFUSED` means nothing is listening on the port — no retry without backoff. `ETIMEDOUT` means the connection was established but the remote side stopped responding — always has a root cause in the server. UNSAID: In Kubernetes, ECONNRESET is common during rolling deployments as Pods are killed — implement retry logic with `axios-retry` or `got`'s built-in retry on ECONNRESET for idempotent requests.

Node.js `setTimeout` / `setInterval` in production failure modes: `setTimeout(fn, delay)` does NOT guarantee exact timing — Node.js event loop busy with I/O can delay callbacks significantly. `setInterval` accumulates drift: if the callback takes 100ms and interval is 1000ms, the next call is at 1000ms from start, not 900ms after completion. For precise recurring work: use `setTimeout` recursively with elapsed-time correction, or use a job queue (BullMQ with cron). `clearTimeout` / `clearInterval` — always store the returned timer ID and clear it in cleanup: `const timer = setInterval(fn, 1000); process.on('SIGTERM', () => clearInterval(timer))`. UNSAID: In tests, use `jest.useFakeTimers()` to mock timers deterministically — real timers make async tests flaky. `setImmediate(fn)` runs after I/O callbacks in the same event loop iteration — useful for yielding to I/O without the overhead of `setTimeout(fn, 0)` (which has minimum 1ms delay).

TypeScript `enum` at runtime generates a bidirectional object (`{ 0: 'Red', Red: 0 }` for numeric enums) — this causes unexpected behavior when iterating over enum values with `Object.keys()` or `Object.values()`. `Object.keys(Direction)` returns `['Up', 'Down', 'Left', 'Right', '0', '1', '2', '3']` — both names and values. Filtering is needed: `Object.values(Direction).filter(v => typeof v === 'string')`. `const enum` is erased at compile time (no runtime object) but cannot be used across module boundaries in declaration files (`isolatedModules` mode). Alternative: `const Direction = { Up: 'UP', Down: 'DOWN' } as const; type Direction = typeof Direction[keyof typeof Direction]` — generates a plain object, no bidirectional mapping, tree-shakeable. UNSAID: TypeScript `enum` members are NOT assignable to `number` by default with strict mode — you cannot pass `Direction.Up` (value 0) where `0` is expected without explicit casting. String enums are fully opaque: `Direction.Up` is assignable ONLY to `Direction`, not to `string`.

Node.js `http.createServer()` default timeout is 0 (no timeout) — a slow client or malicious actor can hold a connection open indefinitely, causing resource exhaustion. Set: `server.setTimeout(30000)` (30s for slow headers/body) and `server.requestTimeout = 30000` (Node.js 18+, per-request timeout from headers start to response end). `server.headersTimeout` (Node.js 11.3+, default 60s) limits time to receive complete HTTP headers — prevents Slowloris attacks. In Express: `request-timeout` middleware or `connect-timeout` adds per-request deadlines. For Kubernetes: set `livenessProbe.timeoutSeconds` shorter than server timeout to allow container restart before all connections are exhausted. UNSAID: `server.keepAliveTimeout` (default 5s in Node.js 18+) must be set HIGHER than the load balancer's idle timeout — if LB timeout (e.g., AWS ALB 60s) > keepAliveTimeout (5s), the server closes the connection first causing 502 errors. Set `server.keepAliveTimeout = 65000` and `server.headersTimeout = 66000` as a baseline.

TypeScript `declaration merging` with `interface` causes unexpected behavior when augmenting third-party types: `interface Window { myLib: MyLib }` — this merges with the global Window interface, affecting ALL code in the project. Common failure: augmenting `Express.Request` in a middleware types file that's NOT included in all compilation units — causes type errors in files that don't include the augmentation. Fix: ensure module augmentation files are always included via `tsconfig.include` or imported explicitly. `declare module 'express' { interface Request { userId?: string } }` must be imported OR be in a file covered by tsconfig. UNSAID: interface merging is ORDER-INDEPENDENT in most cases but overloads in merged interfaces ARE order-dependent — later declarations have lower priority for overload resolution, which is counterintuitive. `type` aliases cannot be merged — attempting to redeclare a type alias is a compile error. For safe extension of third-party types, prefer `type ExtendedRequest = Request & { userId: string }` over module augmentation.

TypeScript `as` type assertion bypasses the type checker without runtime checks — it's a compile-time instruction that generates NO runtime code. `const user = data as User` does not validate that `data` actually matches the `User` type. Common failure: parsing JSON from external sources with `JSON.parse(response) as ApiResponse` — if the API changes shape, TypeScript has no way to detect the mismatch at runtime, leading to `undefined` access errors at unexpected code paths. Fix: ALWAYS parse external data through a runtime validator: `const user = UserSchema.parse(data)` (Zod throws on mismatch), or `const result = UserSchema.safeParse(data)` (explicit error handling). `as unknown as T` (double assertion) is a red flag — it completely bypasses type safety for any transformation. UNSAID: `satisfies` keyword (TypeScript 4.9+) validates an expression against a type WITHOUT changing the expression's type — use `satisfies` over `as` when you want to check compatibility: `const config = { port: 3000 } satisfies Config` keeps `port` typed as `3000` (literal) while validating the shape.

Node.js `JSON.parse` and `JSON.stringify` edge cases: `JSON.stringify(undefined)` returns `undefined` (not a string), `JSON.stringify({ key: undefined })` returns `'{}'` (key omitted). `JSON.stringify({ key: NaN })` returns `'{"key":null}'` — NaN and Infinity serialize to `null`. `JSON.parse('null')` returns `null` (valid JSON). `JSON.parse` throws `SyntaxError` on invalid input — always wrap in try/catch. Circular references: `JSON.stringify(obj)` throws `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON` — use `flatted` or `safe-stable-stringify` for circular-safe serialization. Date objects: `JSON.stringify(new Date())` returns ISO string; `JSON.parse` does NOT restore Date objects — returns string. Prototype methods are omitted: `JSON.stringify(new Map())` returns `'{}'`. UNSAID: `JSON.parse` with a `reviver` function can restore types: `JSON.parse(str, (key, value) => key === 'createdAt' ? new Date(value) : value)`. For high-throughput JSON: `fast-json-stringify` with a schema can be 5-10x faster than `JSON.stringify` for structured data.

Node.js `path.join()` and directory traversal: `path.join(baseDir, userInput)` is NOT safe against directory traversal. `path.join('/uploads', '../../../etc/passwd')` returns `/etc/passwd`. Fix: `const safe = path.resolve(baseDir, userInput); if (!safe.startsWith(path.resolve(baseDir) + path.sep)) throw new Error('Path traversal detected')`. `path.normalize()` collapses `..` but doesn't prevent traversal — `path.normalize('/base/../../../etc/passwd')` returns `/etc/passwd`. `path.join()` also collapses `..` without bounds checking. UNSAID: URL paths are a separate traversal surface: `new URL(userInput, 'https://example.com').pathname` normalizes but doesn't prevent `%2F` encoded slashes. In Express: use `express.static(dir, { index: false, dotfiles: 'deny' })` — serves only files within `dir`. For file upload destinations: use `crypto.randomUUID()` as the filename rather than the user-provided name — completely eliminates path-based attacks.

vLLM defaults gpu_memory_utilization=0.9, meaning it pre-reserves 90% of GPU VRAM for the KV-cache page pool at startup. Under concurrent high-context requests this headroom is consumed entirely, producing CUDA out-of-memory errors that crash the worker process — not a graceful 429. The fix is NOT to lower gpu_memory_utilization blindly: lowering it reduces throughput. The correct approach is to (1) profile actual peak KV-cache usage with `--max-num-seqs` and `--max-model-len` constraints, (2) set gpu_memory_utilization to leave headroom for CUDA kernels (~5-10%), and (3) configure `--max-num-batched-tokens` to cap total in-flight token count. Many teams hit this on their first multi-user deployment because single-user benchmarks never exhaust the pool.

vLLM treats AWQ, GPTQ, FP8, and GGUF as distinct quantization backends with separate kernel implementations — they are NOT interchangeable. Common failure modes: (1) Loading an AWQ-quantized model without `--quantization awq` causes silent weight corruption or crashes, not a clear error. (2) GPTQ models require `--quantization gptq` and are slower than AWQ on vLLM because vLLM's GPTQ path uses Marlin kernels only on Ampere+, falling back to slow triton on older GPUs. (3) FP8 is only supported on Hopper (H100/H200) and Ada Lovelace (L40S); using `--quantization fp8` on A100 raises an unsupported hardware error. (4) GGUF is not natively supported in the main vLLM server — it requires llama.cpp or conversion first. Always verify the quantization format matches both the `--quantization` flag AND the target GPU architecture before deployment.

vLLM's speculative decoding (--speculative-model) requires that the draft model and target model share identical vocabulary and tokenizer. Using a mismatched draft model (e.g., a 1B distilled model with a different tokenizer than the 70B target) causes silent token ID misalignment: the draft produces tokens that are accepted by the verifier's numerical check but decode to wrong text in the target's vocabulary. This manifests as subtly garbled output rather than an error. Always verify tokenizer identity with `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(draft).vocab_size == AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(target).vocab_size` and ideally confirm they share the same tokenizer class before enabling speculative decoding in production.

vLLM's --max-model-len parameter truncates input prompts silently by default when they exceed the configured limit. A request with a 16K-token prompt sent to a server configured with --max-model-len 8192 will be truncated to 8192 tokens from the right (the end), not rejected. The model then generates a continuation of a truncated context, often producing incoherent output with no error returned to the client. Production deployments should set --max-model-len to match the model's true context window, and application code should validate prompt length before sending. Alternatively, configuring truncation_on_max_len=True in the request raises an explicit error instead of silently truncating.

vLLM multi-node inference using Ray (--worker-use-ray with multiple hosts) has a common deployment failure where workers start successfully but inference silently hangs. The root cause is almost always network asymmetry: Ray's collective communication (NCCL all-reduce for tensor parallelism across nodes) requires symmetric bidirectional bandwidth between all worker pairs, but production Kubernetes or cloud networks often have asymmetric routing or firewall rules that block NCCL's preferred port range (starting at 29400). Diagnosing this requires running `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` to see which NCCL operation stalls. Fixes: (1) open NCCL port range in security groups, (2) set NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME to the correct network interface explicitly, (3) use InfiniBand over RoCE when available. Silent hangs rather than error messages make this failure class disproportionately time-consuming to diagnose.

Destructuring a reactive() object loses reactivity. When you write `const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 })`, count is now a plain number — the Proxy binding is severed. The same trap applies to function parameters and return values. The fix is either (a) keep accessing via the reactive object (state.count), (b) use toRefs() to convert all properties to individual ref() wrappers before destructuring, or (c) use ref() directly instead of reactive() for values you intend to destructure. This is one of the most frequent Vue 3 migration bugs and the primary reason many teams default to ref() for everything.

ref() wraps primitive values in a { value } container so the Proxy can track mutations. Inside <script setup>, refs auto-unwrap in the template — you write {{ count }} not {{ count.value }}. But inside JavaScript (setup function body, composables, watchers) you must always use count.value. Forgetting .value produces silent bugs: the assignment mutates the raw primitive and is not tracked. A secondary trap: reactive() auto-unwraps nested refs — reading state.myRef gives the unwrapped value — which can make behavior inconsistent depending on how deeply a ref is nested. The mental model requires distinguishing template-land from JS-land at all times.

Vue Router's navigation guards are often used for authentication, but the common failure is making the auth check synchronous against a reactive store value that may not be hydrated yet (e.g., after a page refresh before the user info API returns). The guard sees an unauthenticated state and redirects to login, only for the user to be redirected back after the auth check completes — a 'flash of unauthenticated content' or redirect loop. The correct pattern is to await an auth initialization promise inside beforeEach before checking auth state, or use a dedicated authReady flag in Pinia that resolves after the initial auth check. Never redirect based on auth state if auth state may still be loading.

Vue component keys are used to force re-creation rather than reuse. The subtle failure is using a non-unique or non-stable key — such as array index — in v-for lists that undergo reordering, insertion, or deletion. When the key is the array index, Vue reuses the DOM element at position 0 for whichever item is now at position 0, potentially showing stale local state (input values, animation states) from the previous item. Always use a stable unique identifier (database ID, UUID) as the key. The opposite failure: using a key on a component to force reset when props change (a legitimate pattern for resetting form state) but forgetting that this destroys and recreates the component, triggering full lifecycle including re-fetches.

Vue's Suspense component (experimental, ships stable in Vue 3.3+) enables async component trees to show fallback UI while awaiting async setup(). The failure mode is nesting multiple async components under a single Suspense boundary and assuming fallback shows until all are resolved — this is correct, but it means a slow component blocks the entire subtree. The fix is to use Suspense boundaries at lower granularity, wrapping each independent async section. A second failure: error handling inside Suspense requires a separate ErrorBoundary-equivalent using onErrorCaptured in a wrapper component — Suspense itself does not have an error slot in the current API.

Vue 3 reactive() vs ref(): reactive() returns a Proxy of an object — destructuring it loses reactivity because you extract plain values. `const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 })` — count is now a plain number, not reactive. ref() wraps a single value in an object with a .value property — it maintains reactivity through reassignment. UNSAID: the standard advice is to prefer ref() over reactive() because ref() works consistently regardless of destructuring. If you use reactive(), use toRefs() when destructuring: `const { count } = toRefs(state)`. Pinia uses reactive() internally but exposes state via storeToRefs() to enable reactive destructuring. The pattern: use ref() for primitives, ref() for objects too unless you specifically need reactive()'s nested object feel.

Vue 3 watch vs watchEffect: watchEffect automatically tracks reactive dependencies used in its callback — no explicit source needed. watch requires explicit source(s) and only runs when those change. Gotcha: watchEffect runs immediately (before mounting), which can cause issues if it accesses DOM or component refs not yet mounted. watch with `immediate: true` runs immediately; without, it only runs on source changes. UNSAID: watchEffect is convenient but can obscure which reactive values trigger the effect — making it harder to understand component behavior. watch is more explicit and predictable. For complex dependency tracking, watchEffect; for clear intent, watch. The `flush: 'post'` option runs effects after DOM updates — necessary when accessing updated DOM elements.

Vue 3 component emit validation: `defineEmits(['update:modelValue', 'close'])` declares emittable events but doesn't validate payload types. For TypeScript validation: `defineEmits<{ 'update:modelValue': [value: string]; close: [] }>()`. UNSAID: undeclared emits still work (Vue doesn't block them) but generate warnings and break IDE autocomplete for parent components. The v-model pattern on custom components: component emits `update:modelValue`, parent uses `v-model`. Vue 3 allows multiple v-model bindings: `v-model:title` and `v-model:content` on the same component, emitting `update:title` and `update:content` respectively — eliminating the need for separate props+emits for multi-field form components.

Vue 3 reactivity pitfalls with arrays: while Vue 3 Proxy handles array index mutations (`arr[0] = value`) correctly, there are subtle issues with reactive arrays and Vue's rendering. Using non-reactive arrays in reactive() objects is fine. UNSAID: the Vue 2 array mutation method wrappers (push, pop, splice, sort, reverse, shift, unshift) were needed because Vue 2 couldn't detect array mutations. In Vue 3, all array mutations are detected by Proxy. The remaining gotcha: replacing an array entirely `this.items = newItems` in Options API must use the component's reactive reference — reassigning a local variable doesn't work. In Composition API, `items.value = newItems` (for ref) or `state.items = newItems` (for reactive object property) both work correctly.

Vue 3 custom directive lifecycle hooks differ from Vue 2: `mounted` replaces `inserted`, `updated` replaces `update`, `beforeUnmount` replaces `unbind`. Directives have hooks: created, beforeMount, mounted, beforeUpdate, updated, beforeUnmount, unmounted. UNSAID: custom directives in Vue 3 should be used sparingly — they manipulate the DOM directly, bypassing Vue's reactivity system. The appropriate use cases: DOM-focused libraries (focus management, resize observers, intersection observers, third-party plugins). Component-level logic belongs in composables, not directives. The binding parameter in directive hooks contains: value (current value), oldValue (previous value), arg (directive argument), modifiers (object of modifiers).

Vue 3 reactivity with class instances: reactive() and ref() work with plain objects (POJOs) but have limitations with class instances — methods accessing `this` may not be tracked correctly, and class-based inheritance can confuse the Proxy wrapper. UNSAID: the Vue 3 docs recommend using plain objects for reactive state rather than class instances. If you must use classes, ensure methods don't bypass the Proxy (don't store class instance in a plain variable, always access through the reactive wrapper). The ORM-style pattern (Vuex models with class methods) that was popular in Vue 2 is generally replaced with Pinia stores + plain TypeScript interfaces in Vue 3.

Vue 3 SSR with Nuxt 3: server-side rendered components have access to Nuxt's server context (event, cookies, headers) via useNuxtApp(). Client-side components use `if (process.client)` guard or `<ClientOnly>` wrapper. Hydration mismatches cause SSR errors — same rules as React hydration. UNSAID: Nuxt 3's SSR rendering pipeline is fully asynchronous — setup() can await data fetching directly. The `useFetch`, `useAsyncData`, and `$fetch` composables are Nuxt-specific and handle SSR deduplication (fetch on server, no refetch on client after hydration). Using regular fetch() in setup() without Nuxt's composables causes double-fetching: once on server, once on client after hydration. Nuxt's auto-imports eliminate manual imports for Vue and Nuxt composables — a significant DX improvement.

Vue 3 computed property with side effects is an anti-pattern: computed properties should be pure transformations of reactive state with no side effects (no API calls, no DOM mutations, no state writes). Side effects in computed cause: (1) unpredictable execution timing, (2) infinite reactivity loops (computed reads A, writes B, B affects A), (3) multiple executions per render. UNSAID: the fix is always to move side effects to watch or watchEffect. Computed properties are lazily evaluated (only when dependencies change AND the value is read), which makes side effects even less predictable. The performance optimization: computed values are cached between renders and only recomputed when dependencies change — this cache is broken by side effects.

Vue 3 reactivity loss from Object.assign: `Object.assign(state, newData)` on a reactive object works correctly — Object.assign mutates the target object, and the Proxy intercepts the property assignments. BUT: `Object.assign({}, state, newData)` creates a plain object (loses reactivity). UNSAID: the subtle reactive-vs-non-reactive issue: Proxy traps only fire when accessing through the proxy. Creating a new plain object with spread or Object.assign bypasses all traps. The spread of a reactive object `{ ...reactiveObj }` is safe for creating a snapshot (non-reactive) but incorrect for maintaining reactivity. In Composition API, `const state = reactive({...})` then `state.field = value` correctly triggers updates. Pinia handles this correctly internally — store state modifications go through the store, not direct object manipulation.

Vue 3 lifecycle hooks in Composition API: onMounted, onBeforeUnmount, onUpdated, etc. A key difference from Options API: multiple onMounted calls in the same setup() are all called (they stack), not overridden. UNSAID: this is intentional for composables — each composable can register its own lifecycle hooks without conflicting. The composable pattern: `useFoo()` calls onMounted and onBeforeUnmount internally — the caller doesn't need to know about the lifecycle management. Vue 3 also adds onServerPrefetch (SSR only, await data before rendering on server) and onActivated/onDeactivated (KeepAlive). The setup() function itself runs before onBeforeMount — it's the equivalent of the constructor/created combination from Options API.

Vue 3 Pinia with SSR: Pinia stores are singletons in SPA mode but must be per-request in SSR mode to prevent state leakage between users. Nuxt 3 handles this automatically via the pinia plugin. In custom SSR setups, create a new Pinia instance per request: `const pinia = createPinia()` inside the request handler. UNSAID: this is the same SSR state leakage problem as React's module-level state. Server-side singleton stores leak user A's data to user B in concurrent requests. Nuxt 3's SSR-safe Pinia: `useStore()` returns the store scoped to the current SSR context (via vue app injection). The `$pinia` symbol on the Vue app ensures each request gets its own store instances. Testing SSR behavior: verify stores reset between requests with concurrent test scenarios.

Vue 3 nextTick usage: `await nextTick()` waits for Vue to complete DOM updates after a reactive state change. Required when you need to read DOM values (dimensions, positions) after state changes. UNSAID: the common mistake is reading DOM dimensions in the same tick as state changes — the DOM hasn't updated yet. Pattern: `count.value = 10; await nextTick(); const height = element.value.offsetHeight`. In tests with Vue Test Utils, `await nextTick()` waits for reactive updates to complete before assertions. Multiple nextTick calls are sometimes needed for deeply nested reactive updates. nextTick returns a Promise — always use `await nextTick()` for reliable timing.

Vue 3 performance anti-pattern: using v-for with expensive computed properties inside the loop. Each list item's computed runs for every render. UNSAID: move expensive computations outside the v-for loop when possible. For item-specific computations, memoize with a Map keyed by item ID rather than computing in the template. v-memo inside v-for can memoize individual items: `<div v-for='item in list' v-memo='[item.id]'>` — skips re-rendering each row unless item.id changes. This is more granular than v-memo on the parent list.
