Performance-sensitive lists (100+ items with complex rendering) benefit from: v-memo for partial memoization, virtual scrolling for large datasets, and careful computed property design.

Vue 3 TypeScript strict template checking: by default, Vue templates have loose TypeScript checking. Enabling strict mode in tsconfig.json + volar.config.ts increases strictness. Template expression errors are caught at build time with `vue-tsc`. UNSAID: the most common TypeScript-template mismatch: using a possibly-undefined property in a template without null check — passes TypeScript in the script block but not in the template with strict mode. Enabling strict template type checking catches these at build time instead of runtime. The `<template lang='ts'>` is not required for TypeScript — the script block's types flow into the template automatically via Volar. The `defineComponent()` wrapper provides TypeScript inference in IDEs without Volar for legacy setups.

Vue 3 reactive state mutation in child components: mutating a prop directly (`props.items.push(item)`) bypasses Vue's reactivity system in the parent — the parent doesn't know the mutation happened. Props should be treated as read-only in child components. UNSAID: Vue 3 adds a runtime warning for direct prop mutation. The fix: emit an event with the new value (`emit('update:items', [...props.items, item])`) or use v-model for two-way binding. For deeply nested objects passed as props, the mutation warning may not trigger (Vue can't easily detect deep mutations through props without reactive wrapping) — this is a silent bug pattern. The safest pattern: treat all props as read-only, always use emits for parent communication.

Vue 3 shallowRef vs ref: shallowRef wraps the value in a ref but does NOT deep-convert the value to reactive. Mutations to the inner object properties don't trigger updates — only replacing the .value reference triggers reactivity. UNSAID: use shallowRef for large data structures (arrays of hundreds of objects) where deep reactivity is unnecessary — it avoids the Proxy overhead for every nested property. The failure mode: `shallowRef({ count: 0 })` then `obj.value.count++` — no reactive update triggered. You must do `obj.value = { ...obj.value, count: obj.value.count + 1 }` (replace the reference) to trigger an update. shallowReactive() similarly creates a reactive object where only the top-level properties are reactive.

Vue 3 component instance vs reactive state: `getCurrentInstance()` inside setup() returns the component instance — but using it for state management is an anti-pattern. Component instances are internal implementation details, not public API. Accessing `instance.ctx` or `instance.data` directly bypasses reactivity tracking. UNSAID: getCurrentInstance() is intended for plugin development, not application code. The correct state access pattern: use the composables and reactive primitives (ref, reactive, computed) that Vue exposes, not the internal component instance. The failure pattern: using getCurrentInstance() to access parent component's state — this creates tight coupling and bypasses Vue's data flow model.

Vue 3 memory leaks from unregistered watchers: creating watchers outside of component setup() (in setTimeout callbacks or event handlers) don't auto-cleanup when the component unmounts. Use watchEffect's return value (cleanup function) or call `const stop = watch(...)` and call `stop()` in onBeforeUnmount. UNSAID: watchers created inside setup() are automatically stopped when the component unmounts. Watchers created outside setup() (e.g., in a utility function called from a callback) must be manually stopped. The pattern for safe watcher creation outside setup: check `getCurrentInstance()` — if it returns a value, the watcher can be auto-registered to the component lifecycle; if null, manual cleanup is required.

Vue 3 Pinia type inference breaks with complex store compositions: when store A uses store B, and TypeScript's circular type inference kicks in, the types may not resolve correctly. UNSAID: the fix is to not circularly depend stores. If store A and B need shared state, extract it to a store C that both A and B use. The Pinia documentation explicitly warns against circular store dependencies. Runtime behavior works (JavaScript modules handle circular dependencies), but TypeScript inference fails. Additionally, Pinia stores' TypeScript types for the setup-style stores are more complex to define than Options-style stores — setup stores have better runtime flexibility but require more TypeScript ceremony.

Vue 3 scoped styles specificity: `<style scoped>` adds a unique attribute selector to all rules, increasing specificity. Overriding scoped styles from parent components requires either ::v-deep() (deep selector) or the `!important` hack (never use). Pattern: `:deep(.child-class) { ... }` in parent component reaches into child's scoped styles. UNSAID: overusing :deep() creates implicit coupling between parent and child CSS — the child doesn't control its own styling. The proper pattern for customizable components: CSS custom properties (variables) exposed by the child component, set by the parent. This is an explicit API contract. The scoped attribute is randomly generated at build time — hardcoding `data-v-xxxx` selectors in tests or external CSS is brittle and breaks on rebuild.

Vue 3 hydration mismatch in Nuxt: using Date.now(), Math.random(), or browser-only APIs during SSR causes hydration mismatches — the server renders one value, the client renders another. The `<ClientOnly>` component wraps content that should only render client-side. UNSAID: `<ClientOnly>` renders nothing on server and only renders its default slot on client after hydration. This fixes hydration mismatches but eliminates the SEO and TTFB benefits for the wrapped content. The fallback slot in `<ClientOnly>` provides server-side placeholder content. For user-specific content (showing logged-in user's name), server-side rendering via cookies (read in server component) avoids the ClientOnly pattern entirely — better UX and SEO.

Vue 3 event handling memory leak: registering DOM event listeners with addEventListener in onMounted without removing them in onBeforeUnmount causes leaks for every component mount/unmount cycle in SPAs. UNSAID: VueUse's `useEventListener` composable handles this correctly — it registers and removes event listeners in sync with component lifecycle. Manual addEventListener requires symmetric removeEventListener in cleanup. The leaked listeners accumulate across navigation: a component mounted on every page visit leaves one more listener each time. Long-running SPAs with this pattern see degrading performance as listener count grows. The pattern for window-level event listeners: always use VueUse's useEventListener or implement explicit cleanup.

Nuxt 3 server-side rendering and API routes: the `process.server` guard is required for code that should only run on the server (database access, secret environment variables). Exposing `process.env.DATABASE_URL` in client-side code leaks credentials to the browser. UNSAID: Nuxt 3's `runtimeConfig` distinguishes public (available client-side) and private (server-only) config. `useRuntimeConfig().public.apiBase` is safe to use client-side; `useRuntimeConfig().databaseUrl` is server-only. Environment variables prefixed with `NUXT_PUBLIC_` are automatically exposed; others are server-only. The common mistake: using `process.env.SECRET_KEY` in a component without `process.server` guard — Nuxt bundles this into the client JavaScript during build.

Vue 3 v-for key reactivity: keys must be unique and stable. Using index as key causes the same reconciliation bugs as React — wrong component reuse on insert/delete. Keys must come from stable data IDs. UNSAID: Vue's reconciliation with keys is identical to React's — the key identifies which virtual DOM node maps to which real DOM node. Without keys, Vue uses in-place patch strategy (updates the content of existing elements) — correct for simple lists, but wrong for stateful elements (inputs, components with internal state). Adding :key forces Vue to track identity explicitly. The debugging symptom of wrong keys: input values appearing in the wrong rows after filtering or sorting operations.

Vue 3 reactive() and Array.from(): `Array.from(reactiveArray)` returns a non-reactive copy of the array. This is correct for creating snapshot copies but wrong if you need to maintain reactivity. UNSAID: methods that create new arrays from reactive arrays always produce non-reactive plain arrays. The reactive context is only maintained through direct property access on the reactive Proxy. When passing reactive arrays to third-party functions that return new arrays, the returned arrays won't be reactive — you must re-wrap them: `reactive(thirdPartyFn(reactiveArray))`. This is rarely a problem in practice because most array operations in Vue should be done through computed properties (which maintain reactivity) rather than imperative transformations.

Vue 3 setup() timing: setup() runs after props resolution but before component mounting. onMounted callback runs after the component is mounted to the DOM. Accessing template refs ($refs) inside setup() returns null — the DOM doesn't exist yet. UNSAID: this is one of the most common Vue 3 confusion points: `const el = ref(null); console.log(el.value)` in setup() prints null. The ref is populated after mounting. The correct access point is onMounted(). The timing sequence: props resolved → setup() runs → beforeMount lifecycle → component renders → DOM created → mounted lifecycle. Template refs are populated during the 'component renders' step, making them available in onMounted but not in setup().

Vue 3 Nuxt 3 environment variables: `process.env.SECRET` is only available server-side. Client-side access requires adding the variable to `runtimeConfig.public` in nuxt.config.ts AND using `NUXT_PUBLIC_` prefix in .env. Non-prefixed env vars are server-only even if they're in runtimeConfig.public. UNSAID: the Nuxt 3 env var system has three tiers: (1) Server-only: standard .env vars, never exposed to client, (2) Public: NUXT_PUBLIC_ prefix in .env or runtimeConfig.public in config, available in both, (3) Build-time: accessed during build, baked into the bundle. Accessing a server-only variable in a client component causes an undefined value at runtime — no error, just undefined. The Nuxt DevTools environment tab shows the current effective runtime config, helpful for debugging missing variables.

Vue 3 scoped slot with dynamic component: using scoped slots with `<component :is='dynamicComp'>` requires the dynamic component to properly define the slot contract in its template. If the dynamic component doesn't expose the slot, the slot content is silently dropped. UNSAID: this is a Vue-specific gotcha — TypeScript won't catch mismatched slot contracts between the dynamic component and the parent's slot template. The fix: use TypeScript generic slot typing (available in Vue 3.3+ with `<script setup generic='...'>`), or avoid dynamic components with complex slot APIs. The debugging approach: temporarily replace the dynamic component with a static known-good component to isolate whether the issue is the dynamic dispatch or the slot contract.

Vue 3 TypeScript infer-component-emit-types: when consuming a component via template, parent TypeScript doesn't validate emit payloads by default — it's a Volar feature that must be enabled. Without proper Volar setup, passing wrong handler signatures to @emit doesn't cause TypeScript errors. UNSAID: the Volar IDE extension enables template-level TypeScript checking including emit type validation. Without Volar, .vue template TypeScript is not checked — only the script block is checked. Running `vue-tsc --noEmit` in CI catches these template TypeScript errors. The type-safe emit pattern: `defineEmits<{ 'update': [id: string, value: number] }>()` provides full TypeScript inference for emitted payloads.

Vue 3 reactive computed value caching: computed values cache their results. If the computation doesn't access any reactive state, it computes once and never updates — even when you expect it to. The common case: a computed that accesses `Date.now()` or `Math.random()` — these are not reactive, so the computed never invalidates. UNSAID: use watchEffect or a watcher with `{ immediate: true }` when you need periodic updates from non-reactive sources. The correct approach for time-based values: `const now = useNow()` from VueUse — this creates a reactive ref that updates every second. Storing Date.now() result in a ref and updating it with setInterval is the manual equivalent. The mental model: computed = pure function of reactive inputs only.

Vue 3 global error handler missing async errors: `app.config.errorHandler` captures synchronous rendering errors and caught exceptions. It does NOT capture unhandled Promise rejections from event handlers. Add `window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection', handler)` for comprehensive async error monitoring. UNSAID: the combination of app.config.errorHandler + window.onerror + window.onunhandledrejection provides full error coverage. Sentry's Vue plugin registers all three automatically. The errorHandler receives errors thrown in: template expressions, lifecycle hooks, watch callbacks, and component event handlers with try/catch. Errors in setTimeout/setInterval callbacks outside of Vue's control require window.onerror.

Vue 3 Nuxt 3 middleware execution order: global middleware (middleware/ directory), then route-specific middleware, then page-level middleware. Server middleware (server/middleware/) runs before Nuxt's middleware and handles raw H3 requests. UNSAID: confusing Nuxt middleware (route-level, runs in Nuxt context) with server middleware (Nitro-level, runs before Nuxt router) is a common error. Server middleware has access to the raw request/response — use it for logging, auth headers, and API proxying. Route middleware has access to the Vue/Nuxt context — use it for auth guards and redirects. Middleware that access Pinia must use the Nuxt plugin pattern (useNuxtApp().$pinia) since the Nuxt app isn't initialized in server/middleware/.

Vue 3 computed with getter and setter: two-way computed property for v-model bindings in child components. `computed({ get: () => props.modelValue, set: (value) => emit('update:modelValue', value) })`. UNSAID: this is the correct pattern for wrapping a prop in a v-model-compatible local computed — the computed acts as the intermediate layer between parent state (via props) and the input element (via v-model). The failure mode: binding v-model directly to a prop: `<input v-model='props.title'>` — Vue warns about direct prop mutation. The computed getter/setter pattern resolves this cleanly. In Vue 3.4+, defineModel() generates this pattern automatically — prefer defineModel() for simplicity.

FAILURE: Mutating Pinia state directly outside of actions breaks the devtools timeline and makes state changes untraceable. UNSAID: Unlike Vuex which enforced immutable state mutations via explicit commit calls, Pinia allows direct state mutation but this should be reserved for simple cases in the component accessing the store. For any state change that represents a domain operation (login, add to cart, update settings), define it as a store action — this makes the devtools show meaningful named operations in the timeline instead of anonymous mutations. For batch updates, use $patch({ field1: val, field2: val }) which records as a single operation.

FAILURE: Using v-if and v-for on the same element causes priority bugs and performance issues. UNSAID: In Vue 2, v-for had higher priority than v-if, so the loop ran before the condition was checked — iterating all items even when the condition was false. In Vue 3, v-if has higher priority, which is often the opposite of what developers expect. The correct pattern: wrap the v-for in a template element with v-if for overall condition (show/hide the list), or use a computed property to filter the array before rendering, then v-for on the result. Never rely on v-if/v-for co-existence — it's an anti-pattern in all Vue versions and the official style guide gives it Priority A (essential) avoidance.

FAILURE: Not using key attribute on v-for items or using index as key causes DOM reuse bugs and broken animations. UNSAID: Vue's virtual DOM diffs use the key to identify nodes. Without a key (or with index as key), Vue may reuse a DOM node for a different list item when the array changes — causing visible glitches like input values appearing in the wrong field, or CSS transitions running on the wrong element. Keys must be unique and stable (not generated fresh on each render with Math.random()). For animated lists with TransitionGroup, incorrect keys cause animations to apply to the wrong items. The rule: always use the item's stable unique identifier (database ID, UUID) as the key.

FAILURE: Using setTimeout or setInterval inside computed properties or watchers causes memory leaks and infinite re-render loops. UNSAID: Computed properties must be pure and synchronous — accessing a reactive dependency inside a timeout callback doesn't establish reactive tracking because Vue's dependency collection happens synchronously. Intervals inside watchers run but are never cleaned up when the component unmounts. The correct patterns: use onMounted to start intervals with cleanup in onUnmounted; use watchEffect for reactive side effects (it auto-tracks dependencies); use debounce/throttle from VueUse (useDebounceFn, useThrottleFn) for rate-limited reactive responses instead of manual setTimeout.

FAILURE: Creating Pinia stores outside of the Vue app context (before app.use(pinia)) breaks SSR and causes state leakage between requests. UNSAID: In SSR, Pinia stores should be created per-request, not globally — otherwise state from one user's request leaks into another's. Nuxt 3 handles this automatically: useUserStore() called inside a composable or component creates a per-request store during SSR. The mistake is calling stores at module level: const store = useUserStore() at the top of a file that gets imported during SSR — this runs outside the request context and shares state across requests. The fix: always call useXXXStore() inside setup functions, composables, or lifecycle hooks, never at module level.

FAILURE: Using v-model without specifying the modelValue prop and update:modelValue emit breaks the two-way binding contract. UNSAID: v-model on a component is syntactic sugar for :modelValue='value' @update:modelValue='value = $event'. Without explicitly defining modelValue in defineProps and emitting update:modelValue, v-model silently fails — the parent passes the value, but changes don't propagate back. Vue 3.4's defineModel() macro eliminates this boilerplate: const model = defineModel() creates both the prop and the emit automatically. For multiple v-model bindings (v-model:title, v-model:content), defineModel('title') and defineModel('content') create named bindings. This is the most common SFC binding mistake for developers new to Vue component design.

FAILURE: Not using computed properties for expensive derived state causes unnecessary recalculations and performance issues. UNSAID: A template expression like {{ users.filter(u => u.active).sort(...).slice(0, 10) }} re-executes on every render. If the users array is large, this runs on every re-render including unrelated state changes. computed() caches the result and only re-runs when its reactive dependencies change. The related mistake: calling methods in templates for derived values ({{ getActiveUsers() }}) — methods are not cached and run on every render. Rule: any derived value that is pure (same input → same output) and potentially expensive belongs in computed(). Methods are for event handlers and operations with side effects.

FAILURE: Using async components without loading/error states in production causes blank flash or uncaught promise rejection. UNSAID: defineAsyncComponent(() => import('./Component.vue')) without error handling silently fails if the chunk fails to load (network error, 404 after deployment). Production-ready async components need: loadingComponent for the loading state, errorComponent for failure with retry, delay option (typically 200ms) to avoid loading flash for fast connections, timeout to show error state if loading takes too long. The pattern: create a standardized AsyncWrapper component that wraps defineAsyncComponent with consistent loading/error UI across the app, ensuring all async components behave predictably.

FAILURE: Accessing this in arrow functions inside Options API methods breaks Vue's context binding. UNSAID: In Vue Options API, this refers to the component instance — but only in regular function declarations (function() {}), not arrow functions (() => {}). Arrow functions capture the outer this (usually undefined or the module scope). This bug is subtle: const methods = { fetchData: async () => { this.data = await api.get() } } — this.data is undefined. The fix: use regular functions for all Options API lifecycle hooks, computed, methods, and watchers. This is a Vue 2/3 Options API gotcha that doesn't affect Composition API (which doesn't use this at all). Linting with vue/no-arrow-functions-in-watch catches some cases.

FAILURE: Not awaiting nextTick when accessing DOM after reactive state changes causes operating on stale DOM. UNSAID: Vue's DOM updates are asynchronous — changing reactive state doesn't immediately update the DOM. Code that immediately accesses the DOM after a state change reads the pre-update DOM. nextTick() returns a promise that resolves after Vue flushes the DOM update queue. Pattern: await nextTick() after state changes before DOM measurements, focus() calls, or scroll operations. The common bug: setting this.showInput = true then immediately calling this.$refs.input.focus() — the input isn't in the DOM yet. Fix: await nextTick() between the state change and the DOM operation.

FAILURE: Using reactive() instead of ref() for primitive values prevents the reactivity system from detecting changes. UNSAID: reactive() only works with objects — reactive(0) or reactive('hello') returns the primitive unchanged, not wrapped in a Proxy. Assigning a new primitive value to a reactive reference silently does nothing: const count = reactive(0); count = 5 // no reactive update. Always use ref() for primitive values. For objects, both ref() and reactive() work, but ref() is often preferred for consistency — one mental model for all reactive state. The practical rule: use ref() for everything; only use reactive() when you explicitly want to avoid the .value access for a complex object that you won't reassign.

FAILURE: Not handling SSR-specific code execution (browser APIs in server context) causes 'window is not defined' errors in Nuxt. UNSAID: In Nuxt SSR, setup() runs on both server and client. Code accessing window, document, localStorage, or navigator fails on the server. The solutions: 1) useNuxtApp().$nuxt.isHydrating check; 2) import.meta.client / import.meta.server conditionals; 3) onMounted() for browser-only code (onMounted doesn't run on server); 4) process.client check (Nuxt injects this). The cleanest pattern: all browser API access in onMounted(), all data fetching in useAsyncData/useFetch (which handles server/client correctly). VueUse composables are SSR-safe by default — they check for browser environment internally.

FAILURE: Blocking the Vue rendering thread with synchronous heavy operations causes janky UI and unresponsive interactions. UNSAID: Large synchronous computations in computed properties, watchers, or template expressions freeze the browser main thread. Common offenders: filtering/sorting large arrays (10k+ items) synchronously, complex string processing in templates, heavy cryptographic operations. Solutions: 1) Move computation to Web Workers using comlink for clean API; 2) Use requestIdleCallback for non-critical operations; 3) Virtualize large lists instead of rendering all items; 4) Cache expensive computations in computed() with appropriate dependencies. VueUse's useWebWorker composable simplifies Web Worker integration.

FAILURE: Circular dependencies between Pinia stores cause initialization errors and unpredictable behavior. UNSAID: Store A importing Store B while Store B imports Store A creates a circular dependency that breaks module initialization. The symptom: one store is undefined when the other tries to use it. Solutions: 1) Extract shared logic to a utility function that both stores import independently; 2) Lazy-load the circular dependency inside actions (const otherStore = useOtherStore() inside the action, not at module level); 3) Restructure to eliminate the circular dependency (often indicates a design issue where logic belongs in a third shared store). Pinia's setup stores are more flexible with circular deps than options stores because actions are just functions.

FAILURE: Using $emit directly in templates with incorrect event names causes silent bugs that are hard to debug. UNSAID: Vue 3 emits are case-sensitive in script setup TypeScript definitions but camelCase/kebab-case is auto-converted in templates. The bug: emitting 'update:modelValue' but listening for 'update:model-value' (kebab-case) works in templates but not when programmatically calling watch or manually listening. The defineEmits type definition is the source of truth. More subtle: emitting without defining in defineEmits works at runtime but loses TypeScript checking and ESLint warnings. Always define all emitted events in defineEmits — it documents the component's output API and enables type checking of emit payloads.

FAILURE: Not clearing Pinia store state on user logout causes data leakage between user sessions in SPAs. UNSAID: When a user logs out and another logs in (in the same browser tab without page reload), Pinia stores retain the previous user's data unless explicitly reset. The pattern: define a $reset() action in each store that restores initial state, and call it for all stores on logout. For setup stores (Composition API style), $reset() is not auto-generated — implement it manually using a factory function for initial state that both init and $reset() use. pinia-plugin-reset extends this capability. The security impact: if a store contains payment info, personal details, or session data, not clearing it is a privacy vulnerability.

FAILURE: Overusing watchers instead of computed properties causes complex state dependency chains that are hard to debug. UNSAID: The default instinct from imperative programming is to watch state changes and respond with updates to other state (watch A → update B → watch B → update C). This creates imperative state dependency chains that are hard to trace. The declarative alternative: computed properties express B as a function of A, and C as a function of B. This is simpler, more testable, and Vue optimizes the dependency tracking automatically. Rule: watchers are for side effects (API calls, DOM operations, localStorage writes) in response to state changes, not for computing derived state. Computed properties are for derived state.

The single most common Playwright/Puppeteer failure in production is the race between network requests and DOM readiness — specifically, calling page.waitForSelector() or reading innerHTML immediately after page.goto() before the application's internal data-fetch XHR calls have completed. The surface symptom is inconsistent extraction: the title appears but product prices are missing, or a list renders 0 items on some runs. The root cause: SPA frameworks render a skeleton shell synchronously, then populate it with data from async API calls. page.waitForLoadState('load') or even 'networkidle' is not reliable — 'networkidle' waits for 500ms of no new requests, but many sites have background polling that prevents it from ever triggering. The correct fix is to wait for the specific DOM element that only appears after the data is loaded (waitForSelector with a leaf-node selector), or to intercept and await the specific XHR response using page.waitForResponse() with a URL pattern match.

Proxy rotation as a bot evasion strategy fails when implemented as naive IP cycling without fingerprint consistency. The trap: modern anti-bot systems (Cloudflare, PerimeterX, Akamai Bot Manager) do not rely on IP alone — they correlate TLS fingerprint (JA3/JA4 hash), HTTP/2 frame ordering, browser canvas fingerprint, WebGL renderer string, navigator.languages, and timezone against each other. Rotating the IP while keeping the same headless browser fingerprint achieves nothing because the non-IP signals create a unique identifier that is sticky across IPs. The correct approach requires fingerprint rotation in addition to IP rotation: randomizing viewport size, user-agent, language headers, and using stealth plugins (playwright-extra with puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth) that patch the known headless detection surfaces. Even then, sites with ML-based bot scoring will fingerprint behavioral patterns (mouse movement entropy, click timing distributions) that are hard to spoof.

JavaScript rendering is not binary — it exists on a spectrum, and misclassifying a site's rendering mode causes systematic extraction failures. The failure taxonomy: (1) Server-side rendered (SSR): full HTML in the response body — plain HTTP client works, no JS needed. (2) Static site generation (SSG): pre-built HTML files — same as SSR from the scraper's perspective. (3) Client-side rendered (CSR): empty shell + JS bundle — requires headless browser. (4) Hybrid/Next.js ISR: some routes SSR, some CSR — the same domain may require different strategies per URL path. The diagnostic trap: checking the homepage only. Many sites SSR the landing page for SEO but CSR product detail pages or user dashboards. The correct diagnostic is to curl the exact URL you need to scrape and check whether the target data appears in the raw HTML. Running curl -s URL | grep 'target-text' is the 30-second test that prevents hours of unnecessary Playwright infrastructure setup.

BeautifulSoup's lxml parser and html.parser produce different parse trees for malformed HTML, and choosing the wrong one causes silent data loss. The failure: html.parser is Python's built-in and requires no installation, so it is the default in many tutorials. However, lxml uses libxml2's error-correction heuristics which more closely match browser behavior — for real-world malformed HTML (unclosed tags, nested tables, invalid nesting), lxml produces the DOM structure you see in the browser's Inspector, while html.parser may collapse or restructure it differently. The practical consequence: a CSS selector that works when you inspect the page in Chrome fails when applied to BeautifulSoup's html.parser tree because the trees differ. The correct default is BeautifulSoup(html, 'lxml') for any scraping work on real sites. Use html.parser only when lxml is unavailable and you have verified the target HTML is well-formed.

Async concurrency in Python web scraping with asyncio + aiohttp produces a common failure mode: uncapped semaphore concurrency causes connection pool exhaustion or per-domain rate limiting even when the code 'looks' throttled. The trap: beginners create asyncio.gather() with 100 coroutines thinking that 'async' means they won't hammer the server simultaneously. In fact, asyncio.gather() with 100 coroutines issues all 100 requests as fast as the event loop can schedule them — there is no implicit throttling. The correct pattern requires an explicit asyncio.Semaphore(N) where N is your desired per-domain concurrency cap, and all fetch coroutines must acquire the semaphore before making the request. Additionally, aiohttp's TCPConnector must be shared across all requests (not created per-request) and given a matching limit parameter — creating a new connector per request bypasses the connection pool entirely, defeating keep-alive and creating thousands of TCP connections.

Storing raw HTML in a database as the scraping record is a deceptively poor archiving strategy that creates significant operational problems. The failure modes: (1) Storage cost — a single SPA page with inlined JavaScript can be 2–5 MB of raw HTML, making it 100x larger than the extracted structured data. (2) Re-extraction fragility — re-parsing stored HTML with a new extractor assumes the extractor logic is compatible with the DOM structure of the HTML as it existed at crawl time, which is only true if extraction was deterministic. (3) GDPR and legal compliance — storing full page HTML may capture personal data (user-generated content, profile information) that was not the extraction target, creating unnecessary data retention liability. The correct archival pattern: store the structured extracted data as the primary record; store a content hash (SHA-256 of the normalized HTML) for change detection; store raw HTML only in a cold archive (S3 Glacier, object storage with lifecycle policies) with a 90-day TTL, not in the operational database.

HTTP redirect handling is a surprisingly common source of silent data loss in scraping pipelines. The failure pattern: a site migrates from HTTP to HTTPS, or restructures its URL scheme, leaving 301 redirects in place. Libraries like httpx follow redirects by default up to a limit (default 20), but if the final destination returns a different schema than expected (e.g., a login page instead of the content), the pipeline continues with wrong data. The second failure: some sites use 302 temporary redirects to inject CAPTCHA challenges or cookie consent pages — the scraper follows the redirect, receives the challenge page HTML, parses it against the content extractor, and returns empty or nonsense fields without logging an error. The correct mitigation: always log the final URL after redirect resolution and compare it against the expected URL pattern; implement a redirect-chain inspector that flags unexpected domain changes (e.g., target.com → accounts.target.com signals a login redirect); never treat a 200 status code as a guarantee of correct content.

User-agent string spoofing is necessary but insufficient for bot evasion, and treating it as sufficient is the most common beginner mistake that leads to immediate blocking. The failure: setting a realistic Chrome user-agent string in a plain requests session changes one HTTP header while leaving dozens of bot-revealing signals untouched: the TLS handshake has a Python ssl fingerprint (not a Chrome fingerprint), the Accept-Language header is missing or wrong, there is no Sec-CH-UA client hint header, and there are no browser-generated headers like Upgrade-Insecure-Requests, Sec-Fetch-Site, Sec-Fetch-Mode, or Sec-Fetch-Dest. Modern anti-bot systems score the full header set — missing any of the 8–12 headers that Chrome sends by default immediately raises the bot score. The correct approach for plain HTTP (non-headless) scraping: use curl_cffi (Python binding for curl with browser TLS fingerprinting) or httpx with a realistic header set copied from a browser's Network tab, including all Sec-* headers in the correct order.

Encoding detection is the invisible bug that corrupts non-ASCII content in web scraping at a rate that only surfaces in production with international sites. The failure chain: HTTP response declares charset=utf-8 in the Content-Type header, but the actual bytes are Windows-1252 or ISO-8859-1 — a common legacy pattern on European e-commerce sites. Python's requests library uses the Content-Type charset declaration directly without verification, so response.text returns mojibake (e.g., 'café' becomes 'cafÃ©'). The same bug occurs when the HTTP header charset is absent and requests defaults to ISO-8859-1 per HTTP/1.1 spec, but the actual encoding is UTF-8. The correct fix: use response.content (raw bytes) and decode with chardet or charset_normalizer to detect the actual encoding empirically — charset_normalizer is the modern replacement for chardet and is already a dependency of requests. The Playwright equivalent: page.content() always returns a Python string with correct decoding because the browser handles encoding detection natively.

Anti-bot detection fingerprinting causes web scraping systems to silently fail by returning CAPTCHAs or empty responses instead of expected content. Modern anti-bot services (Cloudflare, DataDome, Akamai Bot Manager) use 50+ signals: TLS fingerprint (JA3 hash), HTTP/2 frame ordering, browser canvas fingerprint, WebGL renderer, font enumeration, timing patterns, and behavioral mouse movement analysis. Headless Playwright is detected by: missing Chrome extensions in navigator.plugins, inconsistent window.chrome object, predictable timing of interactions, missing WebRTC leak, and automation-specific properties (navigator.webdriver=true). Teams discover this when production scraping success rate drops from 95% to 10% overnight after a CDN updates its bot signatures. Mitigation: use playwright-extra with stealth plugin, randomize viewport sizes and user agents, implement human-like delays (gaussian distribution 500-3000ms), use residential proxy rotation. Even with mitigations, detection arms-race means regular maintenance is required.

evidence_refs.note: Puppeteer stealth plugin for anti-bot evasion
Bot detection test site for validating stealth settings

statement: Memory leak accumulation in long-running Playwright browser instances causes gradual performance degradation and eventual OOM crashes. Each browser page retains JavaScript heap, image cache, event listener accumulations, and WebSocket connections that aren't always cleaned up on page.close(). Observed pattern: fresh Playwright instance uses 200MB; after processing 500 pages in a loop without browser restart, memory grows to 2-4GB; OS OOM killer terminates the process. Root causes: (1) Images loaded but not unloaded from memory cache; (2) Event listeners attached inside loops not removed; (3) CDP (Chrome DevTools Protocol) connections accumulating; (4) Third-party tracker scripts executing indefinitely in background tabs. Fixes: restart browser context every 100-200 pages; use browser.newContext() to create isolated contexts that are fully cleaned up on close(); intercept and block image/media requests when not needed (reduces memory by 40%); set page.setDefaultTimeout() to prevent hanging pages from accumulating.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright browser context documentation
Playwright best practices for resource management

statement: Session state loss between browsing tasks causes authentication failures in multi-step workflows. Automated browsers requiring login must maintain authenticated sessions across page navigations, but teams implement naive session management that loses cookies on browser restart or context creation. Failure scenarios: (1) Browser pool assigns different workers to sequential steps of a multi-page workflow, each with fresh sessions; (2) Session cookies expire mid-workflow (15-30 minute expiry common for banking/medical sites); (3) CSRF token from step 1 form not carried to step 2 submission; (4) localStorage authentication tokens not copied to new browser contexts. Proper session management: serialize cookies and localStorage to persistent store (Redis) after each successful authentication; rehydrate session state when assigning worker to authenticated domain; implement session health check (verify authentication status) before using cached session; handle session expiry with automatic re-authentication using stored credentials from vault.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright authentication state persistence documentation
Playwright storage state serialization for session persistence

statement: Proxy detection countermeasures on commercial sites cause residential proxy pool exhaustion and cost overruns. Teams purchase residential proxy subscriptions at $15-50/GB then discover that aggressive proxy rotation (new IP every request) burns through bandwidth 5-10x faster than expected: (1) Each browser startup downloads ~500KB-2MB of resources per session with a new IP; (2) CAPTCHA challenges triggered by fresh IPs require multiple page reloads and human CAPTCHA solving; (3) Sites serve different content to suspected proxy IPs (reduced functionality, rate limiting, honeypot pages); (4) IP reputation scoring means residential IPs that have been used by many scrapers are flagged as datacenter-like. Optimal proxy strategy: sticky sessions (same IP for 10-30 minute session), rotate on authentication challenges not proactively, warm up new IPs by visiting the homepage naturally before targeting content pages, and maintain a pool of 'trusted' IPs that haven't been flagged.

evidence_refs.note: Residential proxy usage patterns and best practices
Residential proxy rotation strategies

statement: Infinite scroll pagination failure causes incomplete data extraction for social media, news feeds, and e-commerce listings. Teams write extractors that trigger scroll-to-bottom once, extract visible content, and return incomplete datasets. Instagram loads 12 posts initially and requires 50+ scroll events to load 600 posts; LinkedIn company page shows 10 employees without scrolling but has 1000+. Failure modes: (1) Single scroll event doesn't trigger next data load if element isn't in exact viewport position; (2) Intersection Observer requires precise scroll positioning that differs by browser version; (3) Lazy images don't load until scrolled into view, leaving extraction with broken image URLs; (4) Virtual DOM rendering (react-window, vue-virtual-scroller) only renders visible items — scrolling up removes earlier items from DOM, making accumulated extraction impossible; (5) Rate limiting triggers after 10-20 scroll events, returning incomplete pages. Solution for virtual lists: intercept XHR/fetch calls during scroll to capture all paginated API responses directly, bypassing DOM extraction limitations.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright page.evaluate for custom scroll logic
Playwright network request interception documentation

statement: Race conditions in concurrent browser automation cause data corruption and incomplete extractions. Teams run 20 parallel browser workers all writing to a shared extraction queue, then discover: (1) Multiple workers extract the same URL (duplicate work, database constraint violations); (2) Worker A starts extraction, worker B marks URL as in-progress, worker A's result is discarded because URL shows 'already processed'; (3) State machine transitions (pending→processing→completed) have TOCTOU race where two workers both read 'pending' before either writes 'processing'; (4) Shared browser context causes cross-worker session contamination. Solutions: (1) Atomic URL claiming: use Redis SET NX (set if not exists) or database SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to atomically claim URLs; (2) Worker-scoped browser contexts: never share contexts between workers; (3) Heartbeat mechanism: worker must renew claim every 30s, expired claims return to queue after 2 minutes; (4) Idempotent extraction: extraction operations must be safe to retry, with database upsert semantics.

evidence_refs.note: Redis distributed lock patterns for concurrent systems
PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED documentation

statement: Stale element references cause intermittent extraction failures in dynamic Single Page Applications. Playwright's locator API resolves elements lazily and re-queries the DOM on each interaction, but older handle-based APIs (ElementHandle) cache DOM references that become invalid after any DOM update. Failure scenarios: (1) Select dropdown menu item: click to open dropdown (DOM updates with new elements), then reference to dropdown option becomes stale; (2) List pagination: get all row handles on page 1, click 'next page' (DOM replaces), then attempt to read from page 1 handles causes StaleElementReferenceException; (3) React/Vue re-render: any state update triggers component re-render, invalidating all ElementHandles for that component subtree; (4) Accordion expand: clicking accordion header triggers DOM insertion of new content nodes, staling references to sibling elements. Migration: replace all ElementHandle usage with Playwright's Locator API (page.locator()) which re-queries the DOM automatically. The Locator API adds 10-50ms overhead per interaction but eliminates stale reference errors entirely.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright Locator API documentation - recommended over ElementHandle
Playwright ElementHandle deprecation notes

statement: Dialog and popup interruption causes extraction workflow failures when unexpected browser dialogs block page interaction. JavaScript alert(), confirm(), and prompt() dialogs freeze browser interaction until dismissed; modal overlays (cookie consent, subscription prompts, survey widgets) overlay content and prevent element clicking; browser-native permission dialogs (geolocation, notifications, camera) require programmatic dismissal. Teams that don't handle these experience: (1) alert() dialog blocks all further page.click() calls indefinitely; (2) Cookie consent overlay prevents clicking underlying content links; (3) Permission dialog pauses workflow awaiting non-existent user interaction; (4) Exit intent popups trigger on mouse movement near viewport edge during scroll simulation. Playwright solutions: (1) Auto-dismiss all JavaScript dialogs: page.on('dialog', dialog => dialog.dismiss()); (2) Grant permissions proactively: browser.newContext({ permissions: ['geolocation'] }); (3) CSS injection to hide cookie banners: page.addStyleTag({ content: '[class*="cookie"] { display: none !important }' }); (4) Wait for and close modals before interacting with underlying content.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright dialog handling documentation
Playwright context permissions configuration

statement: Geo-IP content variation causes extraction inconsistency when web services serve different content by geographic location. Teams running browser automation from cloud regions (US-East, EU-West) discover that target sites serve different prices, product catalogs, language content, and availability based on detected IP location. E-commerce sites show EUR prices to EU datacenter IPs vs USD to US IPs; streaming services show different content libraries; news sites show region-specific articles. Failure modes: (1) Price comparison application shows all prices in USD because cloud workers are in US-East, missing EU price variations; (2) Content availability checker reports product 'available' from US when it's actually 'sold out' in target market; (3) A/B test analysis contaminated because datacenter IPs consistently land in control group. Solutions: use geo-distributed proxies matching target market regions; set Accept-Language headers explicitly; add timezone configuration to browser context to reinforce locale; verify extracted content includes expected regional markers (currency symbol, language).

evidence_refs.note: Playwright geolocation and locale context options
MaxMind GeoIP database for IP geolocation

statement: iFrame content access failures account for 15-20% of web extraction errors because iFrame content is treated as a separate browsing context. Teams attempt to select elements inside iFrames using page.locator() and get 'element not found' errors, not understanding that page-level locators don't traverse iFrame boundaries. Common scenarios: (1) Google Maps embedded in a page; target content (address, hours) is inside the maps iFrame; (2) YouTube embeds: title, description require iFrame access; (3) Third-party widgets (customer reviews, payment forms): entirely inside vendor iFrames; (4) Cross-origin iFrames: subject to same-origin policy; JavaScript can't access cross-origin iFrame DOM directly. Playwright solution: use page.frameLocator('iframe[name="content"]').locator('#target') for same-origin iFrames; for cross-origin iFrames, extract only publicly-accessible attributes (src, title). Security constraint: cross-origin iFrame content is intentionally inaccessible from parent frame; the only way to access it is to navigate directly to the iFrame URL.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright frame and iFrame interaction documentation
Playwright FrameLocator API documentation

statement: TLS certificate validation errors cause automated browsing failures on enterprise internal sites and self-signed certificate environments. Corporate intranets, staging environments, and IoT device management portals commonly use self-signed or internal CA certificates. Playwright launched with default settings rejects these, throwing 'NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID'. Teams work around this by setting ignoredHTTPSErrors=true globally, inadvertently disabling SSL validation for all browsing including external sites — creating man-in-the-middle attack vulnerability. Proper handling: (1) Whitelist specific domains for certificate bypass: route.continue() with custom options for known-safe internal hosts only; (2) Install enterprise CA certificate into the browser: pass --ignore-certificate-errors-spki-list with the SPKI fingerprint of the internal CA; (3) Never use --ignore-certificate-errors globally in production systems accessing external financial/healthcare/authentication sites; (4) Detect certificate errors separately from content errors and alert differently. Security audit should check for any global certificate bypass configurations.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright ignoreHTTPSErrors configuration
Certificate chain validation and CA trust concepts

statement: Dynamic URL fingerprinting causes URL deduplication failures in web crawlers. Modern sites append tracking parameters to URLs (utm_source, fbclid, gclid, session tokens) that make semantically identical pages appear as unique URLs. A product page may appear as 50+ distinct URLs due to referrer tracking: example.com/product/123, example.com/product/123?utm_source=email, example.com/product/123?fbclid=abc123, example.com/product/123?ref=homepage. Crawlers that use raw URL as deduplication key will: (1) Crawl the same product page 50+ times; (2) Create duplicate entries in the content database; (3) Exhaust crawl budget on parameter variations instead of new pages; (4) Pollute internal linking analysis with false edge diversity. Canonical URL normalization: strip tracking parameters by whitelist (keep only parameters that affect content: page, category, id); normalize URL encoding; follow canonical link rel tags; check HTTP response headers for canonical redirects. After normalization, URLs should dedup 60-80% in typical e-commerce crawls.

evidence_refs.note: Google canonical URL best practices
Scrapy URL normalization for broad crawls

statement: Timezone and locale mismatch in web automation causes extraction of incorrect data from region-aware websites. Browser automation scripts run from UTC servers but target websites may render content based on the browser's reported timezone. A flight booking site shows departure times in local airport time if browser timezone is set to airport timezone, but in server UTC if not configured. Price comparison sites may show different promotions based on reported locale. Teams discover this when extracted prices don't match what users see in their local browsers, or when event times are 5-8 hours offset. Playwright fix: configure timezone and locale in browser context: browser.newContext({ timezoneId: 'America/New_York', locale: 'en-US' }). Additional considerations: (1) Some sites detect locale via Accept-Language header which must also be set; (2) JavaScript Date() objects in page context will use configured timezone; (3) Currency formatting (1,234.56 vs 1.234,56) varies by locale and affects regex-based price parsing; (4) Date format parsing (MM/DD/YYYY vs DD/MM/YYYY) depends on locale — always verify with multiple test cases.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright browser context timezone and locale configuration
Accept-Language header for locale negotiation

statement: Base64-encoded content and data URIs cause content extraction blind spots. Modern web applications increasingly serve content as: base64-encoded images embedded in HTML (data:image/png;base64,...), JavaScript-rendered SVG with embedded text that CSS-selectors miss, and canvas-rendered text that is invisible to DOM selectors. Financial sites render account numbers as images to prevent automated scraping; CAPTCHA challenges are images; some PDF viewers render page content to canvas elements. Teams extract apparent empty divs while the actual content is visually rendered by JavaScript to canvas. Detection: check if visible text in screenshot doesn't match DOM text extraction — large discrepancy indicates canvas or image-rendered content. Solutions: (1) For canvas text: inject JavaScript to access canvas context and read rendered text where possible; (2) For image-embedded text: OCR pipeline (Tesseract, EasyOCR) on extracted images; (3) For SVG text: query SVG text elements directly as DOM nodes; (4) Alert when extraction confidence is low due to canvas/image-heavy pages.

evidence_refs.note: Canvas API documentation for understanding canvas-rendered content
Tesseract OCR for image text extraction

statement: Mobile-only content access failures occur when automation targets desktop-optimized paths while business-critical content is served exclusively via mobile web or native apps. Many modern services (Instagram Direct, WhatsApp Business, certain banking features) serve complete functionality only to mobile user agents. Teams using desktop Playwright headless miss: (1) Mobile-specific navigation menus and content sections; (2) App-interstitial pages that redirect to app store on desktop; (3) Different API endpoints called by mobile vs desktop JavaScript bundles; (4) AMP (Accelerated Mobile Pages) variants with different content structure. Solution: configure mobile emulation in Playwright with device descriptors: page.emulate({ viewport: { width: 375, height: 812 }, userAgent: 'Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 14_0 like Mac OS X)...' }). Built-in device list: Playwright.devices['iPhone 13'] includes correct viewport, user agent, deviceScaleFactor, and touch event support. Verify extraction works on both desktop and mobile variants for each target site during onboarding.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright device emulation documentation
Playwright built-in device descriptors for mobile emulation

statement: Background script execution interference causes intermittent extraction failures on news and media sites. Modern websites execute dozens of third-party scripts (analytics, ad networks, A/B testing frameworks, chat widgets) that consume CPU, modify DOM structure, and insert dynamic content that conflicts with extraction logic. Common failures: (1) Google Tag Manager fires 200ms after page load and injects new DOM elements that duplicate content selectors, causing double-extraction; (2) Intercom chat widget overwrites CSS classes on body element, breaking layout-based selectors; (3) A/B testing script (Optimizely, VWO) randomly swaps content after initial render — extraction timing determines which variant is captured, causing 50% extraction inconsistency; (4) Ad refreshes reload iframe content, causing stale element references for any handles captured to ad-containing regions; (5) Service Worker script intercepts network requests and returns cached responses that differ from server content. Mitigation: block known third-party script domains via page.route(), accept a small risk of breaking site functionality vs the extraction reliability gain.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright request blocking for third-party scripts
Chrome request blocking API for filtering third-party scripts

statement: Single-point-of-failure in browser farm architecture causes total extraction service outage. Teams deploy all browser workers on a single host or in a single availability zone, then discover that cloud maintenance events, network failures, or hardware failures cause 100% extraction downtime. Common architecture mistakes: (1) Single Redis instance for URL queue — if Redis fails, all workers lose their task queue and in-flight work; (2) Single NFS mount for screenshot storage — NFS mount failure blocks all workers from writing results; (3) Workers sharing a single external proxy pool account — pool service outage disables all external browsing; (4) Database connection pool exhausted when all 50 workers connect simultaneously — connection limit causes all workers to queue; (5) Docker daemon crash on single host kills all containers simultaneously. Resilient architecture: distribute workers across 2+ availability zones; use Redis Sentinel or Cluster for queue HA; configure connection pooling with max-connection limits per worker; maintain backup proxy provider; implement graceful degradation when storage unavailable (cache results locally, flush when storage recovers).

evidence_refs.note: Redis Sentinel for high availability
Playwright browser pool management documentation

statement: JavaScript SPA routing confusion causes browsers to navigate to incorrect states during automated interactions. Single-page applications use client-side routing (React Router, Vue Router, Next.js) where URL changes don't trigger full page loads. Failures: (1) page.goto('/app/dashboard') triggers a server request that returns the SPA shell, then client-side router renders dashboard — extraction fires before routing completes; (2) page.goBack() in SPA context doesn't re-render previous state, just pops history causing empty page; (3) Hard page.reload() in an SPA resets application state, losing multi-step navigation context; (4) URL hash routing (#/section) causes browser to navigate to anchor rather than trigger router; (5) pushState-based routing: URL visible in address bar doesn't match any server route, causing 404 on direct navigation. Correct approach: always trigger navigation by clicking application links rather than using page.goto() for internal navigation in SPAs; wait for route-specific DOM elements rather than load events; use page.waitForURL() to confirm routing completed before extracting.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright waitForURL for SPA routing completion
React Router navigation patterns

statement: Font loading failures corrupt visual verification and screenshot-based testing in headless browsers. Google Fonts and custom web fonts are fetched at render time; headless browsers without network access or with blocked font CDNs render text as fallback system fonts, causing layout shifts, character rendering differences, and visual test failures. Teams running visual regression tests in CI (network-isolated containers) discover that screenshots taken in CI look different from production screenshots because: (1) Google Fonts CDN blocked → fallback to Arial, changing character widths and line breaks; (2) Icon fonts (Font Awesome, Material Icons) blocked → icons render as squares or missing characters; (3) Font subsetting: CDN serves only characters used on page, but subset varies by locale causing visual inconsistency. Solutions: (1) Bundle required fonts locally and serve from test fixtures; (2) Configure browser to use font cache from pre-warmed profile; (3) Block font CDNs explicitly and set CSS font-family fallbacks consistently; (4) In visual tests, use 'perceptual hash' comparison that tolerates minor font rendering differences.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright screenshot options and font handling
CSS font loading and fallback specification

statement: Network timeout misconfiguration causes extraction workers to hang indefinitely, exhausting the worker pool. Default browser navigation timeout is 30 seconds in Playwright, but CDN-served pages can sometimes load resources for minutes when external CDN is slow. Teams observe worker utilization at 100% with zero extractions completing, because all workers are blocked waiting for slow-loading pages. Cascading failure: slow pages fill the worker pool; new URL requests queue indefinitely; queue grows unbounded; alert fires hours later when queue depth exceeds threshold. Timeout hierarchy in Playwright: (1) navigation timeout: time for initial page load event (set 15-20s for most sites); (2) element timeout: time to find/interact with element (set 10s); (3) test/action timeout: total time for any single action; (4) global timeout: maximum time for entire workflow. Set aggressive timeouts and handle TimeoutError explicitly with retry logic. Fast-fail is better than slow-hang: a 15-second timeout with 3 retries completes in 45 seconds max vs 5-minute hang with default settings.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright timeout configuration documentation
Playwright error types including TimeoutError

statement: Color scheme and dark mode variations cause visual extraction failures when websites dynamically switch content based on user preference or time of day. Some sites: (1) Serve different price displays in dark mode (font-based vs image-based); (2) Change table structures in mobile vs desktop themes; (3) Show different content blocks based on prefers-color-scheme media query. Headless browsers default to light mode, but a significant portion of users browse in dark mode (30-40% by OS survey data). Teams discover this when user-reported screenshots don't match automatically captured screenshots — the site is showing different layouts. Playwright configuration: browser.newContext({ colorScheme: 'dark' }) or 'light' or 'no-preference'. Additionally: (1) Verify if target site is time-aware (some news sites show different layouts during off-hours); (2) Check if targeted ads or A/B tests change content (use content-hash comparison across multiple sessions to detect variation); (3) For critical sites, run extraction in both color schemes and compare results; significant differences warrant investigation.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright color scheme emulation documentation
CSS prefers-color-scheme media query

statement: JavaScript execution errors silently break extraction by partially rendering pages. When a page's JavaScript throws an uncaught exception, the browser may: (1) Stop JavaScript execution mid-render, leaving page partially populated; (2) Continue running other scripts but skip the failed module entirely; (3) Render placeholder loading states instead of actual content. Teams don't detect these failures because HTTP status 200 is returned and the page DOM has some content — just not complete content. Detected by: page.on('pageerror') listener capturing console.error and uncaught exceptions; checking if expected dynamic elements are present; comparing text density to baseline. Common causes: (1) API call fails with 5xx → loading state never transitions to content state; (2) Content Security Policy blocks inline script execution; (3) jQuery or React version conflict with third-party library; (4) Missing feature flag value causes null pointer in conditional rendering code. Mitigation: implement page health check by counting expected element types after load; if count falls below expected minimum, classify as partial load and retry.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright pageerror event for JavaScript error detection
Playwright console message capture documentation

statement: Subdomain and CDN-based A/B testing causes inconsistent extraction results that are difficult to debug. Companies run experiments by routing different user cohorts to different subdomains or CDN edge configurations (next.example.com, beta.example.com, experiment-42.example.com). Automated browsers hit different CDN PoPs or get assigned different experiment buckets across runs, receiving different page variants that have different HTML structures and extractable fields. Teams observe: (1) Extraction success rate 85% on Monday, 65% on Tuesday — variance correlates with experiment rollout percentage; (2) Price extracted as text on variant A, as structured data-attribute on variant B; (3) Feature page lists 6 features in control and 8 features in treatment — analysis incorrectly attributes feature count change to actual product change; (4) Redirect chains differ by experiment: control goes to /pricing, treatment goes to /pricing-v2. Detection: log the final resolved URL after all redirects; compare HTML size across samples; use hashing to identify when you received different page variants. Request specific experiments to be excluded from automated monitoring IPs.

evidence_refs.note: HTTP redirects and final URL resolution
Playwright response URL for detecting redirects and CDN routing

statement: Pre-signed URL expiry causes broken extraction workflows when target files have time-limited access tokens. Cloud storage (AWS S3, GCP GCS) and CDN providers generate pre-signed URLs with expiration times (15 minutes to 7 days) for secure file access. Teams extract these URLs during initial page scrape, queue them for later download, then discover that download attempts 2+ hours later fail with 403 AccessDenied. Similar pattern with: (1) Streaming video source URLs with 1-hour JWT tokens; (2) Report download links that expire after one click; (3) OAuth-protected media with access tokens that expire every 30 minutes; (4) One-time download tokens that become invalid after first use. Fix: detect pre-signed URL patterns (look for X-Amz-Signature, token=, access_key query parameters); set TTL on queued download tasks shorter than expected expiry; for critical downloads, execute immediately rather than queuing; implement re-fetch-and-download logic that re-navigates to the source page if download URL has expired.

evidence_refs.note: AWS S3 pre-signed URL documentation and expiry handling
HTTP 403 Forbidden status code scenarios

statement: File upload form automation failures stem from differences between browser-native file upload and automated file injection. Standard HTML file inputs (<input type='file'>) require special handling in automation because: (1) page.setInputFiles() must be called on the actual hidden file input element, not a custom drag-zone overlay or label element that triggers the input; (2) Multiple file upload: setInputFiles() accepts an array but some inputs enforce single-file-only and throw silent errors; (3) File size limits: browser enforces max file size before upload begins — automation doesn't receive the validation error if it bypasses the UI check; (4) MIME type validation: client-side validation may reject files by extension while server accepts all MIME types; test both checks; (5) Drag-and-drop upload zones: some sites only accept files via DataTransfer/drop events, not input element selection — require simulating drag-and-drop with page.dispatchEvent(); (6) Chunked upload APIs: large files use multipart upload with progress tracking; headless browser must wait for all chunks to complete before submission. Always verify the entire upload flow including server-side confirmation, not just file selection.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright file upload documentation
Playwright dispatchEvent for drag-and-drop simulation

statement: Silent encoding corruption in web content extraction causes downstream data quality issues that surface long after collection. When extraction systems don't explicitly handle character encoding, they silently corrupt non-ASCII characters: French accents, Japanese kanji, Arabic right-to-left text, mathematical symbols, and emoji. Failure modes: (1) Response parsed as UTF-8 when server sends windows-1252 → British pound sign £ becomes garbage characters; (2) HTML entities (&, <, >, “) not decoded before storage → raw entity strings stored instead of actual characters; (3) Surrogate pair handling: some JavaScript string processing breaks emoji (U+1F600 requires surrogate pairs in UTF-16); (4) Database encoding mismatch: MySQL with latin1 collation silently drops or corrupts multibyte UTF-8 characters; (5) CSV export with mixed encodings breaks Excel parsing. Prevention: always explicitly specify encoding in HTTP client and HTML parser; use html.unescape() before storage; ensure database collation is utf8mb4 (not utf8 which only handles 3-byte sequences); validate round-trip encoding in tests with multi-script test fixtures.

evidence_refs.note: Python Unicode handling best practices
chardet encoding detection library documentation

statement: Rate limiting state not shared across distributed workers causes ban-triggering over-requests to target domains. Each worker maintains its own local rate limiter, so with 10 workers each thinking they can make 1 request/second to a domain, the actual aggregate rate is 10 requests/second — easily triggering IP bans. Teams discover this when their IP block rate suddenly increases after scaling from 2 to 10 workers without centralizing rate limit state. The domain-level aggregate request rate must be enforced centrally: (1) Redis-based token bucket with INCR and EXPIRE to track per-domain request count across all workers; (2) Each worker acquires a token from central bucket before making a request; (3) Workers respect domain-level delays, not just per-worker delays; (4) Shared ban tracking: when any worker receives 429 from a domain, all workers immediately back off from that domain; (5) Domain rate profiles: store per-domain allowed rates (some domains are more permissive), defaults to conservative global limit. Centralized rate limiting adds 1-5ms overhead per request but prevents expensive IP replacement after bans.

evidence_refs.note: Redis rate limiting patterns documentation
HTTP 429 Too Many Requests handling

statement: Aggressive prefetch and service worker caching serves outdated content to automated browsers in ways that are invisible without cache inspection. Modern PWAs (Progressive Web Apps) install service workers that intercept network requests and serve cached responses. A product page visited by the automated browser may be served from a service worker cache that is 12+ hours old, while the actual server content has updated prices. Teams discover this when: (1) Product prices extracted don't match current website prices visible to fresh visitors; (2) A/B test variants are locked because service worker cached the first variant encountered; (3) Authentication pages are served from cache after logout, creating false positive authentication detection. Solutions: (1) Launch browser with service workers disabled: --disable-service-workers Chrome flag; (2) Clear browser cache and storage state before each extraction: context.clearCookies() + context.clearPermissions() + custom JS to clear localStorage/sessionStorage/caches API; (3) Use incognito context (equivalent to fresh browser) for cache-sensitive extractions; (4) Add cache-busting query parameter to URLs for supported sites.

evidence_refs.note: Service Worker API documentation
Playwright browser context cache clearing methods

statement: Parallel extraction of the same user account across multiple workers causes account lockouts and unexpected state changes. Teams design workers to be stateless and reuse authentication credentials across the pool, then discover: (1) Bank websites detect multiple simultaneous logins from different IPs and force logout on all sessions; (2) E-commerce sites deduplicate cart operations, causing order from worker A to be modified by worker B's concurrent cart action; (3) Profile update workflows conflict: worker A reads profile (name='John'), worker B reads profile (name='John'), worker A writes (name='John Smith'), worker B writes (name='John Doe') — last writer wins; (4) Email automation systems flag accounts showing browsing activity from 5 different IP addresses as compromised. Solution: enforce single active session per authenticated account at a time (session lock in Redis with account_id as key); queue requests for same account, serialize execution; use multiple separate accounts for high-throughput workflows instead of parallelizing the same account.

evidence_refs.note: Redis distributed locks for account session serialization
Playwright authentication state management

statement: Tab explosion in long-running browser sessions causes performance degradation and resource exhaustion. Automated browsers accumulate open tabs when: (1) page.goto() is called without closing the current page first — each call opens a new tab if context is configured for multi-tab; (2) Clicking external links without interception opens new tabs; (3) window.open() calls from page JavaScript create new tabs that are never closed; (4) Download dialogs open new tabs before redirecting to download. A browser session with 50+ open tabs: uses 5-10GB RAM (200MB per tab), has severe GC pressure causing 500ms+ latency spikes, and triggers Chrome's built-in tab throttling that freezes background tab JavaScript. Detection: monitor browser context page count after each extraction; alert if >5 pages open per context. Prevention: (1) Explicitly close pages after extraction: page.close(); (2) Block window.open() by intercepting page.on('popup', p => p.close()); (3) Route download URLs to filesystem instead of letting them open in new tab; (4) Set max pages per context limit and reject new page creation above limit.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright page.close() documentation
Playwright browser context page event for popup detection

statement: Content behind login walls causes false negatives in web monitoring systems when authentication silently fails. Teams assume extraction is returning complete data, but a silent authentication failure causes the site to redirect to a login page whose HTML structure partially matches extraction selectors, returning nonsense data. Failure detection is hard because: (1) HTTP 200 is returned for redirect-to-login pages in SPAs; (2) Login page HTML may have product-like elements (promotional content) that partially match product selectors; (3) Session expiry happens gradually — some requests succeed, some fail, making it appear as intermittent extraction issue rather than auth failure. Authentication health checks: (1) Extract a sentinel value that only appears when authenticated (username, account balance, subscription status); verify it's present after every authentication; (2) Check current URL doesn't match login page URL pattern; (3) Monitor authentication success rate as a separate metric from extraction success rate; (4) Proactive session refresh: re-authenticate 15 minutes before expected session expiry, not reactively after failure.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright authentication state management
Playwright page URL checking for login page detection

statement: Accepting invalid or self-generated SSL/TLS certificates without proper validation enables man-in-the-middle attacks on automated browsing sessions containing sensitive credentials. Production teams sometimes configure ignoreHTTPSErrors=true globally as a quick fix for one internal site with a certificate issue, not realizing this disables TLS validation for all requests including logins to banking or payment sites handled by the same browser worker pool. MITM attacks against automated browsers are feasible when: (1) Proxies are used (proxy can inspect/modify traffic if certificate validation disabled); (2) Browser running in a shared cloud environment where ARP spoofing is possible; (3) Rogue DNS entries redirect legitimate domains to attacker servers. Audit checklist: (1) Search codebase for ignoreHTTPSErrors, --ignore-certificate-errors, disableHTTPSErrors; (2) For any instance found, document which domains justify the bypass and restrict to those domains only; (3) Implement certificate pinning for critical high-value authentication flows; (4) Never use global certificate bypass in production workers handling authentication credentials.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright ignoreHTTPSErrors configuration risks
OWASP MITM attack documentation

statement: Large response payloads cause memory exhaustion when stored unmanaged in browser memory during extraction. Modern web APIs increasingly return large JSON responses (product catalogs with 10,000 items, financial data feeds with years of history) that browsers receive in full before JavaScript processes them. When Playwright automation intercepts these responses via page.on('response'), the response body is buffered in browser memory. Teams observe: (1) Single JSON response of 50MB causes browser tab memory to spike to 500MB+ during parsing; (2) Multiple large API responses on a single page create cumulative memory pressure; (3) Browser OOM during extraction of data-intensive pages (maps with satellite imagery tiles, complex 3D visualization data). Solutions: (1) Use response.body() streaming API when available to process chunks without full buffering; (2) Set up request interception to redirect large API responses to a local HTTP proxy that streams to disk; (3) For known large API endpoints, use direct HTTP client request instead of browser request; (4) Set browser memory limit and implement memory monitoring per page, evicting and restarting pages approaching limit.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright response body buffering behavior
Playwright network request interception for large response handling

statement: Headless browser instability on complex 3D/WebGL-heavy pages causes extraction workers to crash rather than return errors. Pages with WebGL visualization (product 3D views, interactive maps, financial charts with hardware-accelerated rendering) push headless browser GPU/software renderer to its limits. Failure modes: (1) SwiftShader software renderer (used in headless without GPU) runs out of memory on large vertex buffers; (2) ANGLE WebGL implementation triggers segfault in headless mode with certain shaders; (3) WebGPU API calls (newer than WebGL) are incompletely implemented in headless builds; (4) Canvas animation with requestAnimationFrame running at 60fps accumulates GPU commands faster than headless can process, causing memory growth until OOM; (5) Chrome SIGABRT or SIGSEGV on malformed WebGL buffers treated as bugs in headless-specific code paths. Mitigation: (1) Block WebGL requests on target pages where 3D content isn't needed; (2) Disable hardware acceleration: launch with --disable-gpu --disable-software-rasterizer flags; (3) Kill and restart browser processes that consume >1GB RAM rather than letting them crash uncontrolled.

evidence_refs.note: Playwright browser launch flags for GPU/WebGL control
Chromium headless browser limitations documentation

statement: Cookie banner acceptance automation failures cause incorrect extraction results on European websites subject to GDPR consent requirements. Many EU sites serve significantly different content to users who haven't accepted cookies: (1) Analytics-personalized product recommendations are hidden; (2) Paywall content is fully locked behind consent wall; (3) Comments sections are disabled without social media cookies; (4) Prices may exclude VAT without tracking consent in some configurations. Automated browsers not accepting cookie banners extract the 'consent-denied' version of pages, which may be incomplete. Failure modes: (1) Cookie banner overlays block click events on underlying content; (2) Cookie manager scripts load asynchronously — clicking 'Accept All' before script loads does nothing; (3) Some sites store consent in localStorage rather than cookies, losing consent when context is cleared; (4) Re-prompting on every session start wastes 2-5 seconds per extraction.
